It emphasizes that appraisers must account for rent restrictions, subsidies, rental assistance, and other government-imposed limitations, while excluding favorable financing from that real estate value.

The document outlines appraisal methodology, stating that the Income Approach is the primary and most important method for valuing subsidized housing, with direct capitalization or discounted cash flow used depending on the stability of the property’s income. It discusses how to build a restricted pro forma, how to support vacancy, expense, and capitalization rate assumptions, and what data Rural Development should provide to the appraiser. It also explains the role of the Cost Approach and why the Sales Comparison Approach is usually less useful for these properties.

A significant section covers rental assistance (RA), explaining that it should be included in the market value, subject to restricted rents, and how it affects income, vacancy, and risk. The document also distinguishes favorable financing from real property value, stating that grants, tax credits, subsidized loans, and similar benefits must be valued separately and not added into the property’s market value. Finally, it defines “as-is” value and clarifies how the term should be used in conjunction with market value, subject to restricted rents.

nodes.title: As-Is Value
nodes.summary:
The partial document explains appraisal guidance for subsidized housing projects, especially how to handle favorable financing, valuation types, and scope-of-work requirements. It covers the need to identify and value separately any favorable financing sources such as low-interest loans, tax credits, tax-exempt bonds, and grants, rather than combining them with market value. It also notes that market value subject to restricted rents includes rental assistance but excludes favorable financing, and that these separate values help loan originators assess project security.

The document then defines and clarifies several value concepts used in appraisals. It explains “as-is” value as the value of the property in its existing physical and legal condition, and says that “as-is” should only be paired with market value subject to restricted rents when appropriate. It also discusses “prospective value,” which is the forecasted value at a future date after completion or stabilization, and recommends using this term instead of “as-improved value” because the latter can be confusing.

Finally, it addresses “value-in-use” for on-farm labor housing. For this property type, the appraiser should estimate value based on its specific use as farm labor housing, using only the Cost Approach. Since market sales and rental income data are usually unavailable, the document says a Highest and Best Use analysis is not required for value-in-use appraisals, and the appraisal report should explain why that analysis was omitted.

nodes.title: Prospective Value
nodes.summary:
This section explains two appraisal value concepts used in Agency guidance: prospective value and value-in-use.

It defines prospective value as a forecast of the property’s value at a future date, typically used for properties under construction, proposed, being converted, or not yet stabilized. It clarifies that the Agency’s term “as-improved value” is intended to mean the same thing, but that this wording can be confusing because it may be misunderstood as referring to the property’s current improved condition or as a hypothetical value as of the inspection date. The document recommends using “prospective value” instead, with the value date tied to the expected completion date of construction.

The section then defines value-in-use as the value a property has for a specific use, which may or may not be its highest and best use. For on-farm labor housing projects, the appraisal should request and report value-in-use rather than market value. It states that the Cost Approach is the only appropriate valuation method for these properties because they do not generate rental income and comparable sales are rare. It also notes that a Highest and Best Use analysis is required for market value appraisals under USPAP, but not for value-in-use appraisals, so the appraisal scope should instruct the appraiser to value the property as farm labor housing without requiring a HBU analysis, while explaining why that analysis was omitted.

nodes.title: Value-in-Use
nodes.summary:
This partial document explains several appraisal value concepts used by the Agency and when each should be applied.

Main points covered:
- **Prospective value**: Defined as the forecasted value of a property at a specified future date, typically for proposed, under-construction, or not-yet-stabilized projects. The document explains that the Agency prefers this term over “as-improved value” or “as-complete value” because those terms can cause confusion.
- **Value-in-use**: Defined as the value of a property for a specific intended use. For on-farm labor housing projects, the appraisal should use value-in-use rather than market value. The document states that the **Cost Approach** is the only appropriate valuation method for this type of property, and a highest and best use analysis is not required when value-in-use is being developed.
- **Liquidation value**: Defined as the probable price under distressed sale conditions with a limited marketing period and an urgent need to sell. It should be requested when the Agency needs to dispose of a property quickly, especially non-program or inventory properties. The document notes that liquidation value should, when possible, be derived from market value using a market-extracted discount.
- **Insurable value**: Defined as the value of the destructible portions of a property for insurance purposes. It is based on replacement cost new of the physical improvements, including direct construction costs and some allowances, but excluding indirect soft costs like administrative, professional, and financing costs. The document also mentions that “insurable cost” is sometimes used, but “insurable value” is more common.

Overall, the section focuses on how to correctly select and describe appraisal value types for different Agency housing and property situations.

nodes.title: Liquidation Value
nodes.summary: The partial document explains appraisal valuation concepts used in USDA/Farm labor housing contexts. It first notes that a report must include a limiting condition stating that value-in-use is based on the property’s specified use as farm labor housing. It then defines liquidation value in detail, listing the conditions under which a property would be sold quickly under compulsion and limited marketing, and explains when liquidation value should be requested in an appraisal and how it should generally be derived from market value. Finally, it defines insurable value for Agency multi-family housing appraisals, describing it as the value of destructible property portions used to determine insurance coverage, based on replacement cost new of physical improvements and including only direct construction costs while excluding indirect soft costs.

nodes.title: Insurable Value
nodes.summary: The partial document explains appraisal value concepts used for Agency housing properties. It first notes that a report should include a limiting condition stating that value-in-use is based on the property’s specified use as farm labor housing. It then defines liquidation value, giving a formal appraisal definition and listing the conditions under which a forced or highly time-limited sale would occur. The document explains when liquidation value should be requested in an appraisal statement of work, especially for non-program properties or inventory properties with very limited marketing time, and states that it should be derived from market value when possible. Finally, it defines insurable value for Agency multi-family housing appraisals, describing it as the replacement cost of destructible improvements and related direct construction costs only, excluding indirect soft costs, and notes that an insurable value calculation worksheet should be used as a guide.

nodes.title: Attachment 7 -B
nodes.summary: The partial document provides only an instruction stating that the response should directly return a description of the document’s main points without any additional text.

nodes.title: PROPERTY RIGHTS VALUED
nodes.summary: The document explains how appraisers should identify and describe the property rights being valued for Section 514/515 affordable housing projects. It emphasizes that the relevant interest is the fee simple estate subject to short-term leases, but that because these properties are also encumbered by restrictive-use agreements and sometimes LIHTC deed restrictions, the appraiser must carefully describe the restrictions affecting value. It references USPAP requirements to identify the real property interest being valued and defines encumbrances versus liens. The document also notes that the property interest is not purely fee simple, leased fee, or leasehold when restricted rents apply, and stresses the need for a thorough discussion of the rights and limitations included in the appraisal.

nodes.title: Attachment 7 -C
nodes.summary: The partial document only contains an instruction stating that the response should directly return a description of the main points covered in the partial document, without including any additional text.

nodes.title: SPECIFIC VALUE TYPES TO BE REQUESTED AND REPORTED BASED ON THE INTENDED USE OF THE APPRAISAL
nodes.summary:
The document explains which appraisal value types should be requested and reported based on the intended use of the appraisal for Agency Multi-Family Housing decisions. It emphasizes that the intended use determines the needed value types, and that multiple values may be required in one appraisal depending on the loan-making, servicing, preservation, or disposition purpose. It also directs State Office staff, Contracting Officers, and appraisers to use this guidance when preparing or reviewing the appraisal scope of work and report.

The text further states that the listed value types are required by Rural Development for agency appraisals, while the contract appraiser must also comply with USPAP and any applicable requirements from other lenders when multiple funding sources are involved. It notes that if Rural Development is authorized as a co-client, agency records may be used to help support the appraisal. Finally, it clarifies that the guidance does not require an appraisal when one is otherwise unnecessary, and it points readers to general guidance, a quick reference list of values to request, and detailed guidance for each value type.

nodes.title: GENERAL GUIDANCE FOR RURAL DEVELOPMENT APPRAISALS
nodes.summary:
The partial document provides general guidance for Rural Development appraisals, focusing on the types of values used, how they are applied, and what appraisers must consider when preparing reports. It explains that Rural Development typically uses three main value types: Market Value, Market Value subject to restricted rents, and Liquidation Value, while also recognizing Prospective Value for future-dated analyses such as construction or improvements. The guidance emphasizes that appraisers must follow 7 CFR Part 3560 and related handbooks, use the correct value terminology, and understand the purpose of the appraisal.

The document also outlines how highest and best use should be analyzed in market value appraisals, including the standard criteria of physical possibility, legal permissibility, financial feasibility, and maximum productivity. It explains the special hypothetical condition of “as unsubsidized conventional housing,” clarifying that Rural Development restrictions and subsidies are ignored in that analysis, but other legal restrictions still apply.

Another major topic is the treatment of subsidized housing and intangible assets. When appraising property subject to restricted rents, the appraiser must consider all restrictions and separately report intangible assets such as financing, tax credits, grants, and other nonphysical benefits, without combining them with real estate value. The document also distinguishes between real estate value and security value, explaining that security value is an underwriting tool used by the agency and is determined by the underwriter, not the appraiser.

Finally, the guidance explains the proper use of extraordinary assumptions and hypothetical conditions, stating that they must be clearly labeled and used only in appropriate situations, such as prospective values or incentive offers. It concludes with quick-reference questions for determining which value requests are needed in cases like transfers, prepayments, and rehabilitation, based on factors such as incentives, existing loans, and new funding.

nodes.title: QUICK REFERENCE OF VALUES TO REQUEST
nodes.summary:
The document is a quick reference guide for selecting the appropriate appraisal value type to request under USDA Rural Development rules, depending on the purpose of the property valuation. It outlines six main scenarios: new funds or subsequent loans, existing loans, valuation of properties that do not qualify for prepayment incentive offers, valuation of properties that do qualify for those offers, value for sale of eligible program property, and value for sale of non-program property.

For each scenario, it specifies the required value request, such as market value, prospective market value, or liquidation value, and indicates whether the value should be based on restricted rents, conventional housing assumptions, or unsubsidized conventional housing. It also notes when the appraiser must consider all existing restrictions, report intangible assets separately, or use a hypothetical condition.

nodes.title: 1: NEW FUNDS OR SUBSEQUENT LOANS (By Rural Development or others)
nodes.summary: The partial document explains appraisal requirements for new funds or subsequent loans from Rural Development or others, specifically for new construction and rehab/repair construction. It describes the required appraised value types, including “Prospective Market Value, Subject To Restricted Rents” under 7 CFR Part 3560.752(b)(1)(i), and possibly “Prospective Market Value… As-If Conventional Housing” under 7 CFR Part 3560.752(b)(1)(ii). It emphasizes that appraisals must account for rent limits, subsidies, expense abatements, restrictive-use conditions, and intangible assets separately from real estate. The document also states the intended use of each value conclusion, clarifies that prospective value relies on extraordinary assumptions and an estimated completion/stabilization date, and requires all hypothetical conditions and extraordinary assumptions to be clearly labeled in the appraisal report.

nodes.title: 2: EXISTING LOAN
nodes.summary: This partial document describes appraisal requirements for an existing loan in cases involving transfer, loan assumption, loan write-down, or subordination. It specifies that the appraised value must be “Market Value, Subject To Restricted Rents” under 7 CFR Part 3560.752(b)(1)(i), taking into account rent limits, rent subsidies, expense abatements, and restrictive-use conditions. The document emphasizes that all intangible assets must be evaluated separately from the real estate and not added to property value. It also explains that the appraisal is intended to help an underwriter determine whether the existing loan is adequately secured, and that all value conclusions in the report must use the exact required value label. Finally, it states that hypothetical conditions are not allowed, the appraisal must reflect the property’s current physical condition, and any extraordinary assumptions or hypothetical conditions must be clearly identified.

nodes.title: 3: VALUE OF PROPERTY (Property does NOT qualify for Prepayment Incentives Offers within 7 CFR 3560.656 Incentives Offers).
nodes.summary:
The document section explains the required property valuation for a property that does not qualify for prepayment incentive offers under 7 CFR Part 3560.656. It states the purposes of the valuation, including validating the sale/marketing price, determining equity, and supporting acquisition into the program. The appraisal value requested is market value under 7 CFR Part 3560.752(b)(1)(ii), with all existing restrictions and prohibitions, including restrictive-use provisions, taken into account.

It also gives special instructions for how the appraisal must be reported: the value conclusions must be clearly labeled as the specified market value, and the intended use is to determine sale/purchase value and the amount and availability of equity. Equity is defined as appraised value minus all loans and liens, not based on contract price or the preferences of any buyer or seller. The section further requires a highest and best use analysis using the four standard criteria, prohibits use of hypothetical conditions, and states that the appraisal must reflect the property’s current physical condition. Any hypothetical conditions or extraordinary assumptions must be clearly identified and made conspicuous in the report.

nodes.title: 4: VALUE OF PROPERTY (Property qualifies for Prepayment Incentives Offers within 7 CFR 3560.656 Incentives Offers. There must be no prepayment prohibitions and RUP’s must have expired.)
nodes.summary: The document section explains how to determine the value of property for prepayment incentive offers under USDA Rural Development rules. It states that the property must qualify under 7 CFR Part 3560.656, with no prepayment prohibitions and expired RUPs. The appraisal must provide a market value based on a hypothetical condition treating the property as unsubsidized conventional housing, and this value is used to validate sale/marketing price and determine available equity. It also clarifies that equity equals appraised value minus all loans and liens, not a contract price or buyer/seller preferences. The appraiser must ignore Rural Development subsidies or restrictions in the highest and best use analysis, while still considering other legal restrictions, and the appraisal must reflect current physical condition. Any hypothetical conditions or extraordinary assumptions must be clearly labeled and conspicuous in the report.

nodes.title: 5: VALUE FOR SALE - ELIGIBLE AS PROGRAM PROPERTY
nodes.summary: This section explains the valuation requirements for property that is eligible as program property for sale, including REOs and foreclosures. It distinguishes between two required appraisal values: market value assuming conventional housing under a hypothetical condition, and market value subject to restricted rents. It specifies that appraisals must consider applicable rent limits, subsidies, abatements, restrictive-use conditions, and intangible assets separately from real estate. The section also states the intended use of these values for determining acceptable bid or sale prices, requires highest-and-best-use analysis, and mandates that all hypothetical conditions and extraordinary assumptions be clearly labeled.

nodes.title: 6: VALUE FOR SALE - NON PROGRAM PROPERTY
nodes.summary: The document section explains valuation requirements for non-program properties being sold, such as properties leaving the USDA program or REO properties. It specifies that the appraiser should provide market value as if the property were a conventional market property, based on highest and best use analysis without USDA program restrictions. In some cases, liquidation value may also be required, using a USDA-specified marketing period. It emphasizes that all value conclusions must be clearly labeled, that the appraisal is intended to help determine an acceptable bid or sale price, and that highest and best use analysis should follow the standard four criteria. It also notes that when liquidation value is requested, USDA must provide the marketing period, and any hypothetical conditions or extraordinary assumptions must be clearly identified in the report.

nodes.title: ATTACHMENT 7 -D
nodes.summary:
The partial document outlines the standard statement of work for multi-family housing contract appraisals under USDA Rural Development. It explains the background and purpose of requiring appraisals for subsidized multi-family housing properties, and states that all appraisals must comply with USPAP and Rural Housing Service supplemental standards. It also details the professional qualifications required of appraisers, including state certification as a General Appraiser and specialized knowledge of subsidized housing, valuation methods, financing, and use restrictions.

The document further describes the appraisal procurement process through a Request for Quote (RFQ) and a Contract for Appraisal Services (CAS). It explains what information each document contains, how appraisers must respond, and their responsibility to understand the assignment scope, fee, timing, delivery requirements, and report distribution instructions. Finally, it addresses subcontracting restrictions, stating that the appraiser or qualified staff must perform the work and that subcontracting requires written consent from the Rural Development Contracting Officer.

nodes.title: STANDARD STATEMENT OF WORK (SOW)
nodes.summary:
The partial document outlines USDA Rural Development’s standard statement of work for appraisals of multi-family housing properties, including the background, required professional qualifications, and the process for responding to an RFQ and entering into a Contract for Appraisal Services. It covers key assignment rules such as subcontracting limits, post-award communication with the COR, client and property contact protocols, confidentiality, nondiscrimination, and handling of assumptions, environmental hazards, and ADA considerations.

It also specifies required appraisal report formats, acceptable valuation approaches, and value types, along with detailed requirements for complete appraisals. These include USPAP compliance, executive summary, legal description, photographs, maps, exhibits, support for income approach analyses, market rent conclusions, deferred maintenance treatment, favorable financing analysis, and inclusion of relevant supporting documents in the addenda.

The document further addresses review and accountability expectations, defense of appraisals if challenged, and payment terms including liquidated damages for late delivery. The attached example appraisal information sheet shows how the SOW is populated in practice, listing appraisal type, report type, client and contacts, intended users and use, property details, and project financing sources for a specific rural housing development.

nodes.title: Attachment 7 -E
nodes.summary: The partial document is an example Appraisal Information Sheet attached to a Scope of Work for USDA Rural Development. It identifies the appraisal type, report format, client, USDA contact, property contact, intended users, intended use, and purpose of the appraisal. It then describes the subject property as a proposed USDA Section 514/516 off-farm labor housing complex called Bedford Falls Apartments in Oregon, including its address, ownership, property interest appraised, and key physical details such as the number of units, buildings, site size, square footage, unit mix, amenities, and planned completion date. The document also outlines the project financing structure, listing the USDA Section 514 loan and 516 grant, a HOME grant from Clackamas County, and tax credit funding from OHCS.

nodes.title: APPRAISAL INFORMATION SHEET (EXAMPLE), ATTACHMENT TO SOW
nodes.summary: The partial document provides an example appraisal information sheet and a data package checklist used for USDA Rural Development housing projects. It outlines key appraisal details such as appraisal type, report type, format, client and contact information, intended users and use, property type, ownership, address, and a detailed description of the proposed Bedford Falls Apartments project, including unit mix, site size, building characteristics, amenities, and parking. It also lists the project’s financing sources, including a USDA Section 514 loan, Section 516 grant, HOME grant, and tax credits. The document specifies required value opinions, including prospective market value subject to restricted rents, values of the loan subsidy, grant, tax credits, and insurable value, and states that the Cost and Income Approaches are required while the Sales Comparison Approach is not. It also notes the presence of rental assistance, assumptions and limiting conditions, number of report copies, and mailing instructions. The second attachment is a checklist of required appraisal data and supporting documents, such as contracts, legal descriptions, mortgage documents, environmental and inspection reports, market study, surveys, plans, rent rolls, operating statements, budgets, cost estimates, ownership history, and financing details.

nodes.title: Attachment 7 -F
nodes.summary: The document is an appraisal data package checklist for USDA Rural Development housing projects. It lists the key documents and information that must be included in an appraisal submission, such as the sales contract, legal description, mortgage or deed of trust, HAP contract, restrictive use provisions, environmental and lead-based paint reports, inspection reports, market study, prior appraisal, survey, site and floor plans, unit mix, rent roll, tenant waiting list, operating statements, budget, construction or rehabilitation cost documents, ownership history, and detailed financing information.

nodes.title: APPRAISAL DATA PACKAGE CHECKLIST
nodes.summary: This partial document contains two Rural Development multi-family housing attachments. The first is an “Appraisal Data Package Checklist,” which lists the documents and information that should be included in an appraisal package, such as the sales contract, legal description, mortgage or deed of trust, HAP contract, environmental and lead-based paint reports, inspections, market study, prior appraisal, survey, site and floor plans, unit mix, rent roll, tenant waiting list, operating statements, budget, construction or rehabilitation cost documents, ownership history, and financing details. The second is a “Sample Transmittal Letter,” which provides a template for commissioning an appraisal from an MAI appraiser, including the property description, scope of valuation, purchase order reference, deadline for completion, submission requirements, payment terms, and enclosed contract documents.

nodes.title: Attachment 7 -G
nodes.summary: This partial document is a sample transmittal letter used in Rural Development’s multi-family housing appraisal process. It outlines how to formally engage an appraisal firm, including the property details, quoted appraisal price, required value estimate, submission deadline, and delivery of three copies of the completed appraisal. It also explains that the government has 15 days to review the appraisal, invoices should be sent to the Contracting Officer’s Representative, and payment will follow the Prompt Payment Act upon acceptance. The letter references enclosed procurement and contract documents such as the purchase order, COR designation, and appraisal contract terms, and it closes by confirming that the letter memorializes prior oral discussions and sets the contract completion dates.

nodes.title: SAMPLE TRANSMITTAL LETTER
nodes.summary: The partial document includes two main sections from USDA Rural Development appraisal guidance. First, it provides a sample transmittal letter for ordering a multi-family housing appraisal, outlining what details to include such as the property address, requested value estimate, delivery deadline, number of copies, invoice instructions, and contract enclosures. Second, it begins a guide for valuing interest credit subsidy in Section 514 and 515 housing appraisals, explaining what interest credit subsidy is, why it matters in appraisals, and the basic methodology for calculating its present value using discounted cash flow.

nodes.title: Attachment 7 -H
nodes.summary:
This document explains how to value the interest credit subsidy in USDA Rural Housing Service direct loans for Section 514 and Section 515 multi-family housing properties. It describes interest credit as a federal subsidy that lowers the borrower’s effective mortgage rate, creating a monthly savings stream equal to the difference between a conventional loan payment and the actual reduced payment. The guide states that when appraising properties with restricted rents, the subsidy is part of the security value and must be included alongside the real estate value.

The document also outlines the valuation method as a discounted cash flow calculation using three key inputs: the payment amount (the monthly savings from interest credit), the discount rate (the alternative conventional mortgage rate), and the number of periods (the loan term). It emphasizes that the USDA note rate should not be used as the conventional comparison rate, since it does not reflect a market mortgage rate available to the borrower.

nodes.title: GUIDE FOR VALUATION OF INTEREST CREDIT SUBSIDY
nodes.summary:
The document explains how to value the interest credit subsidy for USDA Rural Housing Service direct loans under Sections 514 and 515. It defines interest credit subsidy as the gap between a borrower’s reduced mortgage payment and the payment that would be required at a conventional market rate, and treats that subsidy as a present value cash flow benefit. The guide outlines the valuation method using three inputs: the payment savings, the conventional market interest rate as the discount rate, and the loan term or remaining term as the discount period.

It also distinguishes between existing properties and new construction. For existing properties, the subsidy is generally valued by discounting the monthly payment savings over the remaining loan term. For proposed new construction with a loan term shorter than the amortization period, the analysis must also account for a balloon payment by subtracting the present value of that balloon from the value of the payment savings. An example problem with a Section 515 loan shows how to calculate the subsidy using financial calculator steps and arrives at a rounded value of about $591,000.

The conclusion emphasizes that appraisers should obtain the current loan balance and use the actual borrower payment and current conventional market rate. It also notes that interest credit subsidy must be valued separately from the real property’s restricted-rent market value and separately for any original and subsequent RHS loans.

nodes.title: Attachment 7 -I
nodes.summary: This document is a USDA Rural Development form used to calculate a property’s insurable value. It provides fields for identifying the property and then breaks down the valuation into sections for base cost, total base cost per square foot, building area, and total replacement cost new. It also lists exclusions such as excavations, foundations, site work, site improvements, architect’s fees, and underground piping, along with inclusions like appliance packages and patios/balconies. The form concludes by combining replacement cost, exclusions, and inclusions to determine the final insurable value.

nodes.title: USDA Rural Development Insurable Value Calculation
nodes.summary:
The partial document contains two USDA Rural Development attachment forms related to property appraisal processing.

The first form, Attachment 7-I, is an “Insurable Value Calculation” worksheet. It includes fields for property identification and a structured calculation of insurable value, covering base cost items such as main structure, sprinkler, and other costs, along with adjustments/multipliers, building area, total replacement cost new, exclusions, inclusions, and the final concluded insurable value. It is laid out as a blank calculation template with dollar and square-foot entries.

The second form, Attachment 7-J, is a “Cover Letter for Released Appraisal.” It is a standard letter template used by USDA Rural Development to send a copy of an appraisal report to the recipient. The letter explains that the Rural Development State Appraiser can be contacted about errors or deficiencies, will review whether issues are material to value, and may accept the appraisal, accept it with revisions, accept it as modified by the reviewer, or reject it. It also notes that the appraisal review report is for internal Agency use and will not be released.

nodes.title: Attachment 7 -J
nodes.summary: This document is a USDA Rural Development attachment providing a cover letter template for releasing a real property appraisal. It includes placeholders for the date, office address, property information, and sender details. The letter states that a copy of the appraisal report is enclosed and explains that the Rural Development State Appraiser should be contacted to discuss any errors or deficiencies found in the appraisal. It also outlines the possible outcomes of the appraisal review: acceptance, acceptance with appraiser revisions, acceptance with reviewer modifications, or rejection. Finally, it notes that the appraisal review report is for internal agency use and will not be released.

nodes.title: COVER LETTER FOR RELEASED APPRAISAL
nodes.summary: This partial document is a USDA Rural Development form letter used to transmit a released real property appraisal to the agency’s State Office. It includes placeholders for the date, office address, property information, and sender details. The letter explains that the enclosed appraisal report has been provided for review and directs recipients to contact the Rural Development State Appraiser with any identified errors or deficiencies. It also describes the review process, stating that the State Appraiser will determine whether issues are material to value and decide among four outcomes: accept the appraisal, accept it with revisions by the appraiser, accept it with modifications by the reviewer, or reject it. Finally, it notes that the appraisal review report is for internal agency use and will not be released.

nodes.title: CHAPTER 8: LOAN CLOSING AND PROJECT LEASE -UP
nodes.summary: The partial document contains only an instruction telling the responder to directly return a description of the partial document’s main points and not include any other text.

nodes.title: 8.1 INTRODUCTION
nodes.summary: The partial document is a chapter from HB-1-3560 describing Chapter 8, which focuses on loan closing and project lease-up for multifamily housing projects. It explains that after loan approval, the Loan Originator prepares for closing and later monitors lease-up after construction is completed. The chapter outlines the loan closing process in three stages: preparing for closing, going to closing, and post-closing activities, including document preparation, execution of closing documents, transfer of property ownership, completion of the loan docket, and forwarding files to loan servicing staff. It also introduces Section 2, which covers Agency oversight during project lease-up, including monitoring rent and occupancy compliance through meetings and site visits. The excerpt further provides an overview of loan closing responsibilities, the roles of the borrower, Agency, closing agent, and Loan Processing Staff, and emphasizes that closing should occur promptly after loan approval because delays increase costs and reduce effective use of program funds. It also notes that the timing of closing depends on the project’s construction financing method, such as interim financing or multiple advances.

nodes.title: SECTION 1: LOAN CLOSING [7 CFR 3560.72]
nodes.summary: The partial document introduces Chapter 8 of HB-1-3560, which covers loan closing and project lease-up for Multi-Family Housing projects. It explains that after loan approval, the Loan Originator must prepare for closing, conduct the closing, and complete post-closing tasks such as finalizing the loan docket, filing documents, and transferring the file to Loan Servicing. The chapter also outlines the Agency’s responsibilities during project lease-up, including monitoring borrower compliance with rent and occupancy requirements through meetings and site visits. The section on loan closing provides an overview of the timing and general requirements, the roles of the borrower, Agency, closing agent, and Loan Processing Staff, and the importance of moving quickly from loan approval to closing, especially because obligated funds create costs to the Agency and taxpayers. It also notes that the timing of closing depends on the project’s construction financing method, such as interim financing or multiple advances.

nodes.title: 8.2 OVERVIEW
nodes.summary:
The partial document introduces Chapter 8 of HB-1-3560, which focuses on loan closing and project lease-up for Multi-Family Housing projects. It explains that after loan approval, the Loan Originator must prepare for closing and later oversee lease-up activities. The chapter is divided into two main sections: Section 1 covers the loan closing process, including preparation, the closing itself, and post-closing tasks; Section 2 covers Agency responsibilities during project lease-up, such as monitoring compliance with rent and occupancy requirements.

The excerpt also gives an overview of loan closing, outlining the roles of the borrower, the Agency, the closing agent, and Loan Processing Staff. It emphasizes that the timing of closing depends on the project financing method, such as interim financing or multiple advances, and stresses the importance of moving quickly from loan approval to closing to minimize costs and make efficient use of program funds.

nodes.title: 8.3 TIMING OF LOAN CLOSING
nodes.summary:
The partial document explains Chapter 8 of HB-1-3560, focusing on loan closing and project lease-up for Multi-Family Housing projects. It introduces the overall process after loan approval, including preparing for closing, attending closing, and completing post-closing tasks, as well as the Agency’s role in monitoring lease-up after construction is finished.

The section on loan closing covers:
- The timing of closing, which depends on whether the project uses interim financing or multiple advances.
- The importance of moving quickly from loan approval to closing to reduce costs and use funds efficiently.
- The responsibilities of the borrower, Agency, closing agent, and Loan Processing Staff.
- Requirements that must be met before closing, such as secure financing, completed or adequately funded construction, proper property security and lien position, and compliance with program and civil rights requirements.

Overall, the excerpt describes the key rules, timing, and prerequisites for successfully closing a loan and transitioning into project lease-up.

nodes.title: 8.4 LOAN CLOSING REQUIREMENTS
nodes.summary: The partial document explains USDA Rural Development loan closing procedures and requirements under HB-1-3560. It covers the timing of loan closing for interim financing versus multiple advances, then outlines the conditions that must be met before closing, including secure financing, completed or adequately funded construction, proper property security and lien position, and compliance with program and civil rights requirements. It also includes a summary of agency staff responsibilities during pre-closing, loan closing, and post-closing, such as selecting and coordinating with the closing agent, reviewing documents, issuing closing instructions, attending the closing, recording security instruments, entering accounting data, and forwarding the final loan file for servicing.

nodes.title: Exhibit 8 -1
nodes.summary: The partial document contains no substantive content beyond an instruction to directly return a description without any additional text.

nodes.title: Summary of Loan Closing Activities Performed by Agency Staff
nodes.summary: The document outlines the main loan closing activities performed by agency staff for construction loans using interim financing. It covers three phases: preparing for closing, loan closing, and post-closing activities. In preparation, staff select a closing agent or title company, review title documents, provide materials to the Office of General Counsel, and send closing instructions to the closing agent. Before closing, the closing agent arranges the closing date and prepares documents, while agency staff review the promissory note and interest credit agreement, determine the initial check amount for multiple advances, and order the loan check. During closing, staff may attend if needed, while the closing agent reviews documents, obtains signatures, and disburses funds. After closing, the agent records the mortgage or deed of trust and secures title, and agency staff review and sign closing documents, enter data into the accounting system, establish the amortization effective date, compile final documents for legal review if needed, and forward the loan docket to loan servicing staff.

nodes.title: 8.5 CHOOSING THE CLOSING AGENT AND TITLE COMPANY
nodes.summary: The partial document explains how a closing agent or title company is chosen for Agency real estate loans and outlines their responsibilities during the closing process. It states that the borrower selects an attorney or title company, subject to Agency approval and RD Instruction 1927-B. It also emphasizes that closing agents must report the recipients and amounts of section 514 loan and section 516 grant payments on the HUD-1 Settlement Statement and Form RD 3550-25, using an attachment if needed. Finally, it notes that the closing agent performs specific pre-closing and post-closing duties, with a detailed task list referenced in an exhibit.

nodes.title: 8.6 CLOSING AGENT RESPONSIBILITIES
nodes.summary:
The partial document explains the requirements for selecting a closing agent/title company and outlines the closing agent’s responsibilities for Agency real estate loans. It states that every loan closing must use an approved closing agent, attorney, or title company chosen by the borrower and selected under RD Instruction 1927-B. It also requires disclosure of the recipients and amounts of section 514 loan and section 516 grant payments on the HUD-1 Settlement Statement and Form RD 3550-25 to help prevent fraud, waste, abuse, kickbacks, referral fees, and equity skimming.

The document then details the closing agent’s duties before, during, and after closing. Before closing, the agent must provide a title insurance binder, verify closing conditions, check lien position, insurance, taxes, flood coverage, and other required information, and arrange the closing date. On the day of closing, the agent must complete the closing statement, confirm there are no outstanding judgments, review and sign documents, and disburse funds. After closing, the agent must record security documents, return executed documents to the Agency, and secure a title insurance policy within 14 days.

nodes.title: Exhibit 8 -2
nodes.summary: This partial document outlines the responsibilities of a closing agent in a loan transaction, organized by stage: prior to closing, on the day of closing, and after closing. It explains that the closing agent must provide a title insurance binder, verify that closing conditions can be met, ensure required insurance, taxes, and other borrower information are in place, and coordinate the closing date. On the day of closing, the agent must prepare the closing statement, check for outstanding judgments, review and sign documents, and disburse funds. After closing, the agent must record security documents, return executed documents to the agency, and obtain final title insurance within the required timeframe.

nodes.title: Responsibilities of a Closing Agent
nodes.summary: The partial document explains the responsibilities involved in closing a loan, especially the duties of the closing agent and loan processing staff. It covers what must happen before closing, on the day of closing, and after closing, including title insurance, lien and judgment checks, insurance and tax verification, disbursement of funds, recording documents, and returning executed materials to the Agency. It also outlines the Loan Originator’s role in coordinating the closing process, working with the applicant and closing agent, ensuring all closing conditions are met, and reviewing the title insurance binder to confirm ownership, lien status, and judgment resolution.

nodes.title: 8.7 LOAN PROCESSING STAFF RESPONSIBILITIES
nodes.summary:
The partial document outlines loan processing staff responsibilities, focusing on the Loan Originator’s role in coordinating the loan closing process. It explains that the Loan Originator must ensure all closing conditions are satisfied, required documents are prepared, and coordination occurs with both the applicant and closing agent before closing. It also states that closing cannot happen until all conditions from the Agency’s approval documents are met.

The document further describes the Loan Originator’s duties in working with the applicant to explain closing requirements, including conditions to be met, selection of a closing agent, necessary documents, insurance requirements, and applicant responsibilities. It notes that these items may be discussed in earlier meetings or at a preclosing meeting.

Finally, it begins a section on reviewing the title insurance binder, instructing the Loan Originator to verify that the borrower will own the property, prior liens are removed, and outstanding judgments are resolved.

nodes.title: A. Work with the Applicant
nodes.summary:
This section explains the responsibilities of the Loan Originator in the loan closing process. It emphasizes coordinating all closing activities, ensuring all closing conditions are met, and making sure required documents are prepared before the closing date. The Loan Originator must work with the applicant, the closing agent, and the Office of General Counsel to secure closing instructions and verify that closing does not occur until all loan approval conditions have been satisfied.

The section also outlines the Loan Originator’s duty to educate the applicant about closing requirements, including conditions that must be met before closing, how to select a closing agent, what documents must be provided, insurance requirements, and the applicant’s responsibilities at closing. Finally, it begins describing the review of the title insurance binder, which must confirm proper property ownership transfer and that prior liens and judgments have been addressed.

nodes.title: B. Review Title Insurance Binder
nodes.summary:
This partial document outlines the responsibilities of loan processing staff, especially the Loan Originator, in preparing a loan for closing. It explains that the Loan Originator must coordinate the closing process, ensure all conditions for closing are met, and work with the applicant, closing agent, and OGC to prepare and review required documents.

Main points covered include:
- Working with the applicant to explain closing requirements, responsibilities, insurance, and needed documents.
- Reviewing the title insurance binder to confirm ownership, clear liens, resolved judgments, and acceptable property rights/title exceptions.
- Preparing and submitting the loan docket to OGC to obtain loan closing instructions.
- Sending closing instructions and supporting materials to the closing agent once OGC approves the loan for closing.
- Obtaining the loan check, with disbursement dependent on filing the mortgage and the type of financing involved.

nodes.title: C. Prepare Documents for Closing Instructions
nodes.summary: The partial document explains the loan closing process and responsibilities of the Loan Originator and OGC. It covers reviewing title and property rights to ensure the lien position and title are acceptable, returning documents for correction if information is missing or unsatisfactory, assembling and submitting the loan docket to OGC, and obtaining closing instructions once loan conditions are met. It also describes forwarding closing instructions and supporting documents to the closing agent, ensuring all required forms are executed and returned, and obtaining the loan check, noting that funds cannot be disbursed until the mortgage is filed.

nodes.title: D. Provide Closing Agent with Closing Instructions and Materials
nodes.summary: The document section explains the closing process for a loan, focusing on ensuring the title and property rights are acceptable, reviewing legal descriptions and title exceptions for any risks to the lien position, and returning incomplete or unsatisfactory title insurance binders for correction. It outlines the Loan Originator’s responsibilities to assemble and submit the loan docket to OGC for review, obtain closing instructions only after all loan conditions are satisfied, and then provide those instructions and required documents to the closing agent. It also notes that all necessary closing forms and supporting materials must be attached and executed, and concludes by describing the process for obtaining the loan check, including the rule that funds cannot be disbursed until the mortgage is filed.

nodes.title: E. Obtain Loan Check
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines the loan closing process for an HB-1-3560 project. It focuses on reviewing title information and legal descriptions to ensure the property’s lien position, title, suitability, and security value are acceptable before closing. It explains how the Loan Originator must prepare the loan docket, obtain closing instructions from OGC, and send those instructions and required documents to the closing agent. It also covers obtaining the loan check through AMAS, including checks on disbursed amounts, accrued interest, and the Maximum Debt Limit, as well as how funds are handled for interim financing versus multiple advances. Finally, it describes establishing the borrower’s monthly payment schedule, setting up the initial disbursement, and notes that loan closing involves execution of all required documents to transfer ownership and secure financing.

nodes.title: F. Establish Payment Schedule
nodes.summary: The partial document explains procedures for loan disbursement and closing in HB-1-3560. It covers how loan checks are issued for interim financing versus multiple-advance loans, including holding back funds in a supervised account when needed. It also describes the Loan Originator’s required steps in AMAS to request a loan/grant check, verify undisbursed amounts, disbursed amounts, accrued interest, and Maximum Debt Limit, and complete the check request screen. In addition, it outlines establishing the borrower’s monthly payment schedule and how initial disbursements are handled at closing, including funds for land, legal fees, and other closing costs. The section ends by introducing loan closing actions, where the Agency and borrower execute the necessary documents to transfer ownership and secure financing.

nodes.title: G. Establish Initial Disbursement
nodes.summary: The passage explains how loan checks and disbursements are handled during loan closing, especially for interim financing and multiple-advance loans. It covers the use of AMAS to request loan checks, including checking undisbursed amounts, disbursed amounts, accrued interest, and the Maximum Debt Limit before submitting a request. It also describes how payment schedules are established, with monthly payments due on the first of each month and any pre-first-payment interest added to the loan. In addition, it outlines initial disbursement procedures, noting that interim financing may disburse all funds at closing or place held-back funds in a supervised account, while multiple advances release funds over time starting with initial expenses. The section concludes by introducing actions required at loan closing, where the Agency and borrower execute the necessary documents to complete the transaction.

nodes.title: 8.8 ACTIONS AT LOAN CLOSING
nodes.summary: The partial document explains procedures for loan disbursement, closing, and postclosing administration. It covers how loan checks are issued for interim financing or multiple advances, including use of the AMAS system, checking loan balances and debt limits, and preparing payment requests. It describes establishing the borrower’s monthly payment schedule and how initial disbursements are handled, including holding funds in supervised accounts when needed. The document then outlines required actions at loan closing, such as reviewing and executing all necessary documents, handling any changes to security instruments or promissory notes, and disbursing funds. Finally, it details postclosing responsibilities: reviewing closing documents, recording the mortgage or deed, obtaining title insurance, and establishing the amortization effective date for interest accrual and repayment scheduling.

nodes.title: 8.9 POSTCLOSING ACTIVITIES
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines loan closing and postclosing procedures. It explains that at closing, required documents must be reviewed and executed, any changes to security instruments or promissory notes must be clearly marked and initialed, and funds must be disbursed as appropriate. It then covers postclosing responsibilities, including obtaining and reviewing all closing documents, ensuring the mortgage is recorded and lien priority is proper, securing and delivering title insurance within 14 days, and establishing the Amortization Effective Date, which determines when interest accrues and the payment schedule begins.

nodes.title: A. Obtain and Review Closing Documents
nodes.summary: The partial document explains loan closing and postclosing procedures. It covers execution and review of closing documents, handling any required changes to security instruments or promissory notes, and disbursement of funds at closing. It then outlines postclosing responsibilities, including obtaining and reviewing all closing documents, ensuring the mortgage is properly recorded to secure lien priority, obtaining and delivering title insurance, and establishing the Amortization Effective Date, which determines when interest accrues and the repayment schedule begins.

nodes.title: B. Record Mortgage or Deed
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines loan closing and postclosing procedures. It explains that at closing, all required documents must be reviewed and executed, any changes to security instruments or promissory notes must be clearly marked and initialed, and funds must be disbursed as appropriate. It then describes postclosing responsibilities: collecting and reviewing closing documents, ensuring the mortgage is properly recorded and lien priority is secured, obtaining and delivering title insurance within 14 days, and establishing the amortization effective date that determines when interest accrual and loan payments begin.

nodes.title: C. Secure Title
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines the loan closing and postclosing process. It explains that at closing, required documents from Attachment 8-A must be reviewed and executed, any changes to the security instrument or promissory note must be clearly marked and initialed, and funds are disbursed as appropriate. After closing, documents must be obtained, reviewed, recorded, and filed. The Loan Originator reviews the mortgage and other closing documents to confirm proper lien priority and execution, then signs the Loan Closing Instructions once everything is verified. The closing agent is responsible for recording the mortgage or deed of trust and providing the Agency with recorded or confirmed copies. The closing agent must also secure the title insurance policy and deliver it within 14 days. Finally, the document explains how to establish the Amortization Effective Date, which determines when interest accrual and payment scheduling begin.

nodes.title: D. Establish the Amortization Effective Date
nodes.summary: This section outlines the required steps at loan closing and the postclosing process. It explains how closing documents must be reviewed and executed, how changes to security instruments or promissory notes must be marked and initialed, and that funds are to be disbursed as appropriate. After closing, the document covers the handling of all closing documents, including review by the Loan Originator, recording of the mortgage or deed of trust, and delivery of the title insurance policy within 14 days. It also describes how to establish the Amortization Effective Date, enter closing information into AMAS, complete and transfer the loan docket to servicing staff, and update the Multi-Family Housing Information System (MFIS).

nodes.title: E. Put Closing Information into AMAS
nodes.summary: This excerpt outlines post-closing loan processing steps. It instructs the Loan Originator to enter closing information into AMAS using the Loan Closing screen, then complete the loan docket and transfer it to servicing staff after all closing documents are filed. It also notes that the Loan Originator must enter the appropriate information into the Multi-Family Housing Information System (MFIS).

nodes.title: F. Complete Loan Docket and Transfer to Servicing Staff
nodes.summary: The partial document explains the final steps in the loan closing process. It instructs the Loan Originator to enter closing information into AMAS using the Loan Closing screen, then assemble all completed closing documents into the loan docket and transfer it to servicing staff. It also states that the Loan Originator must enter the appropriate loan information into the Multi-Family Housing Information System (MFIS) for recordkeeping and servicing purposes.

nodes.title: G. Populate Multi -Family Housing Information System (MFIS)
nodes.summary: The document describes post-closing loan processing and early project operations for USDA multifamily housing loans. It covers entering closing information into AMAS, completing and transferring the loan docket to servicing staff, and recording information in MFIS. It then explains project lease-up requirements after closing, including that the borrower must follow program rules for management, occupancy, rents, subsidies, and financial management, with marketing beginning at least 90 days before rental activity under the AFHMP. Finally, it outlines monitoring responsibilities during lease-up, emphasizing coordination between loan processing and servicing staff to ensure affirmative marketing, eligibility and occupancy rules, and correct rent charges are being followed, with monthly review of project worksheets and tenant certifications.

nodes.title: SECTION 2: PROJECT LEASE -UP
nodes.summary: The partial document contains only an instruction requesting a description of the document’s main points, but no substantive content to summarize.

nodes.title: 8.10 PROJECT LEASE-UP REQUIREMENTS
nodes.summary: The partial document explains project lease-up requirements for borrowers after loan closing. It states that borrowers must follow program rules for project management, occupancy, rents, subsidies, and financial management, and must begin affirmative fair housing marketing at least 90 days before rental activity. It also describes the Agency’s role in helping borrowers understand their responsibilities and closely monitoring early lease-up. The document lists the relevant regulatory sections for these requirements. It further outlines lease-up monitoring procedures, including coordination between Loan Processing and Loan Servicing Staff, monthly review of project worksheets and tenant certifications, and verification that affirmative marketing, occupancy eligibility, and rent rules are being properly followed.

nodes.title: 8.11 MONITORING PROJECT LEASE-UP
nodes.summary:
The partial document explains the requirements and procedures for project lease-up in Section 2 of HB-1-3560. It covers what happens once the loan is closed and the applicant becomes a borrower, including responsibilities for project management, occupancy, rents, subsidies, financial management, and affirmative fair housing marketing. It emphasizes that marketing must begin at least 90 days before rental activity and that borrowers must comply with rent and occupancy rules during lease-up.

The document also describes Agency monitoring of lease-up through project worksheets, tenant certifications, and close coordination between Loan Processing Staff, Loan Servicing Staff, and Field Staff. It identifies key early monitoring events: the pre-occupancy conference, the 90-day visit, and the 11-month warranty inspection.

A major portion focuses on the pre-occupancy conference, held 90 to 120 days before lease-up, where the Agency, borrower, and management agent review program requirements and agree on how the property will be operated. Topics include management documents, initial operating capital and budget issues, tenant eligibility and grievance procedures, and civil rights/affirmative marketing requirements.

nodes.title: A. Pre- Occupancy Conference
nodes.summary: The partial document explains early project monitoring procedures for housing operations, focusing on three key checkpoints: the pre-occupancy conference, the 90-day visit, and the 11-month warranty inspection. It details the purpose and timing of the pre-occupancy conference, which occurs before lease-up and brings together the Agency, borrower, and management agent to review program requirements, management documents, initial operating funds, tenant eligibility and grievance issues, and civil rights obligations such as fair housing marketing, required posters, recordkeeping, and language access. It then describes the 90-day visit, held after occupancy begins, as a review of early lease-up progress and compliance, with emphasis on tenant files, the approved marketing plan, waiting list procedures, use of the borrower’s operating capital contribution, and financial management systems.

nodes.title: B. The 90-Day Visit
nodes.summary: The partial document describes required early project oversight and compliance steps for a USDA rural housing project. It explains that the approved marketing plan and fair housing-related posters must be posted and provided to the borrower, along with a review of civil rights data collection requirements and the need to provide materials in other languages when a significant portion of the population needs them. It then outlines the 90-day visit, which should occur between days 60 and 90 after occupancy begins, to confirm lease-up progress and compliance with program rules by reviewing tenant files, the marketing plan, waiting list procedures, borrower contribution use, financial management systems, and project maintenance. Finally, it covers the 11-month warranty inspection, which is a physical inspection near the end of the builder’s warranty period to identify warranty-related repairs and other maintenance issues, and notes that the inspection should be tracked through MFIS.

nodes.title: C. The 11-Month Warranty Inspection
nodes.summary: The passage outlines post-construction project maintenance requirements and the 11-month warranty inspection process. It notes that the project should have a maintenance plan and that initial maintenance must be completed satisfactorily. It then explains that about eleven months after project completion, the Agency should perform a physical inspection to check the condition of items covered by the one-year builder’s warranty, identify any needed repairs before the warranty expires, and assess broader maintenance or physical condition issues. It also states that this inspection should be tracked through the MFIS.

nodes.title: ATTACHMENT 8 -A
nodes.summary: This attachment lists the required loan closing documents and pre-closing items for a housing loan. It covers the documents that must be prepared and executed at closing, including the promissory note, interest credit and rental assistance agreement, mortgage, financing statements, title insurance, organizational papers, affidavits, tax and insurance evidence, deeds, and loan closing instructions. It also identifies additional items that must be completed by loan closing, such as meeting specified loan conditions, providing evidence of initial operating capital and equity contribution, and certifying the availability or unavailability of other government assistance.

nodes.title: LOAN CLOSING DOCUMENTS
nodes.summary: This document section lists the loan closing documents and requirements for a housing loan transaction. It specifies the forms and legal papers that must be prepared before closing and executed or submitted at closing, including the promissory note, interest credit and rental assistance agreement, mortgage, financing statements, title insurance, organizational documents, affidavits, tax and insurance evidence, deeds, and closing instructions. It also identifies additional items that must be completed by loan closing, such as satisfying loan conditions, documenting initial operating capital and equity contributions, and certifying the availability or unavailability of other government assistance.

nodes.title: Other items to be completed not later than loan closing:
nodes.summary: The partial document covers two main areas: loan closing requirements and the start of Chapter 9 on project construction. It lists the documents and certifications that must be prepared and submitted at loan closing, including the promissory note, mortgage, insurance, title insurance, organization papers, tax evidence, and other closing conditions such as initial capital and equity contributions. It then introduces the construction chapter, explaining that the borrower is responsible for managing construction while the Agency monitors the process to protect its interests and ensure compliance with plans and specifications. The chapter overview outlines the Agency’s construction oversight role, including review of design documents, contractor concurrence, preconstruction conferences, inspections, change orders, payments, and final close-out.

nodes.title: CHAPTER 9: PROJECT CONSTRUCTION
nodes.summary: This partial document introduces Chapter 9 on project construction in HB-1-3560 and explains that, while the borrower manages construction, the Agency must oversee the process to ensure compliance with project plans and protect the government’s security interest. It outlines the chapter’s structure: construction requirements, contracting procedures, and Agency responsibilities during construction and close-out. The excerpt also describes the Agency’s role from the initial application through review of planning documents, coordination with environmental requirements, concurrence in contractor selection and contracts, required preconstruction conferences, inspections during construction, approval of change orders and payments, and final inspection before contract close-out.

nodes.title: 9.1 INTRODUCTION
nodes.summary:
The partial document introduces Chapter 9 on project construction and explains that while the borrower is responsible for managing construction, the Agency must monitor the process to protect its security interest and ensure compliance with project plans and specifications. It outlines the chapter’s structure: construction requirements, contracting procedures, and the Agency’s oversight during construction through inspections, change orders, payments, and closeout.

It also describes the Agency’s role from the start of the application process, including the State Architect’s review of plans, specifications, and cost estimates, and coordination with environmental requirements. The Agency must concur in contractor selection and the construction contract, require a preconstruction conference, and continue oversight throughout construction with final inspection before closeout.

nodes.title: 9.2 OVERVIEW OF THE AGENCY’S ROLE IN THE CONSTRUCTION PROCESS
nodes.summary:
This partial document outlines Chapter 9 of HB-1-3560 on project construction. It explains that construction management is the borrower’s responsibility, but the Agency actively oversees the process to ensure compliance with project plans, specifications, and government security interests. The chapter is organized into sections covering construction requirements, contracting procedures, and the Agency’s role during construction.

It describes the Agency’s role from initial application through final inspection, including the State Architect’s review of design documents, consultation on environmental mitigation, and advisory input on plans, specifications, and costs. It also notes that the Agency must concur in contractor selection, the construction contract, change orders, and contractor payments, while the borrower’s project architect remains responsible for construction oversight on behalf of the borrower.

The document explains construction financing options: interim financing, where an outside lender funds construction until the Agency loan closes, and multiple advances, where the Agency disburses funds during construction if interim financing is not available on suitable terms.

Finally, it covers identity-of-interest relationships in construction, requiring disclosure to the Agency, favorable cost comparison with non-IOI projects, and contractor cost certification under established procedures.

nodes.title: 9.3 CONSTRUCTION FINANCING
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses construction oversight and financing procedures for Agency projects. It explains the project architect’s role in monitoring construction, including inspections, payment certifications, and change order approvals. It then outlines two construction financing methods: interim financing, where a lender funds construction and the Agency closes after completion, and multiple advances, where the Agency releases funds as work progresses if interim financing is not available on acceptable terms. Finally, it covers identity-of-interest relationships in construction, requiring disclosure to the Agency, proof that costs are competitive with comparable projects, and contractor certification of actual costs.

nodes.title: 9.4 IDENTIFY- OF-INTEREST RELATIONSHIPS
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines key construction requirements for multi-family housing projects under HB-1-3560. It explains two construction financing methods: interim financing, which is preferred because the Agency finances a completed project, and multiple advances, where funds are released as construction progresses if interim financing is not available. It also covers identity-of-interest relationships, requiring disclosure, favorable cost comparison, and contractor cost certification. The document then describes construction standards, including compliance with Agency development and thermal standards, state and local codes, land and site development rules, and accessibility requirements, with architect certification required using Form RD 1924-25. Finally, it introduces construction methods, emphasizing competitive bid contracts as the default approach, with contracts awarded to the lowest responsible bidder and requiring Agency concurrence, proper licensing, and performance/payment bonds unless waived.

nodes.title: SECTION 1: CONSTRUCTION REQUIREMENTS
nodes.summary: The partial document only contains an instruction stating that the response should directly return a description of the main points covered in the partial document, without any additional text.

nodes.title: 9.5 CONSTRUCTION STANDARDS
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines construction requirements for multi-family housing projects. It explains that projects must meet prescribed construction standards, including agency development standards, thermal performance standards, and all applicable state and local codes. It also requires land and site development to comply with specific rural development instructions, and mandates architect certification that final plans satisfy construction and accessibility standards such as UFAS, FHA/AG, ADA/AG, and relevant state or local rules. The document then describes construction methods, identifying three options: competitive bid, negotiated bid, and owner-builder. It emphasizes that competitive bidding is the default method, requiring public notice, selection of the lowest responsible bidder, agency approval of the contractor and contract price, proper licensing, no debarment, and performance/payment bond protection unless waived.

nodes.title: 9.6 METHODS OF CONSTRUCTION
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines construction requirements for Multi-Family Housing projects. It explains that projects must follow prescribed construction standards, including Agency development standards, thermal performance standards, and all applicable state and local laws and codes. It also states that land and site development must meet specific requirements, and that the project architect must certify compliance with construction and accessibility standards before loan approval using the required form. The document further describes the three allowed construction methods—competitive bid, negotiated bid, and owner-builder—and focuses on competitive bidding, noting that contracts are generally awarded through public bidding to the lowest responsible bidder with Agency concurrence, proper contractor licensing, no debarment issues, and bonding or approved alternatives as required.

nodes.title: A. Competitive Bid
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines construction requirements for multi-family housing projects under HB-1-3560. It explains that projects must comply with Agency construction standards, thermal performance standards, state and local codes, land/site development rules, and accessibility requirements, with architect certification required before loan approval. It then describes the three permitted methods of construction: competitive bid contracts, negotiated contracts, and owner-builder arrangements. For competitive bidding, contracts must generally be awarded to the lowest responsible bidder, with Agency concurrence, proper licensing, and bonding requirements. Negotiated contracts may be allowed by exception when competitive bidding is impractical or unsuccessful, provided costs are reasonable and the contractor is qualified. Owner-builder construction is permitted when the borrower and construction company are the same entity, but it requires proof of qualifications, licensing, and Agency review. The section ends by introducing contractor requirements and noting that the Agency must verify contractor compliance.

nodes.title: B. Negotiated Contract
nodes.summary: The partial document explains construction contracting options and requirements for borrower projects. It covers when borrowers may use a negotiated contract instead of competitive bidding, including the need for an exception, acceptable cost comparison, contractor reliability and licensing, and written documentation. It also describes the owner-builder method, where the borrower and construction company are the same entity, noting qualification and licensing requirements, the absence of a main construction contract, and the need for review by agency staff. Finally, it introduces contractor requirements and notes that the choice between competitive bids and negotiated contracts can depend on construction market conditions.

nodes.title: C. Owner-Builder [RD Instruction 1924.13(e)(2)]
nodes.summary: The partial document explains construction contracting options and requirements for rural development projects. It covers when borrowers may use a negotiated contract instead of competitive bidding, including the need for an exception and conditions such as fair pricing, contractor reliability and licensing, and documented justification. It also describes the owner-builder method, where the borrower and construction company are the same entity, noting that owner-builders must be qualified and licensed, that no main construction contract exists, and that separate subcontract agreements may still be used. The section further states that the agency must review owner-builder requests through the Loan Originator and State Architect/Engineer, and it introduces contractor requirements, including how market conditions can affect whether competitive bidding or negotiated bids are more cost-effective.

nodes.title: 9.7 CONTRACTOR REQUIREMENTS
nodes.summary: The partial document explains contracting and contractor requirements for construction projects under HB-1-3560. It covers when borrowers may use a negotiated contract instead of competitive bidding, including required approval conditions such as fair pricing, contractor reliability/licensing, and documented justification. It also describes the owner-builder arrangement, where the borrower and construction entity are the same, and outlines the review and certification requirements for such cases. The document then details contractor requirements, including debarment and suspension certification, insurance requirements, payment and performance bonds, and the option to use an irrevocable letter of credit as an alternative to bonding.

nodes.title: A. Debarment and Suspensions
nodes.summary: The document section outlines contractor and construction financial safeguards for a project. It covers: requirements for contractors to certify they are not debarred or suspended from federal programs for bids over $25,000; the need for property and liability insurance before a construction contract becomes valid; mandatory payment and performance bonds equal to 100% of the contract value, including conditions for approved sureties and possible waivers when interim financing is involved; and the use of an unconditional, irrevocable letter of credit as an alternative to bonds, including its required amount, duration, and purpose in covering contractor default and completion costs.

nodes.title: B. Insurance
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines construction contracting safeguards and financial protections. It explains debarment and suspension requirements for contractors bidding over $25,000, including the need for certification and agency verification. It then covers insurance requirements, stating that contractors must provide property and liability coverage before a construction contract becomes valid. The document also describes payment and performance bond requirements, including 100% bond coverage, approved surety companies, and possible waiver by an interim lender. Finally, it discusses letters of credit as an alternative to bonds, specifying that they must be unconditional, irrevocable, equal to the contract amount, and remain effective until final acceptance of the work.

nodes.title: C. Payment and Performance Bonds
nodes.summary: The partial document explains contractor requirements for federally funded construction projects.
