The partial document outlines additional design and service requirements for congregate housing projects. It emphasizes that housing should be located near services and shopping, with consideration for emergency access, and that facilities supporting tenant services must be properly justified and designed to accepted standards. The document also covers accessibility and safety requirements, including accessible routes to living units, compliant walkways and corridors, emergency lighting, separation of tenant and service areas, and residential-style interiors to help prevent disorientation. It specifies minimum living unit features such as comparable room sizes, required kitchen appliances, and emergency call systems in bathrooms and bedrooms.  

The second major section focuses on tenant support services, stating that these services are intended to help residents live independently and must remain affordable. Applicants must submit a detailed services plan describing what services will be offered, how often, who will provide them, and backup providers if needed. The document also lists minimum required services, beginning with meal service: at least one cooked meal per day, seven days a week, supported by professional nutrition planning and tenant participation to maintain feasibility.

nodes.title: A. Services Plan
nodes.summary:
The partial document outlines the requirements for a congregate housing services plan in a loan application. It explains that the applicant must submit a detailed services plan covering what services will be provided, how often, who will provide them, backup providers, tenant notification procedures, and staffing needs. It also states that the loan originator must verify these details and obtain commitment letters from service providers, including terms, scope, costs, and licensing requirements. 

The document then describes minimum services that must be offered, focusing first on meal service. It requires at least one cooked meal per day, seven days a week, with meals planned by a qualified dietitian or nutritionist. It also notes that the long-term viability of meal service may depend on tenant participation, so borrowers should encourage tenant use if service fees are needed to support the program.

nodes.title: B. Minimum Services
nodes.summary:
The document section explains the requirements for a congregate housing services plan. It says loan applicants must submit a services plan with their initial application, and the Loan Originator must verify that it includes the types of services, how often they will be provided, who will provide them, backup service providers, how tenants will be informed, and the staffing needed. It also requires letters of commitment from service providers, including details such as service type, scope, cost, term, and any licensing requirements. 

The section then outlines minimum required services, focusing first on meals. At least one cooked meal per day, seven days a week, must be available through the primary provider or an alternate source. Meal planning must involve a professionally trained dietitian or nutritionist, and borrowers should actively encourage tenant participation when the service’s financial viability depends on user fees.

nodes.title: 1. Meals
nodes.summary:
The partial document describes requirements for a services plan that loan applicants must submit with their initial application for congregate housing projects. It explains that the Loan Originator must verify the plan includes the types and frequency of services, service providers and their commitment letters, backup providers, tenant notification procedures, and staffing needs.

The document then outlines minimum required services that must be available, including:
- Meals: at least one cooked meal per day, seven days a week, with dietitian involvement in menu planning and food stamp authorization where applicable.
- Transportation: fixed-schedule transportation based on tenant needs, using public/private sources or a project-sponsored system if necessary.
- Housekeeping: cleaning assistance for tenants upon request, including light and periodic heavier tasks.
- Personal services: limited nonmedical services such as beautician services, nutrition counseling, and health screening.
- Recreational/social activities: encouraged offerings like classes, dinners, library/book exchange, games, videos, and exercise programs.

It also states that access to medical services must be available within reasonable proximity and that transportation must be provided for tenants who need it.

nodes.title: 2. Transportation
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines the service requirements for a housing project, focusing on the supportive amenities that must be available to tenants. It covers meals, noting that if food stamps are accepted, the operator must be authorized by FNS, USDA to accept them. It then describes transportation requirements, emphasizing fixed-schedule service based on tenant needs and allowing use of public/private sources or a project-sponsored system if needed. The document also explains housekeeping services for tenants who request help, including routine light cleaning and periodic heavier tasks. It describes limited nonmedical personal services such as beautician services, nutrition counseling, and health screening, while excluding recurring medical care. Finally, it encourages recreational and social activities and summarizes the required services, including meals, transportation, housekeeping, personal services, recreational activities, and access to medical services within reasonable proximity.

nodes.title: 3. Housekeeping
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines the service requirements for a housing project, focusing on supportive amenities for tenants. It explains that if meal service is provided by an entity eligible to accept food stamps, the entity must be authorized by FNS to do so. It then details required transportation services on a fixed schedule based on tenant needs, including use of public, private, or project-sponsored transportation if necessary. The document also describes housekeeping services for tenants requesting help, with weekly light housekeeping and periodic heavier cleaning. It covers limited nonmedical personal services such as beautician services, nutrition counseling, and health screening, while excluding recurring medical care. In addition, it encourages recreational and social activities like classes, dinners, library or game areas, and exercise programs. Finally, it summarizes required services, including one cooked meal daily, transportation, housekeeping, personal services, recreational activities, and access to medical services within reasonable proximity with transportation provided when needed.

nodes.summary: The document outlines requirements for congregate housing loan processing and application preparation. nodes.title: 11.11 PRELIMINARY MEETING
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines the loan origination and underwriting process for congregate housing projects. It explains what information must be submitted to the National Office for review, what the applicant must receive in the initial and final application packages, and what should be covered at the preliminary meeting. It also details underwriting considerations specific to congregate housing, including evaluating market demand for support services and meal plans, ensuring tenant services are necessary, affordable, and sustainable, and rejecting projects if service costs make them infeasible.

nodes.title: 11.12 UNDERWRITING CONSIDERATIONS
nodes.summary: The excerpt covers underwriting considerations for congregate housing loans. It explains that the Loan Originator must use standard underwriting procedures while also evaluating the special aspects of congregate housing, including market demand, tenant support services, and project management quality. It emphasizes that market feasibility documentation must show a real need for the services offered, especially meal plans, and that these services must be in demand in the target market. The text also states that tenant services are central to congregate housing and must be needed, affordable, and sustainable; if service costs are too high or not comparable to the market, they must be reduced or the project rejected as infeasible.

nodes.title: A. Market Feasibility Documentation
nodes.summary: This section covers underwriting considerations for congregate housing loans. It explains that the Loan Originator must use standard underwriting procedures and also evaluate the special factors unique to congregate housing, including market demand, the availability and adequacy of tenant support services, and project management quality. It emphasizes that market feasibility documentation must show demand for the specific services offered, especially meal plans, since poorly planned meal subscriptions can hurt financial viability. It also states that tenant services are a central feature of congregate housing and must be necessary, affordable, and sustainable. If service costs are too high or not competitive, the applicant should explore alternatives such as community services or volunteers; if costs still cannot be reduced, the project should be rejected as infeasible.

nodes.title: B. Tenant Services
nodes.summary: The document section focuses on underwriting considerations for congregate housing loans. It explains that, in addition to standard underwriting procedures, the Loan Originator must evaluate the market for this type of housing, the adequacy and availability of tenant support services, and the quality of project management. It emphasizes that market feasibility documentation must show there is demand for the services offered, especially meal plans, and that meal plan subscriptions must be carefully planned to support project financial viability. It also states that tenant services are a critical part of congregate housing and must be necessary, affordable, and sustainable. If service costs are too high or not comparable to the market, they must be reduced or replaced with alternatives such as community services or volunteers; otherwise, the project must be rejected as infeasible.

nodes.title: C. Management
nodes.summary: This section discusses management and design requirements for congregate or special senior housing projects. It explains that the Loan Originator must verify the proposed management company has relevant experience, and if not, require additional expertise such as a consultant. It also outlines design considerations, including reviewing project plans to ensure they meet required criteria, are convenient and suitable for tenants, support efficient operations, and are reviewed by the State Architect for program compliance.

nodes.title: 11.13 DESIGN CONSIDERATIONS
nodes.summary:
The document covers loan originator responsibilities and eligibility requirements for special housing projects, focusing on management and design considerations for congregate housing, and then introduces Section 2 on group homes. It explains that the loan originator must verify that a proposed management company has experience with special senior housing or require additional management expertise if needed. It also requires review of project plans to ensure design criteria are met, emphasizing tenant-friendly layout, operational efficiency, and review by the State Architect.  

The section on group homes defines them as shared living arrangements for individuals who can live independently in basic daily activities but need assistance or direction from a trained resident assistant. It notes that group homes may serve elderly or disabled individuals, are not healthcare facilities, and may have restricted occupancy. The document outlines project eligibility rules, including tenancy restrictions, tenant qualification review, the role of legal guardians in signing leases, compliance with all applicable laws, and the requirement that a group home remain a separate, independent entity even if associated with another organization. Finally, it states that applicants must demonstrate the availability of adequate support services on a long-term basis, which may be provided by the project, government agencies, or qualified nonprofit organizations.

nodes.title: SECTION 2: GROUP HOMES [7 CFR 3560.69]
nodes.summary: This section explains the USDA/Rural Housing rules for group homes under 7 CFR 3560.69. It defines group homes as shared rental housing for people who can manage basic daily living but need direction or assistance from a trained resident assistant, and notes they are not healthcare facilities. It outlines how group homes may be limited to certain tenant groups such as elderly, developmentally disabled, or mentally impaired individuals, and describes tenant eligibility, including evaluation of prospective tenants and the use of a legal guardian to sign a lease when needed. It also states that group homes must comply with all applicable federal, state, and local laws and may be affiliated with another organization only if they remain a separate, independent entity. Finally, it covers the requirement that applicants demonstrate the availability of adequate support services on a continuous, long-term basis, whether provided by the project, government agencies, or qualified nonprofit organizations.

nodes.title: 11.14 OVERVIEW
nodes.summary:
The partial document explains USDA housing guidance for group homes under HB-1-3560, Section 2. It gives an overview of what group homes are: shared rental housing for individuals who can manage basic daily living but need some assistance or direction from a trained resident assistant. It notes that group homes may serve elderly or disabled persons, may restrict occupancy to certain tenant groups, and are not meant to operate as healthcare facilities, though limited medical-related help such as medication assistance may be provided.

The section then describes project eligibility requirements. It explains that group homes may limit occupancy to specific categories of tenants, such as elderly, developmentally disabled, or mentally impaired persons. Prospective tenants must be screened to confirm they meet eligibility requirements, and legal guardians may sign leases for tenants who lack legal capacity. It also states that group homes must comply with all applicable federal, state, and local laws and may be affiliated with another organization only if they remain a separate and independent entity.

Finally, the document covers support services. Applicants must demonstrate that necessary support services will be available on a continuous, long-term basis. These services may be provided by the project itself, by government agencies, or by qualified nonprofit organizations with ongoing service programs.

nodes.title: 11.15 PROJECT ELIGIBILITY

The section then outlines key loan origination requirements specific to group homes, including project eligibility, tenancy restrictions for certain tenant groups, tenant screening and legal guardian lease authority, compliance with all applicable laws, and the need for the group home to operate as a separate entity even if associated with another organization. It also states that group homes must comply with all applicable Federal, State, and local laws and may be linked to another organization only if they remain a separate, independent entity.

Finally, it discusses support services, requiring loan applicants to demonstrate that needed services will be available on a continuing long-term basis, whether provided by the project itself, a public agency, or an established nonprofit organization.

nodes.title: 11.16 SUPPORT SERVICES
nodes.summary:
The partial document explains USDA Rural Development rules for **group homes** under multifamily housing loans. It covers:

- **What group homes are**: shared living arrangements for people who can live independently in basic daily tasks but need support from a trained resident assistant; these homes may serve elderly or disabled individuals and are not healthcare facilities.
- **Project eligibility**: who may live in a group home, including the ability to restrict occupancy to certain populations; how tenants are screened for eligibility; when a legal guardian may sign a lease; compliance with federal, state, and local laws; and the requirement that a group home remain a separate entity even if affiliated with another organization.
- **Support services**: applicants must demonstrate that needed services will be available long term and provide a service plan describing access to meals, transportation, recreation/social activities, and medical services, along with documentation from agencies expected to provide support.
- **Design requirements**: group homes should generally look like residential dwellings, include common areas for shared living activities, consider residents’ changing physical and mental needs, provide emergency lighting and accessible entrances, and comply with accessibility and housing standards such as UFAS, FHA/AG, ADA/AG, and local codes.
- **Rents and rental assistance**: rent determination for group homes is handled according to specific instructions in an attachment.

nodes.title: 11.17 DESIGN REQUIREMENTS
nodes.summary: The partial document covers USDA Rural Development requirements for group home projects. It explains that loan applicants must submit a service plan showing how residents will receive long-term supportive services, including kitchen access, transportation, recreational and social activities, and medical services, along with a government agency statement supporting the service needs. It outlines design requirements for group homes, emphasizing residential character, common areas, accessibility, emergency lighting, and compliance with UFAS, FHA/AG, ADA/AG, and local or State codes. It also describes rent and rental assistance rules for tenants in shared units, including limits tied to comparable local rents. Finally, it summarizes application, processing, and underwriting requirements, stating that group home loans follow standard multifamily housing procedures plus additional service documentation, and that the lender must verify market demand, independence from any affiliated organization, and completeness of all required materials.

nodes.title: 11.18 RENTS AND RENTAL ASSISTANCE
nodes.summary: This excerpt focuses on rules and procedures for financing and operating group homes under HB-1-3560. It explains how a “unit” is defined in a group home and how rental assistance can be applied when tenants share a unit or bedroom, including limits tied to comparable local rents. It then outlines application requirements for group home loans, stating that applicants must provide the standard Multi-Family Housing documentation, a service plan, and a supporting narrative from a government agency expected to assist with tenant services. The document also describes the loan processing workflow, including review by the Loan Originator, possible rejection of ineligible or unfeasible applications, National Office review for applications moving to the final stage, and required concurrence when loan amounts exceed delegated authority. Finally, it covers underwriting standards, emphasizing that group home loans must meet general lending standards and that the Loan Originator must verify complete documentation, independent operability if affiliated with another organization, and the existence of a market for the services offered.

nodes.title: 11.19 APPLICATION REQUIREMENTS AND PROCESSING
nodes.summary: The partial document explains how rental assistance applies to shared units in group homes, including limits for shared bedrooms so rents stay comparable to the local market. It then covers the application requirements and processing steps for group home loans, stating that applicants must submit standard Multi-Family Housing documentation plus a service plan and a government support narrative, and that applications move through initial and final review stages with possible National Office concurrence depending on eligibility and loan amount. Finally, it outlines underwriting considerations, emphasizing that group home loans are reviewed under standard loan rules, with special attention to whether the applicant has a market for the services and tenants, whether all required information has been submitted, and whether an affiliated group home can operate independently.

nodes.title: 11.20 UNDERWRITING CONSIDERATIONS
nodes.summary: This section explains how rental assistance works for tenants in group homes, including shared-unit and shared-bedroom situations and the rule that total rent must remain comparable to local market rents. It then outlines application requirements for group home loans, including required Multi-Family Housing documentation, a service plan, and a supporting narrative from a government agency. The document also describes the loan processing workflow through initial and final application stages, including National Office review and concurrence when needed. Finally, it covers underwriting and market feasibility requirements, emphasizing review of tenant/service demand, independence of affiliated group homes, management experience, design standards, and detailed market feasibility documentation such as service demand, expanded market areas, demographic and income data, alternative service providers, and meal service demand.

nodes.title: A. Market Feasibility Documentation
nodes.summary: The partial document contains only an instruction requesting a direct description of the main points covered, without providing any substantive content to summarize.

nodes.title: SECTION 3: MARKET FEASIBILITY CONSIDERATIONS FOR CONGREGATE HOUSING AND GROUP HOMES
nodes.summary: The document section explains additional market feasibility documentation requirements for congregate housing and group homes. It states that market feasibility documentation must specifically demonstrate the need for housing with services and for the particular services proposed, using local aging agencies or surveys of eligible elderly people if needed. It also covers when an expanded market area may be considered and requires proof that the larger area can provide enough tenants, with separate data for any added communities. In addition, it requires income information from the Social Security Office, demographic data for older age groups, discussion of alternative service providers, and evidence of demand for meal services, since meal service is important to the project’s budget and feasibility.

nodes.title: 11.21 ADDITIONAL MARKET FEASIBILITY DOCUMENTATION REQUIREMENTS
nodes.summary:
The partial document covers two main topics from USDA Rural Development’s HB-1-3560:

1. **Additional market feasibility documentation for congregate housing and group homes**
   - Explains what the market feasibility documentation must show for projects serving elderly or special tenant groups.
   - Requires evidence of demand for both the housing and the services offered, such as meal service and other supportive services.
   - Notes that local aging agencies and similar organizations can help assess need, and surveys may be used if other data are unavailable.
   - Describes when an expanded market area may be considered and what the documentation must prove if one is used.
   - Requires income and demographic data for older age groups, discussion of alternative service providers, and analysis of demand for meal services.

2. **Manufactured housing under the Multi-Family Housing program**
   - Gives an overview of manufactured housing loans, including that they are underwritten at the State level and require final National Office approval.
   - Defines manufactured housing and explains that additional design and warranty requirements apply because the units are built offsite.
   - Lists eligibility rules for applicants and projects, including that the applicant must be the first owner and that units must be new, unoccupied, and less than one year old.
   - States that projects must include at least two units and generally cannot be financed for refinancing, except in limited construction-loan situations.
   - Allows rehabilitation only for existing Agency-financed manufactured housing projects and only by experienced rehabilitators.

nodes.title: SECTION 4: MANUFACTURED HOUSING [7 CFR 3560.70]
nodes.summary:
The partial document explains the rules for Multi-Family Housing loans involving manufactured housing under HB-1-3560. It defines manufactured housing and notes that these loans are underwritten at the State level but require final approval from the National Office. The section highlights that, while most processing and underwriting rules are similar to other multi-family projects, manufactured housing has extra design and warranty requirements because it is built offsite.

It also covers eligibility requirements for both applicants and projects. The applicant must be the first owner buying the manufactured homes for a purpose other than resale. The project must meet construction, design, and warranty standards, and each unit must be new, unused, less than one year old from manufacture, part of a project with two or more units, and not financed for refinancing except when replacing a construction loan. Rehabilitation is allowed only for existing Agency-financed manufactured home projects and must be done by experienced professionals to avoid damaging structural integrity.

nodes.title: 11.22 OVERVIEW
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines USDA Multi-Family Housing rules for manufactured housing projects. It explains what manufactured housing is, notes that these loans compete with other multi-family housing projects and require state underwriting with final national approval, and emphasizes that while general processing is similar to other multi-family loans, extra design and warranty requirements apply because the homes are built offsite. It also details eligibility rules for applicants and projects, including that the applicant must be the first owner buying the homes for purposes other than resale, the units must be new and less than one year old, the project must include at least two units, refinancing is generally not allowed except to replace a construction loan, and rehabilitation is only permitted for existing Agency-financed projects and must be done by experienced professionals.

nodes.title: 11.23 ELIGIBILITY REQUIREMENTS
nodes.summary: The document section explains the USDA Agency’s rules for financing multi-family housing projects that use manufactured housing units. It defines manufactured housing, notes that these loans compete with other multi-family housing projects and require state-level underwriting with final National Office approval, and emphasizes that extra design and warranty requirements apply because the homes are built offsite. It also outlines eligibility requirements for applicants and projects, including that the applicant must be the first owner buying the homes for use rather than resale, the units must be new and less than one year old, the project must include at least two units, refinancing is generally not allowed except to replace a construction loan, and rehabilitation is only permitted for Agency-financed projects and should be done by experienced individuals.

nodes.title: A. Applicant Eligibility
nodes.summary: The document section explains the rules for Multi-Family Housing loans involving manufactured housing. It defines manufactured housing, notes that these loans are processed and underwritten similarly to other multi-family projects but require extra design and warranty checks because the units are built offsite, and states that state offices underwrite the loans while final approval comes from the National Office. It also outlines eligibility requirements: the applicant must be the first owner buying the homes for non-resale purposes, and the project must meet specific conditions such as using new, unoccupied homes less than one year old, containing at least two units, not being for refinancing except to replace a construction loan, and only allowing rehabilitation for Agency-financed projects by experienced contractors.

nodes.title: B. Project Eligibility
nodes.summary:
This section explains the rules for USDA Rural Development financing of manufactured housing within Multi-Family Housing projects. It defines manufactured housing, notes that these loans are underwritten at the state level but require final National Office approval, and says most processing rules are similar to other multi-family projects, with extra design and warranty requirements because the homes are built offsite.

It then outlines eligibility requirements for both the applicant and the project. The applicant must be the first owner purchasing the homes for purposes other than resale. The project must consist of at least two manufactured home units, each new and unoccupied, less than one year old from manufacture, and generally not eligible for refinancing except as part of a construction loan payoff. Rehabilitation is allowed only for existing Agency-financed units and should be done by experienced rehabilitators. It also allows redevelopment projects where existing manufactured housing land is purchased for new units.

The section also details design standards. Manufactured homes must meet RD Instruction 1924-A requirements, including minimum size, foundation, anchoring, thermal performance, and HUD manufactured home construction and safety standards. The projects must be planned with a good residential environment, appropriate site layout, and recreational space for families.

Finally, it specifies special warranty requirements: the seller must provide the manufacturer’s warranty to the borrower, and it must identify each unit by serial number.

nodes.title: 11.24 DESIGN REQUIREMENTS
nodes.summary: The document section covers requirements for manufactured housing projects, including the possibility of redeveloping existing manufactured housing sites with new homes. It explains design standards for manufactured homes, such as minimum floor area and width, permanent foundations, anchoring, thermal performance, and compliance with HUD and Agency construction standards. It also emphasizes overall site planning, encouraging attractive, functional residential environments that fit the land and avoid monotonous design, with consideration for family recreational space and durability. Finally, it outlines special warranty requirements, stating that sellers must provide the manufacturer’s warranty to the borrower and identify the units by serial number.

nodes.title: 11.25 SPECIAL WARRANTY REQUIREMENTS
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines USDA Rural Development requirements for manufactured housing projects, including eligibility, design standards, warranties, loan terms, underwriting, and closing procedures. It explains that projects may involve purchasing existing manufactured housing property for redevelopment, and that manufactured homes must meet specific size, foundation, anchoring, thermal performance, and HUD safety standards. It also emphasizes sound site design, family-friendly recreational space, and compliance with Agency construction standards. The document further details warranty obligations from the seller and contractor, limits loan amortization to the lesser of economic life or 30 years, and requires underwriting to verify eligibility, design compliance, and warranties before approval. Finally, it notes that loan closing must include a mortgage or deed of trust on the entire property improved or purchased with the loan.

nodes.title: 11.26 RATES AND TERMS
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines requirements for manufactured housing loan projects, focusing on warranties, loan terms, underwriting, and closing procedures. It states that the general contractor or dealer-contractor must provide a warranty ensuring the manufactured home, foundation, anchoring, and related improvements conform to approved plans, that no hidden transportation damage occurred and double-wide sections were properly joined, and that the manufacturer’s warranty does not replace other borrower rights. It also explains that loan amortization and terms may not exceed the lesser of the security’s economic life or 30 years. For underwriting, loans must follow general chapters plus additional checks on project eligibility, home age, construction/design review with the State Architect, and verification of required warranties before final approval. Finally, it notes that loan closing must follow Chapter 8 and requires a mortgage or deed of trust on the entire property improved or purchased with the loan.

nodes.title: 11.27 LOAN PROCESSING AND UNDERWRITING
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines requirements for manufactured housing loans and projects. It explains warranty obligations for the contractor or dealer-contractor, including assurance that the manufactured home, foundation, anchoring, and improvements meet approved plans, that the home was not damaged during transport and was properly joined if double-wide, and that the manufacturer’s warranty supplements other borrower rights. It then covers loan rates and terms, stating that repayment periods cannot exceed the lesser of the security’s economic life or 30 years. The document also describes loan processing and underwriting requirements, including verifying project eligibility, confirming the home’s age, coordinating on construction/design compliance, and ensuring required warranties are in place before approval. Finally, it notes loan closing rules, including the need for a mortgage or deed of trust on the entire property.

nodes.title: 11.28 LOAN CLOSING
nodes.summary: The partial document explains key requirements for financing and closing manufactured housing projects, including warranty standards, loan term limits, underwriting steps, and closing conditions such as real estate liens, title insurance, and property taxation as real estate. It also begins Section 5 on rural cooperative housing, describing how consumer cooperatives operate, the concept of limited equity and patronage capital accounts, the joint State and National Office underwriting process, and basic eligibility and organizational requirements for cooperative members and boards.

nodes.title: SECTION 5: RURAL COOPERATIVE HOUSING
nodes.summary: The partial document only contains an instruction asking the reader to directly return a description of the partial document without adding any other text. No substantive content or main points are provided beyond that instruction.

nodes.title: 11.29 OVERVIEW
nodes.summary: This section explains rural cooperative housing under HB-1-3560, focusing on how it works, who is eligible, and how it is financed and approved. It describes cooperative housing as multifamily housing owned and managed by a consumer cooperative, where members pay actual costs and build limited equity through patronage capital accounts rather than ownership equity in their units. It also explains that cooperative projects are funded separately from other multifamily housing programs, with underwriting shared between the State Office and National Office, and final approval made by the National Office. The section further outlines the organizational structure of the cooperative, including the Board of Directors, and states that all members must meet eligibility requirements. Finally, it highlights the distinctive tenancy rules for cooperative housing, noting that once admitted, members cannot be removed from membership or tenancy solely because they later no longer meet the original income eligibility standards.

nodes.title: 11.30 ELIGIBILITY AND ORGANIZATIONAL REQUIREMENTS
nodes.summary:
The partial document explains the Rural Cooperative Housing section of HB-1-3560, focusing on how cooperative housing is structured, financed, and approved. It describes rural cooperative housing as multi-family housing owned and managed by a consumer cooperative operating at actual cost, with excess income credited to members’ patronage capital accounts under a limited-equity model. It also outlines the loan origination process, emphasizing that funding comes from the National Office and that underwriting is a joint State Office and National Office process.

The document then covers eligibility and organizational requirements, stating that the consumer cooperative is the loan applicant and must be organized under state or federally recognized tribal cooperative laws, operate for members’ benefit, restrict membership to eligible persons, and maintain a board of directors elected from the membership. It explains member rules, including income eligibility, residence requirements, membership fees, and the principle that eligible members cannot be removed solely because their income later changes. The board’s responsibilities, membership list maintenance, and management role are also noted.

Finally, the section addresses market feasibility and application requirements. Market feasibility is shown through a list of prospective members who have committed to join the cooperative, with extra names recommended to cover possible ineligibility or withdrawals. A professional market study is not required. The document closes by noting that applicants must submit additional items specific to cooperative housing beyond the standard multi-family housing application materials.

nodes.title: Exhibit 11 -2
nodes.summary: The partial document provides only an instruction stating that the response should directly return a description of the document’s main points, without adding any extra text.

nodes.title: Eligibility and Organizational Requirements
nodes.summary:
The partial document outlines eligibility and organizational requirements for consumer cooperative loan applicants, their members, and the board of directors. It explains that the cooperative must be legally organized under state or federally recognized Indian tribe cooperative laws, operate housing on a cooperative, at-cost basis for the benefit of members, and restrict membership to eligible persons. It also specifies requirements for board size, board composition from cooperative members, maintaining membership records, and the board’s role in managing the cooperative and setting rules.

The document also covers member qualifications, stating that members must live in the cooperative housing, meet income eligibility rules, pay a membership fee, and commit to the cooperative concept. It notes that once admitted, a member cannot later lose membership solely because their income changes.

In addition, the document addresses market feasibility for cooperative housing projects, stating that feasibility is demonstrated by prospective members who have clearly expressed intent to join, and that more names than units may be needed to account for ineligible applicants. Finally, it introduces application requirements, indicating that cooperative housing loan applicants must submit additional items beyond those required for other multifamily housing projects.

nodes.title: 11.31 MARKET FEASIBILITY
nodes.summary: The document section outlines eligibility and organizational requirements for consumer cooperative housing loan applicants, cooperative members, and boards of directors. It explains that the cooperative must be legally organized under state or tribal cooperative law, operate housing on a nonprofit-at-cost basis for members, restrict membership to eligible persons, and have at least five directors. It also describes member requirements, such as residing in the cooperative, meeting income limits, paying a membership fee, and signing documents supporting the cooperative concept. For the board, it notes that directors are drawn from the membership, are initially appointed and later elected, maintain membership records, and manage the cooperative’s rules and operations. The section also covers market feasibility, stating that proof is shown through names and addresses of prospective members who intend to join, and that more names than units may be needed to ensure feasibility. Finally, it begins a section on application requirements for cooperative housing projects, indicating that additional submission items are required beyond standard multifamily housing applications.

nodes.title: 11.32 APPLICATION REQUIREMENTS
nodes.summary:
The partial document outlines eligibility, organizational, application, market feasibility, and management requirements for consumer cooperative housing loan applicants. It explains who may apply, including that the cooperative must be organized under state or tribal cooperative law, operate at cost for members’ benefit, restrict membership to eligible persons, and have a board of at least five directors drawn from the membership. It also describes member requirements such as residing in the cooperative, meeting income limits, paying a membership fee, and committing to the cooperative concept.

The document then addresses market feasibility, stating that feasibility is shown by prospective members who have expressed definite intent to join, and that a professional market study is not required. It lists initial and final application materials, including names of interested members, financial statements, certification of interest, budgets with management reserves, an affirmative fair housing marketing plan, a management statement, a limited equity agreement, and a subscription agreement. Finally, it notes that cooperative boards have ultimate responsibility for management and that loan underwriting must account for the special management structure of cooperatives.

nodes.title: 11.33 MANAGEMENT REQUIREMENTS
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines application and management requirements for cooperative housing under HB-1-3560. It lists the documents and certifications needed at the initial application stage, including member interest details, management reserve budgeting, prospective member certification, and financial statements to verify ability to pay occupancy costs. It also describes requirements for the final application stage, such as an Affirmative Fair Housing Marketing Plan, a contingency plan for professional management if self-management is not feasible, a limited equity agreement, and a subscription agreement. The section then introduces management requirements, emphasizing that loan originators must account for the special management needs of cooperative housing and that the cooperative’s Board of Directors holds ultimate responsibility for managing the housing.

nodes.title: A. Cooperative Board of Directors
nodes.summary:
This document section explains the governance and management structure of a cooperative housing project. It covers the role of the Cooperative Board of Directors, including its responsibility to set and enforce policies, manage member occupancy rates, and handle serious violations. It also notes that the Board must provide prospective members with information about the cooperative, its financial obligations, and the meaning of limited equity.

The section further describes the adviser to the Board, who assists during formation and until the Board can manage effectively on its own. It outlines requirements for selecting the adviser, the need for Agency approval, possible compensation, and the adviser’s continued monitoring role.

Finally, it discusses the use of committees within the cooperative, emphasizing that responsibilities should be assigned to committees and shared with prospective members to support self-management of the cooperative.

nodes.title: B. Adviser to the Board
nodes.summary:
The document explains the governance structure of a cooperative housing project. It describes the role of the Cooperative Board of Directors, which is made up of cooperative members and is responsible for setting and enforcing policies, including occupancy rate changes and possible termination of ownership rights for serious rule violations. It also notes that the Board must provide prospective members with information about the cooperative, its financial obligations, and the meaning of limited equity.

It then outlines the role of an adviser to the Board during the cooperative’s formation and early operation. The adviser, who may be an individual or an organization, must be carefully selected, approved by the Agency, and remain the sole point of contact to avoid confusion. The adviser provides guidance, monitors the cooperative for at least two years after it becomes self-managed, and may be compensated in a way that does not overly reduce members’ patronage capital.

Finally, the document discusses committees within the cooperative. It states that the cooperative should define which responsibilities will be delegated to committees, identify the committees, and explain their functions. This information must be shared with prospective members, emphasizing that the cooperative’s success depends on members’ ability to manage it effectively with the adviser’s assistance.

nodes.title: C. Committees
nodes.summary:
This section explains how a cooperative housing project should be governed and managed. It describes the role of the Cooperative Board of Directors in setting and enforcing policies, handling issues like fee increases or rule violations, and providing required information to prospective members about the cooperative and limited equity ownership.

It then outlines the role of an outside adviser to the Board during the formation period and until the Board is able to manage effectively on its own. The adviser must be carefully selected, approved by the Agency, and may receive compensation shared by members.

The document also discusses the use of committees in cooperative operations. It emphasizes member participation, the need for board training, examples of committees that may be formed, and the expectation that members contribute to self-management. If self-management fails, professional management must be hired.

Finally, it introduces acceptable management options for cooperatives: self-management, partial self-management, and contract management, and notes that education and training are important in the early stages of operation.

nodes.title: D. Acceptable Forms of Management
nodes.summary:
The passage explains that a cooperative’s board must be trained to understand how the cooperative operates before taking on management responsibilities. It emphasizes board education programs, voluntary committee participation by members, and the need for members with relevant experience to serve on appropriate committees. It lists possible committees such as maintenance, groundskeeping, communications, budget and finance, rules, recreation, and home service, and notes that if self-management fails, the cooperative must hire professional management.

It then introduces acceptable management options for cooperative housing: self-management, partial self-management, and contract management. The section on self-management states that education and training should be ongoing, that reasonable education costs can be included in the budget, and that training should be relevant to cooperatives and led by qualified professionals. It also notes that outside help, such as bookkeeping services, may be needed at first, making partial self-management an initial step.

nodes.title: 1. Self-Management
nodes.summary:
The passage explains how a housing cooperative should prepare for and manage self-governance. It stresses that prospective board members need training and education before taking over responsibility, and that committee participation is generally voluntary but may require additional member involvement if staffing is insufficient. It lists possible committees such as maintenance, groundskeeping, communications, budget and finance, rules, recreation, and home service.

It then outlines three acceptable management structures for cooperatives: self-management, partial self-management, and contract management. Self-management is the preferred goal, supported by ongoing education and possibly limited outside help during the early stages. Partial self-management allows some services to be contracted out if needed. If those measures are not enough, contract management permits professional agents to handle day-to-day operations under board policies.

Finally, the section describes how management reserves and patronage accounts work. Members pay a monthly management assessment included in occupancy charges, which is held in a reserve account and may later be credited back to members if the cooperative manages itself successfully. If professional management becomes necessary, those funds are used to pay for it and patronage credits are suspended. It also notes that rental assistance cannot be used for the member’s management reserve contribution, though full-time professional management may qualify as a shelter cost.

nodes.title: 2. Partial Self-Management
nodes.summary:
The partial document explains management options and financial treatment for a cooperative housing project. It covers:

- **Adviser and management roles during the cooperative’s formative years**, including guidance on evaluating adviser qualifications.
- **Partial self-management**, where some supervisory or technical services can be outsourced if the cooperative cannot fully manage itself.
- **Contract management**, where professional managers handle day-to-day operations, while the Board sets policy; the Agency helps decide when to switch from self-management to professional management, and has final authority if there is disagreement.
- **Management reserves and patronage accounts**, including how monthly management charges are collected, held in reserve, and later transferred to members’ patronage accounts if the cooperative manages itself successfully.
- **Treatment when professional management is needed**, including using reserve funds to pay for it, suspending patronage accruals if necessary, and raising occupancy charges if reserve funds are insufficient.
- **Rules for rental assistance**, stating that Agency rental assistance cannot be used for members’ management reserve contributions, though full-time professional management may become part of eligible shelter costs.

nodes.title: 3. Contract Management
nodes.summary:
The partial document discusses cooperative housing management options and how management costs are handled financially. It explains three management structures: self-management with adviser support, partial self-management using contracted technical or supervisory assistance, and full contract management through a professional agent. It also covers the role of the Agency in deciding when professional management is needed if the adviser and Agency cannot agree.

The document then outlines management reserves and patronage accounts. It describes how monthly management charges are collected through occupancy rates, held in a reserve account, and later transferred to members’ patronage accounts if the cooperative successfully manages itself. It also explains that if professional management becomes necessary due to poor self-management, the reserve funds are used to pay for it and patronage accumulation is suspended. Finally, it notes that agency rental assistance cannot be used to fund members’ management reserve contributions, though full-time professional management fees may qualify as shelter costs eligible for assistance.

nodes.title: E. Management Reserves and Patronage Accounts
nodes.summary:
The partial document explains cooperative management options and how management costs are handled financially. It covers:

- **Adviser and management roles during cooperative formation**, including guidance on selecting a qualified adviser.
- **Partial self-management**, where some supervisory or technical services may be contracted if the cooperative cannot fully manage itself.
- **Contract management**, where professional managers handle daily operations, with the Board setting policy and the Agency having final approval if there is disagreement about hiring management.
- **Management reserves and patronage accounts**, describing how monthly management fees are collected as part of occupancy charges, held in reserve, and later transferred to members as patronage capital if the cooperative successfully self-manages.
- **Use of reserve funds for professional management**, including what happens if the cooperative needs contract management due to poor self-management and how fees may be covered through increased occupancy charges if necessary.
- **Rules on rental assistance**, stating that Agency rental assistance cannot be used for member contributions to the management reserve, though full-time professional management fees may become eligible as shelter costs.

nodes.title: 1. Paying for Management
nodes.summary: The partial document explains cooperative housing management and membership procedures. It covers three levels of management: self-management, partial self-management using outside technical help, and contract management when professional oversight is needed. It describes how management reserves are collected through monthly occupancy charges, how unused reserves may be transferred to patronage capital, and how those funds are tracked and distributed to members. It also outlines patronage capital accounting rules, including treatment when a member leaves and the handling of inflation equity. The document requires all members to sign an occupancy agreement and sets rules for a cooperative membership fee equal to one month’s occupancy charge, with payment plans allowed under certain limits. Finally, it describes the loan processing procedure for cooperative housing applications, including solicitation through a NOFA, review of eligibility and feasibility, funding availability checks, and submission to the National Office for approval.

nodes.title: 2. Patronage Capital Accounts
nodes.summary: The partial document covers rules and procedures for cooperative housing operations and financing. It explains how patronage capital accounts and management reserve income are assigned, how records must track member contributions, and how inflation equity on cooperative property is treated. It then describes the occupancy agreement required for all cooperative members, including its purpose and key terms. The document also outlines the cooperative membership fee, which is set by the Board of Directors at one month’s occupancy charge and may be paid over time under specific conditions. Finally, it details the loan processing procedure for cooperative housing applications, including funding availability checks, application review, eligibility and feasibility screening, and referral to the National Office for authorization.

nodes.title: F. Occupancy Agreement
nodes.summary: The document section explains several key rules for cooperative housing. It covers how patronage capital accounts work, including assigning remaining income and management reserves as patronage capital, keeping records for each member’s share, and handling inflation equity. It then describes the required occupancy agreement that all cooperative members must sign, outlining member rights, obligations, and monthly charges. It also sets standards for cooperative membership fees, requiring the fee to equal one month’s occupancy charge, be applied uniformly, and allow installment payments with a minimum down payment. Finally, it outlines the loan processing procedure for cooperative housing applications, including submission through a NOFA, review for funding availability, eligibility and feasibility screening, and forwarding approved proposals to the National Office.

nodes.title: 11.34 COOPERATIVE MEMBERSHIP FEE
nodes.summary:
The partial document describes key rules and procedures for cooperative housing. It explains how patronage capital accounts are handled, including assigning year-end income and unused management reserves as patronage capital, keeping records of member contributions, and following IRS patronage distribution rules. It also notes that inflation equity on cooperative property is not part of members’ limited equity when they leave.

The document then covers occupancy agreements, requiring all cooperative members to sign a contract that defines their rights, responsibilities, monthly occupancy charge, and other occupancy terms.

It further outlines the cooperative membership fee, which must equal one month’s occupancy charge, be set uniformly by the Board of Directors, and may be paid in installments without interest, with at least a $25 down payment and a maximum payment period of 12 months.

Finally, it explains the loan processing process for cooperative housing, including solicitation through a NOFA, ordering applications by receipt of complete project proposals, checking funding availability, inviting initial applications when appropriate, and reviewing applications for eligibility and feasibility before forwarding qualifying proposals to the National Office.

nodes.title: 11.35 LOAN PROCESSING
nodes.summary: The partial document covers key procedures and requirements for cooperative housing projects. It explains how patronage capital accounts and management reserves are handled, including recordkeeping, IRS-compliant distribution, and treatment of inflation equity. It outlines the occupancy agreement that all cooperative members must sign, defining member rights, obligations, monthly charges, and occupancy terms. It also describes the cooperative membership fee rules, including the fee amount, payment options, minimum down payment, and repayment period. Finally, it summarizes the loan processing, closing, and post-closing procedures for cooperative housing loans, including application review, funding availability checks, eligibility and feasibility determination, National Office authorization, and use of standard procedures for closing, construction monitoring, and initial occupancy.

nodes.title: 11.36 LOAN CLOSING AND POST -CLOSING ACTIVITIES
nodes.summary: This excerpt explains the final steps in the loan application and approval process. It says that, if the National Office authorizes it, the Leadership Designee informs the applicant that the application appears eligible and feasible, after which the Loan Originator invites the applicant to submit a final application. It then notes that final applications are processed and approved using the procedures in Chapter 5. The section also introduces loan closing and post-closing activities, stating that Loan Originators must follow Chapters 8 and 9 for loan closing, construction monitoring, and initial project occupancy oversight.

nodes.title: Attachment 11 -A
nodes.summary: The partial document is an attachment from HB-1-3560 that sets out additional management requirements for congregate housing and group homes. It explains the purpose and objective of these housing types: to provide shelter and separate, affordable supportive services based on identified market need, while allowing residents to meet any unmet medical, discretionary, or service needs through their own resources. The document also defines key terms such as congregate housing, group home, service agreement, service contract, service plan, and tenant base. Finally, it notes that rent subsidy funds cannot be used to pay for services in congregate housing or group homes.

nodes.title: MANAGEMENT OF CONGREGATE HOUSING AND GROUP HOMES
nodes.summary: The document is an attachment to HB-1-3560 that sets out additional management requirements for congregate housing and group homes under 7 CFR Part 3560. It explains the purpose and objective of these housing types: to provide shelter and separately arranged services that are affordable and based on demonstrated market need, while allowing tenants to meet any additional medical, discretionary, or support needs using their own resources. The excerpt defines key terms such as congregate housing, group home, service agreement, service contract, service plan, and tenant base. It also states that rent subsidies cannot be used to pay for services in congregate housing or group homes.

It explains the purpose and objective of these housing types: to offer shelter and separately identified supportive services that are affordable and based on assessed local need, while allowing residents to meet any additional medical, discretionary, or personal service needs using their own resources. It defines key terms such as congregate housing, group home, service agreement, service contract, service plan, and tenant base. It also notes that rent subsidy funds cannot be used to pay for services in congregate housing or group homes.

nodes.title: II.OBJECTIVE:
nodes.summary: The document excerpt is an attachment to HB-1-3560 that sets out additional management requirements for congregate housing and group homes under USDA rural housing rules. It explains the purpose and objective of these housing types: to provide shelter and separately identified supportive services that are affordable to tenants, while allowing residents to meet any additional medical, discretionary, or service needs through their own resources. The excerpt also defines key terms such as congregate housing, group home, service agreement, service contract, service plan, and tenant base. Finally, it states that rent subsidy opportunities discussed in the referenced section cannot be used to pay for services in congregate housing or group homes.

nodes.title: III.DEFINITIONS:

It explains the purpose and objective of these housing types: to provide housing and separately arranged supportive services that are affordable and tailored to tenant needs. It defines key terms such as congregate housing, group home, service agreement, service contract, service plan, and tenant base. It also notes that rent subsidies cannot be used to pay for services in congregate housing or group homes.

nodes.title: IV.RENT SUBSIDY OPPORTUNITIES:
nodes.summary:
This partial document is an attachment to USDA Rural Housing guidelines that sets additional management requirements for congregate housing and group homes. It explains the purpose and objectives of these housing types, emphasizing that shelter and services must be treated as separate, affordable components and that tenants should be able to meet unmet medical or personal service needs from their own resources. The document defines key terms such as congregate housing, group home, service agreement, service contract, service plan, and tenant base. It also states that rent subsidies cannot be used to pay for services.

The main operational section requires borrowers to comply with existing rural housing regulations and to submit a service plan describing how services will be delivered. It outlines expectations for borrower and management agent experience, including the need for documented expertise or outside assistance if they are inexperienced. The management plan must address special issues unique to congregate and group home housing, including tenant mix, marketing strategy, service packages, referral services, tenant consultation, and emergency evacuation planning.

nodes.title: V. MANAGEMENT OPERATIONS:
nodes.summary: The document section outlines management operations requirements for congregate housing and group homes under 7 CFR Part 3560. It states that borrowers must provide a service plan describing how services will be delivered and must document their experience or obtain assistance if they are unfamiliar with congregate/group home operations. It also details what the management plan must cover, including the tenant mix the project will serve, marketing strategies, the service packages available, referral services and coordination with outside providers, tenant consultation procedures, and emergency evacuation planning with tenant training and coordination with public/community services.

nodes.title: A. Borrower's experience.
nodes.summary: This partial document outlines management operations requirements for congregate housing and group homes under 7 CFR Part 3560. It explains that borrowers must submit a service plan and have appropriate experience or outside assistance in developing management and servicing plans. The document details what a management plan should cover, including tenant mix, marketing strategies, the service package offered to tenants, referral services and coordination with outside providers, tenant consultation processes, and emergency evacuation planning.

nodes.title: B. Management plan.

It explains that borrowers must submit a service plan and management plan detailing how services will be provided and how the project will be operated. Main points include documenting the borrower’s or management agent’s experience, obtaining outside assistance if needed, and describing key management elements such as tenant mix, marketing strategies, service packages, referral services, tenant consultation procedures, and emergency evacuation planning.

nodes.title: 1. Tenant mix.
nodes.summary: The document section explains management operations requirements for congregate housing and group homes under 7 CFR Part 3560. It says borrowers must submit a service plan showing how services will be provided and must document their experience or obtain experienced assistance if they are new to these housing types. It also outlines key elements that must be included in the management plan, such as the intended tenant mix, marketing strategy, service packages offered, referral services and coordination with outside providers, tenant consultation procedures, and an emergency evacuation plan with tenant training and community/public service coordination.

nodes.title: 2. Marketing plan.
nodes.summary: This excerpt focuses on management requirements for congregate housing and group homes under 7 CFR Part 3560. It explains that borrowers must submit a service plan and demonstrate experience or obtain expert assistance in developing both management and servicing plans. The document then outlines what the management plan must cover, including the tenant mix to be served, marketing strategies, service package options and flexibility, referral services and coordination with outside providers, tenant consultation procedures for support and reasonable accommodations, and emergency evacuation planning with tenant training and community/public service coordination.

nodes.title: 3. Service package.
nodes.summary: The document section covers management operations requirements for congregate housing and group homes under 7 CFR Part 3560. It explains that borrowers must submit a service plan and provide information on their experience or obtain expert assistance if they lack experience. It also outlines what the management plan must address, including tenant mix, marketing strategies, service packages, referral services, tenant consultation, and emergency evacuation planning.

nodes.title: 4. Referral service.
nodes.summary:
This section explains management operation requirements for congregate housing and group homes under 7 CFR Part 3560. It says borrowers must submit a service plan and a management plan that address the special needs of these housing types. The document covers:

- The borrower’s or management agent’s experience and ability to provide congregate or group home services, including the need for outside assistance if they lack experience.
- What the management plan must include for these projects, especially features unique to congregate/group homes.
- Tenant mix: who the housing is intended to serve and the level or type of services tenants will receive.
- Marketing plan: how tenants will be attracted and retained from the target market.
- Service package: the basic and alternative services offered, including possible individual adjustments.
- Referral services: how the project will connect tenants to outside services and make those services known.
- Tenant consultation: how staff will help tenants with service decisions, referrals, and reasonable accommodations.
- Emergency evacuation plan: how tenants will be trained for evacuation and how public/community services will be involved.

nodes.title: 5. Tenant consultation.
nodes.summary:
The document section focuses on management operations requirements for congregate housing and group homes under 7 CFR Part 3560. It explains that borrowers must submit a service plan describing how services will be provided, and that borrowers and management agents need to show relevant experience or seek help from experienced organizations if they are not familiar with congregate/group home housing.

It also outlines the key elements a management plan should cover, including:
- tenant mix and the types of residents the project will serve,
- marketing strategies to attract suitable tenants,
- the service package offered and possible variations,
- referral services and coordination with outside providers,
- tenant consultation processes for service changes and assistance needs,
- and an emergency evacuation plan that includes tenant training and coordination with public/community services.

nodes.title: 6. Emergency evacuation plan.
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines rules and guidance for managing and renting congregate housing and group homes under USDA Rural Development regulations. It focuses on management operations, including borrower experience, required management plans, service plans, service agreements, and service contracts, with attention to service provider qualifications, Fair Housing compliance, and the separation of occupancy leases from service contracts. It also covers renting procedures, defining eligible tenants and tenant selection requirements, especially how tenant mix should be planned and documented for congregate housing to balance residents needing different levels of services.

nodes.title: C. Service plan.
nodes.summary: The document outlines requirements for congregate housing and group home projects, focusing on service-related documentation and tenant selection. It explains that borrowers must submit a service plan, service agreements for any services not directly provided, and sample service contracts for Agency review, with attention to Fair Housing Act and Agency compliance. It also states that service contracts cannot be required for occupancy and must be separate from the lease. The document then covers renting procedures, including eligibility standards for tenants and borrower responsibilities in tenant selection. For congregate housing specifically, it emphasizes establishing a tenant mix based on service needs, describing how many tenants will receive various levels of service packages, and requiring Rural Development concurrence with the proposed mix.

nodes.title: D.Service agreements.
nodes.summary:
The document outlines requirements for congregate housing and group home projects, focusing on service-related documentation and tenant occupancy procedures. It explains that borrowers must submit a service plan, service agreements for any services not directly provided, and sample service contracts for Agency review to ensure compliance with Fair Housing Act and Agency rules. It also states that service contracts cannot be required for occupancy and must be separate from the lease.

The document then describes renting procedures, including eligibility standards for tenants and borrower responsibilities in tenant selection. It emphasizes compliance with USDA Rural Development regulations and proper inquiry into applicants’ needs for congregate or group home housing services.

The management plan must specify how many tenants will receive different levels of service packages, and Rural Development must approve the proposed tenant mix.

nodes.title: VI.RENTING PROCEDURE:

nodes.title: 1. Congregate housing.
nodes.summary: This excerpt outlines requirements for congregate housing and group home projects, focusing on service-related documentation and tenant selection. nodes.title: a. Tenant mix.
nodes.summary:
The partial document outlines requirements for congregate housing and group home borrowers regarding service planning, service agreements, and service contracts. It explains that service plans must be submitted and integrated with management plans when applicable, that separate service agreements are required for any services not directly provided by the borrower, and that sample service contracts must comply with Fair Housing and Agency rules.

It then covers renting procedures, including tenant eligibility and tenant selection standards. For congregate housing, it discusses tenant mix, informing applicants about available services, how applicants request services, and the use of waiting lists for different service package needs. For group homes, it explains that occupancy may be limited to specific tenant groups, applicants must show need for the housing, participation in training or rehabilitation cannot be required, and local applicants should be considered first.

Finally, it explains how per-unit rent is determined in group living arrangements, including how operational costs are divided when units are equal or unequal in size.

nodes.title: b. Selecting services needed or wanted by tenants in congregate housing.
nodes.summary:
The passage explains how congregate housing and group home services should be managed, with emphasis on tenant choice, service selection, and occupancy rules. It states that borrowers must inform applicants and tenants about available supportive services, while applicants or tenants must identify which services they want or need. It also allows only essential information to be collected to determine service needs and appropriate accommodations, and notes that waiting lists may be maintained for different service package levels, with priority generally given to those requesting services sooner.

The document then outlines rules for group homes, including that occupancy may be limited to a specific tenant group such as elderly, developmentally disabled, or mentally impaired tenants, as defined in the management plan. Applicants must show a need for the housing, tenants cannot be required to participate in ongoing training or rehabilitation programs, and local applicants should be considered first.

Finally, it explains how to calculate per-unit rental rates in group living arrangements, defining a unit as the space occupied by a tenant household and stating that operational costs should be divided equally when units are the same size or proportionally when unit sizes differ.

nodes.title: c. Waiting lists.

For group homes, it describes occupancy restrictions to specific tenant groups, the need for applicants to demonstrate their housing need, the prohibition on requiring participation in training or rehabilitation programs, and preference for selecting tenants from the local area. Finally, it outlines how per-unit rental rates are calculated in group living arrangements, depending on whether units are equal or unequal in size.

Finally, it explains how to determine per-unit rental rates in group living arrangements by dividing operational costs equally when units are the same size or proportionally when they are different sizes.

nodes.summary: The document section explains rules for congregate housing and group homes. The document then outlines how to determine per-unit rental rates in group living arrangements, including how to divide operating costs when units are equal or unequal in size and how to treat resident assistant units. It also discusses situations where services are provided to non-tenants, requiring proper leasing arrangements, cost coverage, and priority for project tenants.

nodes.title: a. T he size of traditional units is their square footage.

nodes.summary: The document section covers rules for congregate housing units and tenant services. nodes.summary: The document section explains how to determine unit size for traditional and nontraditional housing units and notes that resident assistant units are excluded from rent calculations because they are not revenue-producing. nodes.title: 1. The services to be provided and the fees to be charged (if any) must be fully
nodes.summary:
The partial document outlines requirements for assisted housing projects that provide services to tenants. It emphasizes that all services and related fees must be clearly documented in the service plan and, when applicable, the lease agreement, with lease approval required by Rural Development leadership. It also explains how rent and service charges must be collected and accounted for separately.

The document further describes budgeting and accounting rules for borrowers, requiring separation of project operating finances from service component finances, including proper allocation of employee compensation when staff work on both. It states that separate financial records must be maintained and that funds from one component cannot subsidize the other, while financial reporting on the service component is only required when requested.

Finally, it covers tenancy termination and eviction rules for congregate or group home housing, noting that eligible tenants may remain as long as they can care for themselves with or without services, and that termination must follow Fair Housing principles and the specific Rural Development regulations.

nodes.title: 2. Any lease agreement must be approved by the Leadership Designee or the loan
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines requirements for providing and documenting services in housing projects, including how service fees must be recorded in service plans and lease agreements and approved by Rural Development officials. It explains rent collection rules for service charges, requiring separate accounting even if payments are combined. It also covers borrower project budgeting and financial management, emphasizing that project operations and service components must be kept separate in budgets, records, audits, and reports. Finally, it addresses termination of tenancy and eviction, stating that tenants in congregate or group home housing may remain as long as they remain eligible and able to care for themselves, and that any termination must follow applicable federal regulations.

nodes.title: IX. RENT COLLECTION:
nodes.summary: The document outlines requirements for service-based housing arrangements under USDA Rural Development rules. It explains that all services and related fees must be fully documented in the service plan and/or lease agreement, and any lease must be approved and include a required approval statement. It then covers rent and service charge collection, stating that tenant payments must be documented and accounted for separately even if paid together. The document also requires borrowers to separate project operating budgets from service component budgets, including proper allocation of employee compensation when staff work on both. It further describes accounting and reporting rules, emphasizing separate financial records, no cross-subsidization between project and service funds, and allocation of revenues and expenses in audits and balance sheets. Finally, it addresses tenancy termination and eviction, explaining that residents may remain as long as they remain eligible and can care for themselves, while termination must follow applicable federal housing regulations.

nodes.title: X. BORROWER PROJECT BUDGETS:
nodes.summary:
