It also explains rent collection rules, emphasizing that charges for services must follow rent collection provisions and be separately accounted for even if paid together. The document further requires borrowers to keep project operations and service-component finances separate, including separate budgets, records, and allocation of employee compensation when staff work on both areas.

Finally, it covers termination of tenancy and eviction, noting that tenants in congregate or group housing may remain as long as they remain eligible and can care for themselves, with or without services, and that eviction or termination must follow the applicable federal regulations.

nodes.title: XI. ACCOUNTING AND REPORTING REQUIREMENTS AND FINANCIAL
nodes.summary:

It also covers rent and service charge collection, stating that tenant payments must be properly documented and accounted for separately even if combined in one payment.

The document further requires borrowers to keep project operations and service component finances separate, including separate budgets, revenue, and expense reporting. It specifies how employee compensation must be prorated when staff provide both housing and service functions. Financial management rules emphasize maintaining separate records and preventing funds from one component from being used to support the other, with detailed reporting only required when requested by Rural Development.

Finally, it addresses termination of tenancy and eviction, explaining that tenants may remain in the housing as long as they remain eligible and able to care for themselves, with or without services, and that tenancy can only be ended according to the applicable federal regulations and lease terms.

nodes.title: XII. TERMINATION OF TENANCY AND EVICTION.
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines Rural Development requirements for congregate housing and group home projects, focusing on how support services must be documented, approved, billed, and accounted for. nodes.title: Section 1.01.
nodes.summary:
This partial document is an occupancy agreement for a cooperative housing project between the Cooperative and a Member. It explains that the Member, as a certificate holder and intended resident, agrees to occupy a specified dwelling unit under the cooperative’s charter, bylaws, and rules for a three-year term, renewable under stated conditions.

The main points covered include:
- The purpose and parties to the agreement
- The Member’s right to occupy a dwelling unit as a permanent residence
- Eligibility requirements, including income certification
- The duration of the occupancy agreement

It also begins to set out the financial terms:
- How the monthly occupancy charge is calculated
- The categories of expenses included in that charge, such as operating costs, reserves, taxes, insurance, utilities, maintenance, and loan payments
- The board’s authority to determine and adjust charges
- Treatment of some charges as capital contributions

The document further covers:
- When occupancy charge payments begin and when they are due each month
- The requirement for the Member to provide an annual income certificate
- The beginning of a section on patronage refunds and how they are handled on the cooperative’s books

nodes.title: Section 1.02.
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines rules for a housing cooperative’s financial and occupancy arrangements. It lists the types of expenses included in occupancy charges, such as reserves, taxes, insurance, utilities, repairs, debt payments, and other board-approved costs. It explains that the board of directors sets occupancy charges annually or more often if needed, and that amounts used for mortgage principal or capital expenditures are treated as capital contributions by members. The document also states when occupancy payments begin, how monthly charges are due in advance, and that members must provide annual income certificates to determine charges. Finally, it begins a section on patronage refunds, indicating that the cooperative will allocate refunds to members according to IRS rules.

nodes.title: ARTICLE 2. WHEN PAYMENT OF OCCUPANCY CHARGES TO COMMENCE.
nodes.summary:
The partial document describes a housing cooperative occupancy agreement and focuses on how occupancy charges are calculated, what expenses they cover, and when they must be paid. It lists the costs included in the charges, such as reserves, taxes, insurance, utilities, repairs, debt payments, and other approved expenses. It also explains that the board sets these charges annually or more often if needed, and that part of the payment for mortgage principal or capital expenditures is treated as a capital contribution. 

The document further states when payments begin, requiring payment after notice of availability or upon move-in, and then monthly in advance. It also requires members to provide annual income certificates used to determine charges. Finally, it begins a section on patronage refunds, indicating that the cooperative will allocate patronage capital to members according to IRS guidance.

nodes.title: Section 2.01.
nodes.summary: The partial document describes a cooperative housing occupancy agreement, focusing on how occupancy charges are calculated, what expenses they cover, and when payments begin. It lists the types of costs included in the charges, such as reserves, taxes, insurance, utilities, repairs, debt payments, and other board-approved expenses. It also explains that the board of directors sets these charges annually or more often if needed, and that capital-related portions are treated as members’ capital contributions. In addition, the document outlines the timing of occupancy charge payments, requires members to provide annual income certificates, and begins a section on patronage refunds.

nodes.title: Section 2.02.
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines the rules and components for setting and paying cooperative occupancy charges in a housing project. It lists the types of expenses included in the charges, such as management reserves, taxes, insurance, utilities, reserves, maintenance, debt payments, and other approved expenses. It explains that the board of directors determines these charges annually or more often if needed, and that amounts used for mortgage principal or capital expenditures are treated as capital contributions. The document also states when occupancy payments begin, requiring payment after notice of availability or upon move-in, with monthly payments due in advance. Members must provide annual income certificates to determine their charges. Finally, it begins a section on patronage refunds, indicating that patronage capital will be assigned to members according to IRS rules.

nodes.title: ARTICLE 3. PATRONAGE REFUNDS.
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines the rules for determining and paying cooperative occupancy charges in a housing project. It lists the types of costs included in the charges, such as reserves, taxes, insurance, utilities, repairs, debt payments, and other board- and Rural Development-approved expenses. It explains that the board of directors sets the occupancy charges annually or more often if needed, and that members may be charged only their fair share, with certain mortgage principal or capital expenditure amounts treated as capital contributions. The document also states when occupancy charges begin, requiring payment after notice of unit availability or upon occupancy, with monthly payments due in advance. Members must provide annual income certificates to determine charges. Finally, it begins a section on patronage refunds, indicating that the cooperative will allocate patronage capital to members according to IRS rulings.

nodes.title: Section 3.01.
nodes.summary:
The partial document outlines key terms of a cooperative housing occupancy agreement. It covers how occupancy charges are calculated and paid, including costs for reserves, taxes, insurance, utilities, maintenance, debt service, and other board-approved expenses. It explains that the board determines charges annually, with part of payments toward principal treated as capital contributions, and that members must provide annual income certification.

The document also addresses patronage refunds, stating that excess funds beyond cooperative expenses may be assigned to members as patronage capital. It describes renewal rights for the occupancy term, including notice requirements, obligations before vacating, and conditions for retaining accrued patronage capital.

In addition, the agreement restricts use of the premises to residential purposes only, governs member conduct to avoid nuisances or increased insurance costs, and requires compliance with health and governmental rules. It confirms the member’s right to peaceful possession while the agreement is in effect and begins a rule that subletting is not allowed without the corporation’s consent.

nodes.title: ARTICLE 4. MEMBER'S OPTION TO RENEW.
nodes.summary: The document covers key terms of a cooperative housing occupancy agreement. It explains how any excess money collected beyond cooperative expenses may be allocated and reported to members. It then describes the member’s option to renew the occupancy term, generally in three-year periods, and the conditions required to avoid further liability when not renewing, such as giving proper notice, settling all obligations, returning the membership certificate, and vacating the unit in good condition. The next section states that the premises must be used only as a private residential dwelling for the member and immediate household, with access to common facilities while the member remains in compliance, and it restricts sublessees from voting. It also outlines member responsibilities to avoid increasing insurance costs, prevent nuisance or illegal activity, and comply with health and government regulations, while making the member liable for extra insurance premiums caused by their use. Finally, it affirms the member’s right to peaceful possession of the unit and shared facilities so long as the agreement remains in effect, and begins a new article on prohibiting subletting without the corporation’s consent.

nodes.title: Section 4.01.
nodes.summary: The document outlines several key provisions of a cooperative housing occupancy agreement. It explains how excess collected money may be distributed as patronage capital, and how members are notified of annual assignments. It then covers the member’s option to renew the occupancy term for additional three-year periods, including notice requirements, obligations before expiration, and conditions for ending liability and receiving accrued patronage capital. The document also states that the premises must be used only as a private residential dwelling, with rules limiting use, protecting common property, preventing nuisances or illegal activity, and assigning responsibility for any increased insurance costs caused by the member’s use. Finally, it affirms the member’s right to peaceful possession of the dwelling and facilities so long as the agreement is followed, and begins a new article requiring consent before any subletting.

nodes.title: ARTICLE 5. PREMISES TO BE USED FOR RESIDENTIAL PURPOSES ONLY.
nodes.summary: The document outlines key terms of a cooperative housing occupancy agreement. It explains the member’s right to renew occupancy for additional three-year periods, the conditions required to end or not renew the agreement, and the member’s entitlement to accrued patronage capital if those conditions are met. It also states that the premises may be used only as a private residence for the member and immediate household, with use of common facilities allowed, but no voting rights for approved sublessees. The document sets behavior and compliance rules for the premises, including avoiding nuisances, illegal activity, and increased insurance costs, with liability for extra insurance premiums if caused by the member. Finally, it affirms the member’s right to peaceable possession of the dwelling and community facilities so long as the agreement is properly followed, and introduces a rule that subletting requires the corporation’s consent.

nodes.title: Section 5.01.
nodes.summary: The document outlines key terms of a cooperative housing occupancy agreement. It explains how members may renew their occupancy for additional three-year terms, including the notice required if they choose not to renew, their obligations before vacating, and their entitlement to patronage capital earned during the term. It states that the premises must be used only as a private residential dwelling for the member and immediate household, with approved sublessees allowed certain rights but no voting rights. It also covers rules to avoid increasing insurance costs, disturbing other residents, or violating laws, and makes the member responsible for any extra insurance premiums caused by their use. Finally, it affirms the member’s right to peaceful possession of the unit and community facilities while complying with the agreement, and begins a section on prohibiting subletting without the corporation’s consent.

nodes.title: Section 5.02.
nodes.summary: The excerpt outlines several key terms of a cooperative housing occupancy agreement. It explains how excess money collected by the cooperative may be allocated as patronage capital and that members will be notified annually. It then describes the member’s option to renew the occupancy term for additional three-year periods, including the required notice, transfer of the membership certificate, payment of all obligations, and vacating the unit in good condition; if these conditions are met, the member has no further liability and may receive accrued patronage capital. The document also states that the premises may be used only as a private residence for the member and immediate household, with access to common facilities, and that any approved sublessee has similar use rights but no voting rights. It further imposes rules against behavior that would raise insurance costs, create nuisances, interfere with others, or violate laws, and makes the member liable for added insurance premiums caused by their use. Finally, it affirms the member’s right to peaceful possession of the dwelling and community facilities as long as the agreement terms are followed, and begins a new article stating that subletting is not allowed without corporate consent.

nodes.title: ARTICLE 6. MEMBER'S RIGHT TO PEACEABLE POSSESSION.
nodes.summary: The excerpt outlines the cooperative member’s occupancy agreement, focusing on renewal rights, residential use restrictions, and the right to peaceful possession. It explains that the occupancy term renews automatically for three-year periods unless the member gives timely written notice of nonrenewal and satisfies all exit obligations, including returning the membership certificate, paying amounts due, and leaving the unit in good condition. It also states that members may use the dwelling only as a private residence for themselves and their immediate household, must avoid nuisance or illegal activity, and must not increase insurance risk, with responsibility for extra insurance costs if they do. Finally, it confirms that, as long as the member complies with the agreement, the cooperative guarantees the member’s peaceful use of the dwelling and common facilities.

nodes.title: Section 6.01.
nodes.summary:
The partial document describes the terms of a cooperative housing occupancy agreement. It covers:

- Renewal of the member’s occupancy term, including notice requirements, conditions for renewal or vacating, and return of patronage capital.
- Rules limiting use of the premises to residential purposes only, along with member responsibilities for behavior, compliance with laws, and insurance consequences.
- The member’s right to peaceful possession of the dwelling and common facilities while complying with the agreement.
- Restrictions on subletting and assignment, requiring written consent and allowing termination for unauthorized subleasing.
- Limits on transferability of the agreement and occupancy rights.
- The cooperative’s responsibilities for management, taxes, and insurance, excluding the member’s personal property.
- Utility arrangements, including cooperative handling of common-area utilities and members paying individually metered utilities directly.
- Repair and maintenance responsibilities, dividing obligations between the member and the cooperative, and allowing access to the unit for repairs and emergencies.

nodes.title: ARTICLE 7. NO SUBLETTING WITHOUT CONSENT OF CORPORATION.
nodes.summary: The document outlines key terms of a cooperative housing occupancy agreement. It covers restrictions on subleasing and assignment of the agreement, transfer rules tied to cooperative bylaws, and the cooperative’s responsibilities for management, taxes, insurance, and utilities. It also explains who is responsible for repairs and maintenance: the member handles damage from negligence, misuse, or member-authorized redecoration, while the cooperative covers general repairs and must be allowed reasonable access to the dwelling unit for maintenance and emergencies.

nodes.title: Section 7.01.
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines key terms of a cooperative housing occupancy agreement. It covers rules on subleasing and assignment, stating that the dwelling unit cannot be assigned or sublet without written consent and that unauthorized subleasing can lead to termination of occupancy rights. It then addresses transfer restrictions, limiting transfers to those allowed under the cooperative bylaws. The document also describes the cooperative’s responsibilities for management, operations, taxes, and insurance, while excluding coverage for the member’s personal property. It explains utility arrangements, including the cooperative’s role in providing utilities for common areas and each member’s obligation to pay for separately metered utilities directly. Finally, it details repair responsibilities, specifying which repairs are the member’s responsibility and which are the cooperative’s, along with the cooperative’s right to enter the unit for necessary repairs and maintenance.

nodes.title: ARTICLE 8. TRANSFERS.
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines key terms of a cooperative housing occupancy agreement. It covers rules on subleasing and assignment of the agreement, stating that written consent is required and unauthorized subleasing can lead to termination of occupancy rights. It explains transfer restrictions, limiting transfer of occupancy rights to procedures allowed by the cooperative bylaws. It also describes the cooperative’s responsibilities for management, taxes, and insurance, including property insurance but not members’ personal property. In addition, it addresses utility arrangements, specifying that common-area utilities are handled by the cooperative while each unit is separately metered and members pay their own utility bills. Finally, it details repair responsibilities, dividing maintenance between the member and the cooperative, and giving the cooperative access to units for repairs and maintenance when necessary.

nodes.title: Section 8.01.
nodes.summary:
The document excerpt covers several key provisions of a cooperative occupancy agreement. It states that the agreement cannot be assigned or the dwelling sublet without written consent from the Cooperative and Rural Development, and unauthorized subleasing may lead to termination of occupancy rights. It then explains that membership and occupancy rights are generally not transferable except as allowed under the cooperative bylaws.

The excerpt also outlines the Cooperative’s responsibilities for managing the project, paying taxes and assessments, and obtaining insurance for the property, while excluding personal property insurance for the member. It describes utility arrangements, where the Cooperative provides utilities for common areas, but each unit is separately metered and the member pays their own utility bills directly to the supplier.

Finally, it addresses repairs and maintenance responsibilities, specifying that the member must handle repairs caused by their own negligence or misuse and any personal redecoration or other listed items, while the Cooperative is responsible for all other necessary repairs, maintenance, and replacements. It also grants the Cooperative the right to enter the dwelling unit with reasonable notice to perform repairs or in emergencies.

nodes.title: ARTICLE 9. MANAGEMENT, TAXES AND INSURANCE.
nodes.summary:
The partial document outlines several key provisions of a cooperative occupancy agreement. It states that the member may not assign or sublet the dwelling unit without written consent from the Cooperative and Rural Development, and unauthorized subleasing can lead to termination of occupancy rights. It then explains that occupancy rights and the agreement itself are generally not transferable except through the cooperative’s membership transfer bylaws.

The document also covers responsibilities for management, taxes, and insurance, specifying that the Cooperative must manage the project, pay taxes and assessments, and provide fire, extended coverage, and other insurance for the project, but not for the member’s personal property. It addresses utilities, saying the Cooperative will arrange utilities for common areas while each unit is separately metered and the member pays utility bills directly to the supplier.

Finally, it discusses repair responsibilities: the member must pay for repairs caused by their own negligence or misuse, authorized redecoration, and any specifically listed items subject to approval, while the Cooperative is responsible for all other necessary repairs and maintenance. It also gives the Cooperative the right to enter the unit at reasonable times and with reasonable notice to make repairs, with emergency access allowed as needed.

nodes.title: Section 9.01.
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines key terms of a cooperative occupancy agreement. It covers rules on subleasing and assignment, stating that the unit cannot be sublet or assigned without written consent and that unauthorized subleasing can lead to termination of occupancy rights. It then explains transfer restrictions, limiting transfer of occupancy rights to the cooperative’s bylaws. The document also describes responsibilities for management, taxes, insurance, and utilities: the cooperative handles project management, tax payments, and insurance for the property, while members pay directly for separately metered utilities. Finally, it addresses repairs, distinguishing between member responsibilities for damage caused by negligence or member-ordered changes and the cooperative’s responsibility for other repairs and maintenance, including the cooperative’s right to enter the unit for necessary work.

nodes.title: ARTICLE 10. UTILITIES.
nodes.summary:
The excerpt covers key rules for occupancy and management in a cooperative housing agreement. It explains that the member cannot assign the agreement or sublet the dwelling without written consent, and unauthorized subleasing can lead to termination of occupancy rights. It then states that transfers of occupancy rights are only allowed according to the cooperative’s bylaws.

The document also outlines the cooperative’s responsibilities for managing the project, paying taxes, and obtaining insurance, while clarifying that the cooperative does not insure the member’s personal property. It describes utility arrangements, noting that common-area utilities are handled by the cooperative, but each unit is separately metered and the member pays utility bills directly.

Finally, it addresses repair responsibilities: the member must pay for repairs caused by their own negligence, misuse, or authorized redecorating, while the cooperative is responsible for most other repairs, maintenance, and replacements. It also gives the cooperative the right to enter the unit, with reasonable notice, to carry out necessary work.

nodes.title: Section 10.01.
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines key terms of a cooperative housing occupancy agreement. It covers restrictions on subleasing and assignment of the dwelling unit, transfer rules for member occupancy rights, and the cooperative’s responsibilities for management, taxes, insurance, utilities, and repairs. It also explains the member’s obligations to pay for utilities directly, maintain and repair the unit in certain cases, and allow authorized access for repairs and maintenance.

nodes.title: ARTICLE 11. REPAIRS.
nodes.summary: The document excerpt outlines key terms of a cooperative housing occupancy agreement. It covers rules on subleasing and assignment, stating that the dwelling unit cannot be sublet or the agreement assigned without written consent, and unauthorized subleasing may lead to termination of occupancy rights. It then addresses transfer restrictions, limiting transfers to those allowed under the cooperative’s bylaws. The excerpt also explains management responsibilities, including the cooperative’s duty to operate the project, pay taxes, and maintain insurance for the property, while not insuring members’ personal property. It describes utility arrangements, with the cooperative handling common-area utilities and members paying directly for separately metered unit utilities. Finally, it details repair responsibilities, specifying what repairs and maintenance are the member’s responsibility versus the cooperative’s, and grants the cooperative access to units for necessary repairs and maintenance.

nodes.title: Section 11.01. By Member.
nodes.summary:
The document is a cooperative housing occupancy agreement covering several key areas:

- **Subleasing and assignment restrictions**: The member cannot assign the agreement or sublet the dwelling without written consent from the Cooperative and Rural Development (while it is the mortgagee). Unauthorized subleasing can lead to termination and loss of occupancy rights.

- **Transfer limitations**: The occupancy agreement and the member’s right to occupy the unit generally cannot be transferred or assigned except as allowed by the cooperative bylaws regarding membership transfer.

- **Management, taxes, and insurance responsibilities**: The Cooperative is responsible for managing and administering the project, paying taxes and assessments, and obtaining insurance for the property. However, it does not insure the member’s personal belongings.

- **Utilities**: The Cooperative arranges utilities for common areas as needed, while each unit is separately metered and the member pays utility bills directly to the supplier.

- **Repairs and maintenance**: Members are responsible for repairs caused by their negligence or misuse, any redecoration they authorize, and any other specifically listed items. The Cooperative handles all other necessary repairs, maintenance, and replacements, and is allowed access to units with reasonable notice to carry out those duties, including emergency access.

nodes.title: Section 11.02. By Cooperative.
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines key terms of a cooperative housing occupancy agreement. It covers rules on subleasing and assignment, stating that subletting or transfer is prohibited without written consent and may lead to termination of occupancy rights. It explains the cooperative’s responsibilities for management, taxes, insurance, and utilities, including that utilities for common areas are arranged by the cooperative while individual unit utility bills are paid directly by the member. It details repair obligations, with members responsible for certain repairs caused by their negligence or authorized changes, while the cooperative handles most other maintenance and may enter units with notice or in emergencies. The document also restricts alterations and additions to the unit, requiring written approval for structural changes and installation of certain equipment. Finally, it defines events of default, such as loss of membership, unauthorized transfer, bankruptcy, or receivership, and describes the process by which the cooperative may terminate occupancy and repossess the unit after notice.

nodes.title: Section 11.03. Right of Cooperative to Make Repairs at Member's Expense.
nodes.summary:
The excerpt covers several related housing agreement provisions for a cooperative member:

- **Repairs and maintenance responsibilities:** It explains that if the member fails to make required repairs, maintenance, or replacements satisfactorily, the cooperative board may complete the work and charge the cost to the member’s next monthly occupancy payment.
- **Alterations and additions:** The member may not make structural changes, modify utilities or fixtures, or remove improvements without written consent. If the member stops occupying the premises, they must surrender the unit along with any alterations, additions, fixtures, and improvements.
- **Restrictions on equipment:** The member cannot install or use air conditioners, electric heaters, or power tools without prior written approval, and the cooperative or Rural Development may require removal of such equipment. Failure to remove it can be treated as a default.
- **Default and consequences:** The document defines what events count as member default, including losing cooperative membership, improper transfer or assignment, bankruptcy, or appointment of a receiver. If default occurs, the cooperative may give notice, allow a period to cure the default, and then terminate rights, reenter the unit, remove occupants and property, and repossess the dwelling.

nodes.title: ARTICLE 12. ALTERATIONS AND ADDITIONS.
nodes.summary: The passage covers maintenance responsibilities and the cooperative’s right to make repairs at the member’s expense if the member fails to do so satisfactorily. It then explains rules on alterations and additions, including prohibitions on structural changes, removal of fixtures, and installing certain equipment without written consent, as well as the obligation to surrender all improvements when vacating. Finally, it outlines default provisions, describing events that trigger default, the notice process, the member’s opportunity to cure, and the cooperative’s right to terminate occupancy and repossess the unit, with examples of default including loss of membership, improper transfer, bankruptcy, and receivership.

nodes.title: Section 12.01.
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines several key obligations and rights under a cooperative occupancy agreement. It covers the member’s responsibility to maintain and repair the premises, and allows the cooperative to make necessary repairs at the member’s expense if the member fails to do so. It then addresses restrictions on alterations and additions, prohibiting structural changes or removal of fixtures without written consent, and requiring the member to surrender any improvements when vacating. The document also restricts installation of equipment like air conditioners, electric heaters, and power tools without permission, and allows the cooperative or mortgagee to require removal of such equipment. Finally, it defines events of default, explaining that if certain conditions occur—such as loss of membership, unauthorized transfer of the agreement, bankruptcy, or appointment of a receiver—the cooperative may terminate the agreement after notice and repossess the unit.

nodes.title: Section 12.02.
nodes.summary: This excerpt covers a cooperative housing agreement’s rules on maintenance, alterations, and default. It explains that if a member fails to perform required repairs or maintenance, the cooperative may make the repairs and charge the cost to the member. It also restricts members from making structural changes or removing fixtures without written consent, and from installing certain equipment like air conditioners, electric heaters, or power tools without approval. If a member leaves, they must surrender the unit along with any alterations or improvements. The final section defines events that constitute default, such as losing membership, improper transfer of the agreement, bankruptcy, or appointment of a receiver, and outlines the cooperative’s right to give notice, terminate occupancy rights, and repossess the dwelling unit.

nodes.title: Section 12.03.
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines the cooperative’s rules on repairs, alterations, and defaults in a member occupancy agreement. It states that if a member fails to make required repairs or maintenance, the cooperative board may do the work and charge the cost to the member. It then restricts structural changes, removal of fixtures, and installation of certain equipment without prior written consent, and requires the member to surrender all alterations and improvements when leaving the premises. The final section defines several events that constitute default, including loss of membership, improper transfer or assignment, bankruptcy, and appointment of a receiver, and explains that the cooperative may terminate the agreement, regain possession, and remove occupants and property after notice.

nodes.title: ARTICLE 13. DEFINITION OF DEFAULT BY MEMBER AND EFFECT THEREOF.
nodes.summary: The excerpt outlines a cooperative housing agreement’s rules on maintenance, alterations, and defaults. It explains that members are responsible for repairs and upkeep, and if they fail to make required repairs, the cooperative may do the work and charge the cost to the member’s occupancy payment. It then restricts members from making structural changes, removing fixtures, or installing certain equipment without written consent, and requires surrender of all alterations and improvements if the member leaves. Finally, it defines several events that constitute member default, such as losing membership ownership, improper transfer or assignment, bankruptcy, or appointment of a receiver, and states that the cooperative may terminate rights, repossess the unit, and remove occupants and property after notice and expiration.

nodes.title: Section 13.01.
nodes.summary:
The excerpt covers rules and obligations for cooperative members regarding repairs, alterations, defaults, and compliance with cooperative regulations. It explains that if a member fails to maintain or repair the premises, the cooperative may make the repairs and charge the cost to the member. It also restricts members from making structural changes or installing certain equipment without written consent, and requires surrender of any alterations or improvements when occupancy ends.

The document then details what counts as a default by the member, including loss of membership, improper transfer of rights, bankruptcy, appointment of a receiver, creditor assignments, unpaid obligations, failure to maintain the unit, failure to provide income or eligibility information, and exceeding occupancy limits. It describes the consequences of default, such as termination of rights, eviction, and the cooperative’s right to repossess the unit, while noting that eviction must follow judicial and legal procedures. Finally, it states that members must follow the cooperative’s charter, bylaws, rules, and regulations and support cooperative principles and community standards.

nodes.title: Section 13.02.
nodes.summary: The excerpt outlines the grounds for member default and eviction in a cooperative housing agreement, including failure to pay dues, make required repairs, comply with income or eligibility requests, or otherwise violate the agreement. It explains that members waive certain reentry rights if eviction occurs by court order, and that the cooperative may seek legal remedies without losing rights by not using one remedy immediately. It also states that eviction must follow written notice and judicial action under applicable state or local law and federal regulations. Finally, it begins Article 14, which requires members to follow the cooperative’s charter, bylaws, rules, and regulations, and to support cooperative principles through active participation and community responsibility.

nodes.title: Section 13.03.
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines the grounds and procedures for member default and eviction in a housing cooperative, including specific violations such as failure to pay, maintain the dwelling, provide required income/eligibility information, or comply with occupancy limits. It explains that members waive certain rights to reenter after eviction, that the cooperative may seek legal and equitable remedies, and that not using one remedy does not waive others. It also states that eviction must be carried out through written notice and judicial action under applicable state/local law and federal regulations. The document then begins Article 14, which requires members to comply with all cooperative regulations, support cooperative principles, follow bylaws and rules, and participate in cooperative responsibilities and community standards.

nodes.title: Section 13.04.
nodes.summary: The excerpt outlines the conditions under which a cooperative member may be considered in default or subject to eviction, including failure to pay amounts due, neglecting required repairs, violating agreement terms, failing to provide income/eligibility information, or exceeding occupancy limits. It also explains the cooperative’s remedies, such as injunctions and legal action, while clarifying that not using one remedy does not waive others. Additionally, it requires that eviction notices be written and that eviction occur only through judicial action under applicable law and federal regulations. The final section begins Article 14, stating that members must comply with the cooperative’s charter, bylaws, rules, and regulations, support cooperative principles, and participate responsibly in community and cooperative activities.

nodes.title: ARTICLE 14. MEMBER TO COMPLY WITH ALL CORPORATE REGULATIONS
nodes.summary: The passage outlines conditions under which a cooperative member may be in default or subject to eviction, including insolvency, levy and sale of membership rights, failure to maintain the dwelling, nonpayment, other contractual violations, failure to provide required income/eligibility information, and exceeding occupancy limits. It also explains that the member waives certain reentry rights if evicted by court order, and that the cooperative may seek injunctions and other legal remedies without waiving future rights if it does not use a remedy immediately. The section requires written notice for eviction-related actions and states that eviction can only occur through judicial action under applicable law and federal regulations. The final part begins Article 14, which requires members to follow the cooperative’s charter, bylaws, rules, and regulations, and to support cooperative principles and community standards through participation and responsible conduct.

nodes.title: Section 14.01.
nodes.summary:
The partial document covers several key provisions of a cooperative housing occupancy agreement. It lists additional grounds for default or eviction, including general assignment for creditors, court levy on membership rights, failure to pay for repairs or charges, failure to comply with income/eligibility reporting, and exceeding occupancy limits set by Rural Development. It explains that the member waives the right to reenter the dwelling after court-ordered eviction and that the cooperative may seek injunctions and all legal or equitable remedies. It also states that the cooperative’s failure to enforce one remedy does not waive future rights, and that eviction notices must be written and judicially executed under applicable law.

The document then requires members to follow all corporate regulations, support cooperative principles, and participate in cooperative responsibilities. It addresses fire or casualty loss, giving the cooperative authority to decide whether to restore the dwelling and how occupancy charges are handled during or after restoration. It also grants inspection rights to mortgage holders, cooperative officials, and authorized representatives under reasonable conditions. Finally, it includes a subordination clause making the agreement subject to the cooperative’s mortgage or financing instruments with Rural Development, and authorizes execution of necessary documents to preserve that priority.

nodes.title: ARTICLE 15. EFFECT OF FIRE LOSS ON INTERESTS OF MEMBER
nodes.summary: The partial document covers several housing cooperative agreement provisions. It explains the cooperative’s duty to distribute its rules and regulations and provide adequate notice. It then addresses how fire or casualty damage to a member’s dwelling unit is handled, including the cooperative’s decision whether to restore the unit and how occupancy charges are affected if restoration occurs or does not occur. The document also sets out inspection rights, allowing mortgagee representatives, cooperative officers, and authorized agents to enter the unit for inspections with reasonable notice or immediately in emergencies. Finally, it includes a subordination clause stating that the member’s agreement is subordinate to the cooperative’s mortgage or other financing obligations to Rural Development, and that the member must cooperate in signing necessary documents to establish that subordination.

nodes.title: Section 15.01.
nodes.summary:
The partial document covers several cooperative housing agreement provisions:

- **Delivery of rules and regulations:** The Cooperative must provide the Member with its rules and regulations, or give adequate notice of them through another method.
- **Fire loss and casualty effects:** If the Member’s dwelling is damaged by fire or another casualty through no fault of the Member, the Cooperative determines whether the premises will be restored. If restored, occupancy charges may be suspended partly or fully until completion; if not restored, occupancy charges stop as of the date of loss. It also addresses how the Member may be compensated for redeeming membership and other losses.
- **Inspection rights:** Authorized representatives of mortgage holders, the Cooperative, contractors, utilities, and municipal agencies may enter the dwelling unit to inspect it at reasonable times with reasonable notice, and at any time in emergencies.
- **Subordination clause:** The Member’s agreement is subordinate to the Cooperative’s first mortgage and related financing documents with Rural Development or any replacement security instrument. The Member agrees to sign any needed documents to effect this subordination and authorizes Cooperative officers to sign on the Member’s behalf if necessary.

nodes.title: Section 15.02.
nodes.summary:
The partial document outlines several cooperative housing agreement provisions:

- The cooperative’s obligation to deliver or adequately distribute its rules and regulations to members.
- Procedures for handling fire or casualty damage to a member’s dwelling unit, including the cooperative’s decision whether to restore the unit and how redemption/reimbursement is determined if it does not.
- How occupancy charges are treated after such damage: they may be suspended while repairs are made, or stopped entirely if the unit is not restored.
- The cooperative’s right, and that of mortgagees and authorized representatives, to inspect a member’s dwelling unit at reasonable times with notice, and at any time in emergencies.
- A subordination clause stating that the member’s agreement is subordinate to Rural Development financing and related security instruments, and authorizing execution of any documents needed to effect that subordination.

nodes.title: ARTICLE 16. INSPECTION OF DWELLING UNIT
nodes.summary:
This portion of the document covers several key cooperative housing provisions:

- **Delivery of rules and regulations:** The Cooperative must provide members with its rules and regulations, or distribute them in a way that gives adequate notice.
- **Fire loss and casualty damage:** If a member’s dwelling is damaged by fire or another casualty without the member’s fault, the Cooperative decides whether to repair the unit. If it restores the premises, occupancy charges may be suspended fully or partially until repairs are complete; if it does not restore the unit, charges stop from the date of the loss.
- **Inspection rights:** The Cooperative, mortgagee representatives, and authorized contractors, utilities, or municipal agents may enter and inspect the dwelling unit at reasonable times with reasonable notice, and without delay in emergencies.
- **Subordination clause:** The member’s rights under the agreement are subordinate to the Cooperative’s first mortgage or other financing instruments related to Rural Development. The member must sign documents needed to confirm this subordination, and authorizes the Cooperative officers to act on the member’s behalf if necessary.

nodes.title: Section 16.01.
nodes.summary:
This section covers several key provisions of the cooperative agreement:

- **Delivery of rules and regulations:** The cooperative must provide members with its rules and regulations in a way that gives adequate notice.
- **Fire or casualty loss:** If a member’s dwelling is damaged by fire or another casualty without the member’s fault, the cooperative decides whether the premises will be restored and, if not, what amount will be paid to redeem the member’s interest and reimburse losses.
- **Occupancy charges after damage:** If the cooperative restores the unit, occupancy charges may be reduced or suspended during repairs. If it does not restore the unit, occupancy charges stop from the date of the loss.
- **Inspection rights:** Mortgagee representatives, cooperative officers, and authorized representatives may enter and inspect the dwelling unit at reasonable times with notice, and at any time in emergencies.
- **Subordination clause:** The member’s agreement is subordinate to the cooperative’s mortgage or other security interests related to loans from Rural Development or similar lenders. The member must sign any needed documents to confirm this subordination, and the cooperative or its officers may sign on the member’s behalf if necessary.

nodes.title: ARTICLE 17. SUBORDINATION CLAUSE
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines several cooperative housing agreement provisions. It then covers how fire or casualty damage to a member’s dwelling unit is handled, including the Cooperative’s decision whether to restore the unit, how member occupancy charges are affected, and how compensation or redemption of membership is determined if the unit is not restored. The document also grants inspection rights to mortgagee representatives, Cooperative officers, and authorized agents, allowing entry for inspections at reasonable times, with notice, or immediately in emergencies. Finally, it contains a subordination clause stating that the member’s agreement is subordinate to Rural Development financing or replacement security instruments, and requires the member to execute any necessary documents to confirm that subordination.

nodes.title: Section 17.01.
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines several cooperative housing agreement provisions. Finally, it contains a subordination clause stating that the member’s rights are subordinate to the cooperative’s mortgage or other security interests related to Rural Development financing, and authorizes the member to sign documents needed to carry out that subordination.

nodes.title: Attachment 11 -D
nodes.summary: The document is a cooperative housing survey used to assess whether an applicant understands and is willing to participate in the responsibilities of cooperative living. It asks yes/no questions about sharing responsibilities, serving on the board or committees, helping maintain the property, understanding cooperative housing, and interest in attending an information meeting. It also checks whether the respondent answered honestly and indicates that answering “no” to key responsibility questions may mean the housing is not suitable. The survey then begins a second section collecting household size information, including numbers of adults and children.

nodes.title: COOPERATIVE HOUSING SURVEY
nodes.summary: The document is a “Cooperative Housing Survey” used to gauge interest in a proposed cooperative housing community and to screen potential members. It also includes a warning that answering “no” to key responsibility questions means the housing may not be suitable. It also provides space for details about an informational meeting for a proposed unit cooperative.

nodes.title: Attachment 11 -E
nodes.summary: This document is a Limited Equity Agreement between a cooperative housing corporation and the Rural Housing Service (RHS). It explains that the cooperative is seeking RHS mortgage financing under Section 515 of the Housing Act of 1949 and that RHS aims to provide long-term affordable housing for very-low, low-, and moderate-income households. The agreement sets out the cooperative’s commitments to limit equity distribution, including assigning certain accumulated equity to members’ patronage accounts, notifying members annually of those amounts, and preventing dissolution of the cooperative merely to distribute equity. It also states that if dissolution becomes necessary, the cooperative’s assets must be transferred to another nonprofit or municipal organization to continue serving the same housing purposes.

nodes.title: LIMITED EQUITY AGREEMENT
nodes.summary:
The document is a Limited Equity Agreement between a cooperative housing organization and the USDA Rural Housing Service (RHS). It explains that the cooperative is seeking RHS mortgage financing under Section 515 of the Housing Act of 1949 and outlines the shared purpose of maintaining affordable housing for very-low, low-, and moderate-income households for as long as possible.

The main provisions state that:
- Cooperative equity, excluding appreciation from real estate and assets, must be allocated equally to members as patronage capital.
- Members must be notified in writing each year of the amount credited to their patronage accounts.
- The cooperative cannot dissolve simply to distribute equity, except in cases of default or uncontrollable circumstances.
- If dissolution becomes necessary, assets must be transferred to another nonprofit or municipal organization that will continue the same purpose.
- Members are only entitled to their membership fees and patronage capital, and the cooperative may deduct unpaid occupancy charges or damage costs from those payments.

The document is signed by the cooperative and member, witnessed, and filed in the member’s record.

nodes.title: Attachment 11 -F
nodes.summary:
The partial document outlines the qualifications required for an adviser to a board, especially in the context of cooperative housing and low-income communities. It emphasizes that the adviser should first and foremost be a “friend” to those being helped—someone trusted, supportive, patient, understanding, and willing to listen. The adviser must be able to explain a new housing concept to people accustomed to renting, work with individuals of varying educational backgrounds, and be willing to repeat and clarify complex regulations or legal documents as needed. It also stresses the importance of caring about the people involved, learning from them while teaching, and helping improve their well-being.

The document further notes that the adviser should be able to work effectively with city officials, government representatives, politicians, and other professionals to support local housing goals. In addition, it lists educational background requirements, including experience working with low-income people, administrative skills in setting up management and recordkeeping systems, maintenance planning for buildings and equipment, and training in teaching, group learning, and group dynamics.

nodes.title: QUALIFICATIONS FOR AN ADVISER TO THE BOARD
nodes.summary:
This document describes the qualifications for an adviser to a cooperative board, especially in the context of housing for low-income residents. It emphasizes that the adviser should be a trusted, caring, patient, and understanding person who can explain complex regulations and help people unfamiliar with cooperative housing. The adviser should be able to work closely with residents, listen to their concerns, and support both individual and group learning.

The document also outlines educational and professional background expectations. These include experience working with low-income populations, administrative skills for managing records and maintenance systems, and training in teaching, group dynamics, problem-solving, social casework, resource use, and cooperative management. It further stresses the ability to interpret funding agency documents and teach members to manage the cooperative themselves. Social work experience is presented as the ideal background, though other relevant experience may also be acceptable.

nodes.title: Attachment 11 -G
nodes.summary: The partial document explains the role and relationship of an adviser to cooperative members. It emphasizes that the adviser should teach members management and maintenance skills, help them gradually take on responsibility until the cooperative is self-managed, and promote a cooperative mindset. It outlines specific areas the adviser must be able to teach, including management procedures, working with authority, using resources, board and committee functions, regulatory documents, and attitudes that support cooperation, responsibility, and participation. The document also stresses that members must understand and follow rules, including the obligations in their occupancy agreement and compliance with funding agency regulations.

nodes.title: RELATIONSHIP OF ADVISER TO MEMBERS
nodes.summary:
This attachment explains the relationship between the adviser and cooperative members. It emphasizes that the adviser’s role is to teach members how to manage and maintain the cooperative so they can gradually take over full responsibility and self-management. The document outlines the knowledge and skills the adviser should be able to teach, including management procedures, cooperative living, working with authority, use of resources, board and committee functions, regulatory documents, and attitudes that promote responsibility, fairness, cooperation, and participation.

It also stresses that members must understand and follow the rules and agreements governing the cooperative, including occupancy agreements and funding agency regulations. The adviser is expected to help members learn through doing, assist with individual and family problems, involve members early in planning and decision-making, communicate regulations in understandable language, encourage member ideas, and interpret government rules and guidelines so they can be properly applied in cooperative operations.

nodes.title: The adviser to the board will:
nodes.summary: The document outlines the adviser’s role and responsibilities in a cooperative setting. It emphasizes that regulations and guidelines must be repeatedly taught and used in major decisions, while the adviser helps members learn through doing, participates early in planning, and includes members in decision-making, including selecting the living unit. The adviser is also expected to communicate regulations in understandable language, encourage members’ ideas within government rules, and support group cohesion. A second section details adviser responsibilities to the board, including serving as a backup manager, helping organize the board and committees, monitoring operations, developing and implementing an educational plan, setting up management and maintenance systems, assisting with non-complex financial issues, and advising on new membership applications.

nodes.title: ATTACHMENT 11 -H
nodes.summary: This attachment outlines the adviser’s responsibilities to the board. The adviser serves as a backup manager while helping members learn self-management and maintenance, supports the organization and regular meetings of the board and committees, and assists in ongoing evaluation and monitoring of operations. The adviser also develops and implements the educational plan, helps establish systems for management records and for maintenance of buildings, grounds, and equipment, provides guidance on financial matters that do not require an outside auditor, and advises on evaluating new membership applicants.

nodes.summary:

The second section, “Subscription Agreement,” is a cooperative housing membership agreement. nodes.title: CHAPTER 12: FARM LABOR HOUSING LOANS AND GRANTS
nodes.summary: The partial document contains only an instruction stating that the response should be a direct description of the document’s main points and should not include any additional text.

nodes.title: 12.1 INTRODUCTION
nodes.summary:
The partial document is a chapter from HB-1-3560 about Farm Labor Housing loans and grants. It introduces the loan and grant origination process for farm labor housing projects, noting that the procedures are similar to Section 515 loans but include special requirements specific to farm labor housing. It explains that the chapter builds on the general procedures in earlier chapters and is divided into two main sections: off-farm labor housing and on-farm labor housing.

The excerpt then begins Section 1, which covers Off-Farm Labor Housing loans and grants. It explains that these projects are intended to expand affordable housing for farm laborers regardless of where they work. Funding may come as loans, grants, or both, and rental assistance contracts may also be available. The section describes how off-farm labor housing is typically developed by nonprofits or public bodies, can be located in either urban or rural areas if need is shown, and receives annual funding allocations announced through a Notice of Funding Availability (NOFA). Applicants submit project applications through their Field Office for funding consideration.

nodes.title: SECTION 1: OFF-FARM LABOR HOUSING LOANS AND GRANTS
nodes.summary: This partial document introduces Chapter 12 of HB-1-3560, which covers Farm Labor Housing loans and grants. It explains that the chapter supplements the general loan origination procedures in earlier chapters by outlining the special requirements for farm labor housing projects. The chapter is divided into two main parts: Section 1 on Off-Farm Labor Housing loans and grants, and Section 2 on On-Farm Labor Housing loans. The excerpt then begins Section 1, describing off-farm labor housing as housing for farm laborers regardless of where they work, which may be financed with loans, grants, or both, and may also include rental assistance and technical assistance grants. It notes that these projects are typically developed by nonprofit organizations or public bodies, can be located in urban or rural areas, and are funded through an annual NOFA process with applications submitted to Field Offices.

nodes.title: 12.2 OVERVIEW
nodes.summary:
This partial document introduces Chapter 12 of HB-1-3560, which covers Farm Labor Housing loans and grants. It explains that the chapter supplements the general loan origination procedures from earlier chapters and focuses on the special rules for farm labor housing. The chapter is divided into two main sections: one for off-farm labor housing loans and grants, and one for on-farm labor housing loans.

The excerpt mainly covers off-farm labor housing. It describes these projects as housing intended to expand affordable housing for farm laborers regardless of where they work, and notes that they may be financed through loans, grants, or both. It also mentions the possibility of technical assistance grants for eligible nonprofit or public entities. Off-farm projects are typically developed by broad-based nonprofits or public bodies and are not limited by the usual Section 515 location restrictions, meaning they may be built in either urban or rural areas if need is justified. Funding is allocated annually through a Notice of Funding Availability (NOFA), and applicants submit proposals through their Field Office for selection and further processing.

The document also defines farm labor for Sections 514 and 516, referencing agricultural and aquacultural work such as cultivating, harvesting, packing, storing, and transporting commodities.

A major portion of the excerpt describes applicant eligibility for off-farm labor housing. Eligible loan applicants include broad-based nonprofit organizations, limited partnerships with nonprofit general partners, nonprofit organizations of farmworkers, federally recognized Indian tribes, and state or local government agencies or subdivisions. For grants, the excerpt begins by stating that applicants must meet additional requirements, including being a broad-based nonprofit organization, but the text cuts off before the full grant eligibility list is completed.

nodes.title: 12.3 APPLICANT ELIGIBILITY FOR OFF -FARM LABOR HOUSING
nodes.summary: The partial document explains eligibility and application requirements for USDA Farm Labor Housing programs under Sections 514 and 516. It defines “farm labor” for these programs, including a wide range of agricultural and aquacultural work such as cultivating, harvesting, packing, storing, and transporting unprocessed commodities. It then outlines who can apply for Farm Labor Housing loans, including broad-based nonprofit organizations, limited partnerships with nonprofit general partners, nonprofit farmworker organizations, federally recognized Indian tribes, and state or local government entities. It also begins describing additional eligibility requirements for Farm Labor Housing grants, starting with broad-based nonprofit organizations.

nodes.title: A. Eligible Applicants for Farm Labor Housing Loans
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines rules for Section 514 and 516 farm labor housing programs, focusing on what qualifies as farm labor and who may apply for off-farm labor housing assistance. It explains that farm labor includes a wide range of activities related to cultivating, harvesting, handling, packing, storing, and transporting agricultural or aquacultural commodities in unprocessed form. The document then describes applicant eligibility for Farm Labor Housing Loans and Grants, listing eligible entities such as broad-based nonprofit organizations, limited partnerships with nonprofit general partners, nonprofit farmworker organizations, Federally recognized Indian tribes, and state or local government agencies. It also notes that applicants must satisfy additional requirements from the relevant regulations and handbook sections.

nodes.title: B. Eligible Applicants for Farm Labor Housing Grants
nodes.summary: This section explains the eligibility and funding rules for USDA Farm Labor Housing programs under Sections 514 and 516. It defines farm labor for these programs and then lists who may apply for farm labor housing loans and grants, including broad-based nonprofits, nonprofit farmworker organizations, federally recognized Indian tribes, and state or local government entities. It also describes the maximum grant amount, which is generally capped at 90 percent of total development cost, and notes that applicants must show a documented need for the project. Finally, it covers technical assistance grants, which may be awarded to eligible nonprofit agencies to help other organizations secure housing loans and grants, with restrictions on duplicate funding and conflicts of interest.

nodes.title: 1. Maximum Grant Amount
nodes.summary: The document section describes eligibility and requirements for farm labor housing grants and technical assistance grants. It lists types of eligible organizations, including nonprofits with appropriate board representation, nonprofit farmworker organizations, federally recognized Indian tribes, and state or local government agencies. It explains the maximum grant amount, generally limited to 90% of total development cost or the amount exceeding other available funds, and notes that any remaining financing must be in place at loan closing. It also requires applicants to document a pressing local housing need and demonstrate that the project likely cannot be provided without a grant. Finally, it covers technical assistance grants, stating that the Agency may fund eligible nonprofit agencies to help others secure housing loans and grants, but not when duplicative funding rules apply or when an identity-of-interest exists between the provider and applicant.

nodes.title: 2. Documented Need for the Project
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses eligibility and requirements for farm labor housing grants and technical assistance grants. It explains which entities may qualify, including nonprofit organizations, nonprofit farmworker organizations with required board representation, federally recognized Indian tribes, and state or local government agencies. It outlines the maximum grant amount, limiting it to 90 percent of total development cost or the portion exceeding the applicant’s own resources and approved loans, and notes that any remaining funds must be available at loan closing. It also states that applicants must document a pressing local need for the housing and show it likely cannot be provided without the grant. Finally, it describes technical assistance grants, including who may receive them, their purpose in helping other organizations secure housing financing, and restrictions on duplicate funding and conflicts of interest.

nodes.title: 12.4 TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE GRANTS
nodes.summary:
The partial document outlines requirements and rules for Off-Farm Labor Housing grants and project eligibility. It covers who may qualify as an applicant, including nonprofit farmworker organizations, federally recognized Indian tribes, and state or local government agencies. It explains the maximum grant amount, which is capped at 90% of total development cost or the portion beyond the applicant’s own resources and approved loans, and notes that remaining financing must be secured at loan closing. The document also requires applicants to show a documented need for the housing and that the project would not likely be built without the grant.

In addition, the text describes Technical Assistance Grants, which may be awarded to eligible nonprofit agencies to help others obtain loans and grants for farm labor housing, with restrictions on duplicate funding and conflicts of interest. Finally, it details project eligibility requirements, especially site and design standards, including when projects will not be funded due to market-area conditions such as existing similar projects, vacancy issues, or unmet need for subsidy rather than new housing. It also highlights design considerations like interior and exterior washing facilities for farmworkers, based on the type of labor performed in the area.

nodes.title: 12.5 PROJECT ELIGIBILITY
nodes.summary:
The partial document explains the project eligibility requirements for Off-Farm Labor Housing under HB-1-3560. It covers two main areas: site requirements and design requirements.

Under site requirements, projects must comply with 7 CFR 3560.558, except they do not need to be in a designated place, since farm labor housing can be located in both urban and rural areas. The document also lists situations where applications will not be funded, such as when another similar RHS project is already being processed in the same market area, when a previous assisted project in that area is unfinished or under-occupied, when a Special Note Rent or similar tool is already in place, or when the need is for more rental assistance rather than more housing units.

Under design requirements, applications must comply with 7 CFR 3560.559. The section specifically discusses interior and exterior washing facilities, recommending them to protect tenants and the property from dirt and chemical exposure. Examples include boot washing stations, hose bibs, or accessible bathrooms near the dwelling entrance. Applicants must document the types of work tenant laborers will perform and explain how the proposed washing facilities will meet the project’s needs.

nodes.title: A. Site Requirements [7 CFR 3560.558]
nodes.summary: The partial document explains the project eligibility requirements for Off-Farm Labor Housing loans and grants under HB-1-3560. It outlines two main areas: site requirements and design requirements. Site requirements reference 7 CFR 3560.558 and note that projects may be in urban or rural areas, but applications may be rejected if another similar project is already being processed in the same market area, if an existing assisted project is incomplete or has high vacancy, if a Special Note Rent or similar tool is pending, or if the market need is for rental assistance rather than new housing units. Design requirements reference 7 CFR 3560.559 and include guidance on incorporating interior and exterior washing facilities, such as boot washing stations or hose bibs, to reduce dirt and chemical exposure for tenants. Applicants must also document the type of farm labor performed and explain how proposed washing facilities meet the project’s needs.

nodes.title: B. Design Requirements [7 CFR 3560.559]
nodes.summary:
The partial document explains the eligibility requirements for Off-Farm Labor Housing project applications under HB-1-3560. It covers two main areas: site requirements and design requirements. 

For site requirements, projects must follow 7 CFR 3560.558, except they do not have to be in a designated place, since farm labor housing may be located in both urban and rural areas. It also lists conditions under which applications will not be considered for funding, such as when another similar project is already being processed in the same market area, when an existing assisted project in the area is not completed or is underused, when a Special Note Rent or similar tool is pending or active, or when the market need is for rental assistance rather than new housing units.

For design requirements, applications must comply with 7 CFR 3560.559. A specific design consideration discussed is the inclusion of interior and exterior washing facilities to protect tenants and the housing asset from dirt and chemical exposure. Examples include boot-washing stations, hose bibs, or accessible bathroom facilities. Applicants are expected to document the type of farmwork tenants will perform and explain what washing facilities are needed for the project.

nodes.title: 1. Interior/Exterior Washing Facilities
nodes.summary:
This section explains the eligibility requirements for Off-Farm Labor Housing projects under HB-1-3560. It covers both site and design standards that must be met for a project application to qualify for a loan or grant. 

The site requirements reference 7 CFR 3560.558 and note that Off-Farm Labor Housing may be developed in both urban and rural areas, unlike the usual designated-place requirement. It also lists situations where funding will not be considered, such as when another similar project is already being processed in the same market area, when an approved or assisted project is incomplete or has high vacancies, when a Special Note Rent or similar tool is pending or active, or when the market need is for rental assistance rather than new housing units.

The design requirements reference 7 CFR 3560.559 and state that applications must comply with established design standards. A specific design topic discussed is interior and exterior washing facilities, which should be included as needed to protect both the property and tenants from dirt and chemical exposure. Examples include boot washing stations, hose bibs, or bathroom access near the entrance. Applicants must document the type of farm work tenants will do and explain how proposed washing facilities will meet project needs.

nodes.title: 2. Seasonal Labor Housing

It then outlines eligible loan and grant fund uses, including reimbursement for technical assistance, limited initial operating expenses, tenant-related facilities, and seasonal or temporary-use facilities, along with prohibited uses of funds. Finally, it notes that initial applications for off-farm labor housing must meet the same basic submission requirements as Section 515 project applications.

Finally, it states that initial applications for off-farm labor housing must meet the same basic submission requirements as Section 515 project applications.

nodes.title: B. Completeness Review
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines the process for reviewing and selecting Farm Labor Housing loan and grant applications. It also explains the national ranking and selection process, including how eligible applications are forwarded to the National Office, how national deadlines are set, and how top-scoring applications are funded. nodes.title: D. Scoring and Ranking of Initial Applications
nodes.summary: The passage describes the process for reviewing and selecting Farm Labor Housing loan and grant applications. nodes.title: E. National Ranking and Selection of Initial Applications

nodes.title: 1. Ranking of Initial Applications on the National Level
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines the review, scoring, ranking, and selection process for Farm Labor Housing loan and grant applications. It covers how loan originators must first check applications for completeness and eligibility, including compliance with program rules and noting that designated place requirements do not apply to farm labor housing. It then explains that State Offices score and rank initial applications according to NOFA criteria, with a Loan Review Committee reviewing the results before submission. Finally, it describes the national-level ranking and selection process, where eligible applications are forwarded to the National Office, ranked nationally, and funded in order of score, with unused funds reassigned to the next highest-ranked application if an awardee does not proceed.

nodes.title: 2. Selection of Initial Applications by the National Office
nodes.summary:
The partial document outlines the application review and selection process for Farm Labor Housing and Off-Farm Labor Housing programs. It covers:

- **Completeness review** of initial applications by loan originators.
- **Eligibility review** to ensure applications meet program requirements, including noting that designated place requirements do not apply to farm labor housing.
- **Scoring and ranking** of applications at the State Office level, with Loan Review Committee oversight before submission to the National Office.
- **National ranking and selection** procedures, including how applications are ranked nationally, forwarded for funding consideration, and how unused funds may be reassigned if a selected applicant does not proceed.
- **Notification of selection outcomes**, including the types of letters applicants may receive:
  - selected for further processing,
  - not funded due to lack of funds,
  - denied due to ineligibility.
- **Further processing of initial applications**, with a focus on **project eligibility** requirements for Off-Farm Labor Housing, including:
  - no rural location or designated places requirement,
  - ensuring exterior washing facilities are included in the budget,
  - confirming whether the housing is year-round or seasonal and what construction standards apply.

nodes.title: F. Notification of Initial Applications Selection
nodes.summary: The partial document explains the process for notifying applicants after the national selection of initial applications and the next steps for further processing. It outlines three possible agency letters: one for applicants selected for further review, one for applicants not funded due to lack of funds, and one for applicants denied because they were ineligible, with ECOA notice requirements for certain letters. It then describes further processing requirements for Off-Farm Labor Housing initial applications, noting that standard procedures apply with some special rules. The eligibility review points include confirming that projects do not need to be in rural locations, checking that exterior washing facilities are included in the budget, and ensuring the application addresses whether housing will be year-round or seasonal and what construction standards will be used.

nodes.title: 1. Handbook Letter 106 (3560), Notice of Pre -application Review Action
nodes.summary:
The partial document explains the notification process for initial application selection and the next steps for further processing in the Off-Farm Labor Housing program. It describes how the National Office informs State Offices of selected applications, how applicants are notified, and the three possible letter types they may receive: selection for further processing, lack of funds, or denial due to ineligibility. It also notes that any non-invitation letter must include the standard ECOA notice.

The document then outlines special procedures for reviewing initial Off-Farm Labor Housing applications, emphasizing that while general further-processing rules from Chapters 4 and 5 apply, some requirements differ. In particular, it lists project eligibility factors Loan Originators must check, including that the project does not need to be in a rural area or designated place, that exterior washing facilities are included in the budget, and that the application addresses whether housing will be year-round or seasonal and the standards to which it will be built.

nodes.title: 2. Handbook Letter 107 (3560), A Letter Informing the Applicant of the Lack of Funds
nodes.summary: The partial document explains the notification process for initial application selections and the next steps for further processing in the Off-Farm Labor Housing program. It states that the National Office informs State Offices of selected applications, and State Offices then notify applicants and invite finalists to submit a final application. It outlines three possible agency letters applicants may receive: one for selection for further processing, one for lack of funds with possible later funding if space opens, and one for denial due to ineligibility. The document also notes that any non-invitation letter must include the standard ECOA notice. In the section on further processing, it emphasizes that Off-Farm Labor Housing applications are reviewed under general Chapter 4 and 5 procedures but with special project eligibility checks, including whether the project must be in a rural area, whether exterior washing facilities are properly budgeted, and whether the application addresses seasonal versus year-round use and construction standards.

nodes.title: 3. Handbook Letter 108 (3560), Letter Denying Funding due to Ineligibility
nodes.summary: The document explains the notification process for initial application selections and the types of letters applicants may receive after national review.
