nodes.summary: The partial document explains how loan limits are determined for a housing project under HB-1-3560, including how the Agency calculates the maximum loan amount based on the lesser of total development cost or appraised value minus other loans. It outlines different loan percentage limits and required applicant contributions depending on the applicant type, such as individuals, partnerships, nonprofits, and public bodies, and notes rules for initial operating capital and capitalized construction interest. The document also describes acceptable forms of equity contribution, mainly land or cash, and explains conditions for using land value and when donated land cannot be counted as equity. Finally, it covers subsidy review procedures, requiring applicants to report changes in financing sources and uses, rerun feasibility analysis if needed, and reduce government assistance if funding exceeds project costs by more than $25,000.

nodes.title: Land or Cash Equity Contribution
nodes.summary: The partial document explains several loan and project financing rules under HB-1-3560. It first notes how interest is handled for PASS loans with multiple advances when closing occurs on a date other than the first of the month. It then describes rules for borrower equity contributions, stating that equity may be provided only as land or cash, how excess land value is treated, and when a return to the owner may be allowed without exceeding project security value or rent limits. It also clarifies that donated land cannot count as equity. Finally, the document covers subsidy review procedures, requiring the applicant to disclose changes in project sources and uses, have the Loan Originator rerun the SAUCE program to ensure government assistance is not excessive, and reduce assistance if sources exceed uses by more than $25,000, with proper notifications to relevant agencies and financing parties.

nodes.title: Subsidy Review
nodes.summary:
The document section explains several key requirements for Rural Development housing loans. It covers how PASS loans with multiple advances handle interest capitalization when closing dates are not the first of the month. It describes rules for borrower equity contributions, allowing only land or cash, how excess land value may or may not receive a return to owner, and how donated land is treated. It also explains how cash contributions and other leveraged funds are disbursed and tracked.

The section then outlines subsidy review procedures, requiring applicants to report changes in financing sources and uses, rerun the SAUCE program if needed, and reduce government assistance if funding exceeds project needs by more than $25,000. It lists possible ways to reduce excess assistance, such as reducing LIHTCs, other funding sources, project uses, or loan equity contribution.

Finally, it covers setting loan rates and terms, including using the lower of the interest rate at approval or closing, applying interest credit to reduce the borrower’s payment rate to one percent, and setting loan amortization and repayment periods. It ends with rules for profit base and return on initial investment, allowing limited-profit applicants a return of up to 8 percent on their required initial investment.

nodes.title: SETTING LOAN RATES AND TERMS [7 CFR 3560.67]
nodes.summary: The document excerpt covers options for adjusting project financing when needed, including reducing requested LIHTCs, lowering other funding sources, revising eligible uses, or reducing equity contribution as a last resort. It then explains how loan rates and terms are set under USDA Rural Development rules: loans close at the lower of the approval or closing interest rate, interest credit reduces the effective payment rate to 1 percent, and loan amortization/repayment periods are specified for initial and subsequent repair or rehabilitation loans. Finally, it introduces rules for establishing the profit base and return on initial investment for limited-profit applicants, allowing a return of up to 8 percent on required initial investment.

nodes.title: Interest
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses procedures and rules for Rural Development multifamily housing loans, focusing on how project financing may be adjusted when needed, including reducing LIHTCs, lowering other funding sources, revising eligible costs, or reducing the loan amount. It then explains how loan rates and terms are set: loans close at the lower of the approved or closing interest rate, interest credit reduces the payment rate to one percent, and loan amortization and repayment periods are defined for initial and subsequent loans. Finally, it covers the profit base and return on initial investment, stating that limited-profit applicants may receive a return of up to 8 percent on their required initial investment.

nodes.title: Interest Credit
nodes.summary: The document excerpt covers options for adjusting project financing when needed, including reducing requested LIHTCs, lowering other funding sources, revising eligible uses, or reducing the equity contribution as a last resort. It then explains how Rural Development sets loan rates and terms under 7 CFR 3560.67, including that loans are closed at the lower of the rate at approval or closing, interest credit reduces the payment rate to 1 percent, and loan amortization/terms vary by loan type and project type. Finally, it addresses establishing profit base and return on initial investment under 7 CFR 3560.68, stating that limited-profit applicants may receive up to an 8 percent return on their initial required investment.

nodes.title: Loan Term and Amortization Period
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses how loan terms are set for Rural Development housing projects and how profit/return calculations are determined. It explains options for adjusting funding when project costs change, including reducing LIHTCs, lowering other funding sources, revising eligible costs, or reducing the loan amount as a last resort. It then covers setting loan interest rates and terms: loans use the lower of the approved rate or the closing rate, interest credit reduces payments to 1 percent, and loan amortization and repayment periods are specified for initial and subsequent loans. Finally, it outlines the profit base and return on initial investment, stating that limited-profit applicants may receive a return of up to 8 percent on their required equity contribution.

nodes.title: ESTABLISHING PROFIT BASE AND RETURN ON INITIAL INVESTMENT [7 CFR 3560.68]
nodes.summary: The partial document covers several Rural Development multifamily housing loan and financing requirements. It first lists possible adjustments when project funding sources need to be reduced or revised, such as lowering requested LIHTCs, reducing assistance from other funding sources, revising eligible costs, or decreasing the loan amount as a last resort. It then explains how loan rates and terms are set, including using the lower of the approved or closing interest rate, applying interest credit to reduce payments to 1 percent, and defining loan amortization and repayment periods for initial and subsequent loans. Finally, it introduces rules for establishing the profit base and return on initial investment, stating that limited-profit applicants may receive up to an 8 percent return on their required equity investment.

nodes.title: Return on Required Investment
nodes.summary: The partial document explains how Rural Development multifamily housing loans are adjusted and structured, focusing on ways to resolve financing gaps, set loan rates and terms, and determine permissible returns on investment. It covers options for reducing funding shortfalls, including lowering LIHTC requests, reducing other funding sources, revising eligible project uses, or decreasing equity contribution through a smaller loan amount. It then outlines loan rate rules, interest credit, amortization, and loan term requirements for initial and repair/rehabilitation loans. Finally, it describes how the profit base and return on initial investment are established for limited-profit applicants, including limits on returns for required and additional investments, treatment of tax credit proceeds, and valuation rules for contributed building sites.

nodes.title: Return on Additional Investment
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses how returns to the owner are treated in project budgeting and financing, especially under USDA/HB-1-3560 guidance. It explains that return to owner is recorded as an operational cash use on the budget form and must be approved during the budget process. It then covers when a return is allowed on the applicant’s additional investment, including limits tied to project security value and rent restrictions under CRCU. The document also addresses how LIHTC syndication proceeds may count as the applicant’s own resources under certain conditions, excluding allowable developer fees and expected required contributions. Finally, it explains valuation and return rules for building sites contributed toward equity, including agency appraisal, limits on returns when rent must remain within CRCU, and the requirement for additional funding if the appraised value is lower than expected.

nodes.title: Tax Credit Proceeds
nodes.summary: The partial document explains rules for allowing a return to the owner in project budgeting and identifies several cases where a return on the applicant’s own investment may be permitted. It covers: how return to owner is treated as an operational cash use on the budget and must be approved by the Loan Originator; conditions for a return on additional investment, including limits based on project security value and rent restrictions under CRCU; how proceeds from LIHTC syndication may count as the applicant’s own resources, subject to exclusions for allowable developer fees and expected contributions; and how contributed building sites are appraised, when a return may be allowed on excess value, and what happens if the appraised value is lower than expected and does not satisfy the required equity contribution.

nodes.title: Building Sites
nodes.summary: The partial document explains USDA Rural Development loan program procedures for return on owner investment, tax credit proceeds, and contributed building sites, including appraisal and rent restrictions tied to CRCU. It then outlines final application processing, covering how to reject disapproved loans, notify applicants of appeal rights and ECOA protections, and the steps required to recommend projects for final approval, such as completing environmental review, obtaining Loan Review Committee input, and ensuring management and occupancy procedures comply with civil rights laws.

nodes.title: REJECTING FINAL APPLICATIONS
nodes.summary: The partial document describes procedures for rejecting and approving final loan applications in USDA multifamily housing programs. It explains the steps the Loan Originator must take when a loan is disapproved after docket development, including updating the AMAS system, handling the docket under RD Instruction 2033-A, notifying the applicant of the reasons for rejection, and informing them of appeal rights when applicable. It also includes required ECOA nondiscrimination language for adverse action notices. In addition, the document outlines the preparation steps for recommending a project for final approval, such as completing environmental review, obtaining Loan Review Committee input if needed, and reviewing management and occupancy procedures for civil rights compliance.

nodes.title: RECOMMENDING PROJECTS FOR FINAL APPROVAL
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines procedures for rejecting or approving final applications in the USDA multi-family housing loan process. It explains that if a loan is disapproved after a docket is developed, the Loan Originator must update the system status, handle the docket according to RD Instruction 2033-A, and notify the applicant of the rejection and appeal rights, including required ECOA nondiscrimination language. It also describes the steps for recommending projects for final approval, such as completing environmental review, obtaining Loan Review Committee input when needed, and reviewing management and occupancy procedures for compliance with civil rights laws.

nodes.title: Preparing the Recommendation for Approval
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines procedures for final application decisions in the USDA multifamily housing loan process. It covers how to reject a final application after a docket has been developed, including updating the AMAS system, handling the docket under RD Instruction 2033-A, and notifying the applicant of the rejection and appeal rights, along with the required ECOA notice language. It also explains the steps for recommending a project for final approval, such as completing environmental review requirements, obtaining Loan Review Committee input when needed, and reviewing management and occupancy procedures for compliance with civil rights laws.

nodes.title: Submitting the Completed Docket
nodes.summary: The partial document describes the process for submitting a completed loan docket and the responsibilities of the Loan Approval Official in reviewing and approving the loan. It explains that the Loan Originator submits the docket with a cover memo recommending approval, outlining proposed loan conditions and whether construction financing will use interim financing or multiple advances. It then details the review criteria MFH staff must verify, including applicant eligibility, legal authority, location compliance, authorized use of funds, soundness of the loan, leveraged funds, adequate security, completion of required preapproval documents, environmental compliance, and flood insurance requirements if applicable. Finally, it outlines the loan approval step, where the Agency must complete Form RD 3560-51, have it signed and dated by the applicant, and then execute and return a copy to the applicant.

nodes.title: ACTION BY THE LOAN APPROVAL OFFICIAL
nodes.summary: The partial document explains the process for submitting a completed loan docket and the actions required by the Loan Approval Official. It states that the Loan Originator submits the docket with a cover memo recommending approval, outlining loan conditions and whether construction will be financed through interim financing or multiple advances. It then describes how MFH staff review the docket to ensure the loan complies with policies and regulations, checking applicant eligibility, legal authority, property location, authorized use of funds, loan soundness, leveraged funds, adequate security, completed preapproval documents, environmental compliance, and flood insurance requirements if applicable. Finally, it explains the approval step, including completion of Form RD 3560-51, Obligation Fund Analysis, for applicant signature before the Agency executes and returns a copy.

nodes.title: Reviewing of Docket by Loan Approval Official
nodes.summary: This section explains the process for submitting a completed loan docket and the responsibilities of the Loan Approval Official. It states that the Loan Originator must send the docket with a recommendation for approval and a cover memo describing proposed loan conditions and financing method. It then outlines the review criteria used by MFH staff to verify that the applicant is eligible, the project location and purpose meet program rules, the loan is sound, committed funds and security are adequate, required documents are complete, and environmental and flood insurance requirements are satisfied. Finally, it describes the loan approval step, including completion, signature, and delivery of Form RD 3560-51, Obligation Fund Analysis, before the Agency executes and returns a copy to the applicant.

nodes.title: Approving Loans
nodes.summary: The partial document covers the loan approval and obligation process for Rural Development multifamily housing loans. It explains how the Loan Originator submits the completed docket with a recommendation for approval and how the Loan Approval Official reviews the application for compliance with eligibility, location, purpose, security, environmental, flood insurance, and documentation requirements. It also describes the final approval steps, including execution of Form RD 3560-51 and notification to the interim lender. Finally, it outlines how loan funds are obligated in AMAS, and what actions are required if the loan amount must be reduced before closing, including deobligating excess funds and updating related forms and records.

nodes.title: LETTER TO INTERIM LENDER
nodes.summary: The partial document covers loan approval and fund obligation procedures under USDA Rural Development rules. It explains that loans are approved according to 7 CFR part 3560 and RD Instruction 1901-A, and that once approved, the Loan Originator sends a letter to the interim lender informing them of the approved loan amount and terms. It then outlines how loan funds are obligated after approval, including completing Form RD 3560-51 and entering the obligation in AMAS using screen [M1A] with the correct amortization months. The document also describes how to handle a decrease in the loan amount before closing, such as when financing changes from interim financing to multiple advances, requiring excess funds to be deobligated, records corrected, the National Office notified, and related forms and AMAS entries updated using screens [M1AA] and [M1D].

nodes.title: OBLIGATING FUNDS
nodes.summary: This section explains the loan approval and fund obligation process under HB-1-3560. It states that loans must be approved according to 7 CFR part 3560, subpart B and RD Instruction 1901-A. After approval, the Loan Originator sends a letter to the interim lender notifying them of the loan amount and terms. The section then outlines how loan funds are obligated in AMAS, including completing Form RD 3560-51 and entering the obligation on screen [M1A]. It also covers how to reduce a loan amount before closing if needed, such as when interim financing changes to multiple advances, requiring deobligation of excess funds, correction of related forms and records, notification to the National Office, and updates through AMAS screens [M1AA] and [M1D].

nodes.title: Initial Obligation
nodes.summary: The partial document explains the procedures for approving and closing loans under Rural Development rules, with emphasis on notifying interim lenders and obligating loan funds after approval. It outlines the required steps to initially obligate funds in the system and on Form RD 3560-51, and notes that the loan amortization period must be entered correctly in AMAS. The document also covers how to reduce a loan obligation before closing, including deobligating excess funds, correcting application and AMAS records, notifying the National Office if redistribution is possible, and updating related loan forms and agreements.

nodes.title: Changing the Loan Amount
nodes.summary: This section covers the loan approval and funding obligation process for Rural Development loans. It explains that loans are approved under 7 CFR part 3560 and RD Instruction 1901-A, and that after approval the Loan Originator sends a letter to the interim lender describing the loan amount and terms. It then outlines how loan funds must be obligated, including completing Form RD 3560-51 and entering the obligation in AMAS. The document also describes how to handle reductions in the obligated loan amount before closing, such as when financing terms change, including deobligating excess funds, correcting related forms and records, updating AMAS screens, and notifying the National Office if funds may be redistributed.

nodes.title: Decreasing the Loan Amount
nodes.summary: The partial document explains Rural Development loan approval and fund management procedures. It covers notifying an interim lender after approval, obligating loan funds in AMAS and on required forms, and how to handle changes to the obligated loan amount by either deobligating excess funds or, rarely, increasing the loan with National Office approval. It also describes ordering loan checks, including special handling for multiple advances and interim financing, and emphasizes monitoring for accrued interest and debt limits. Finally, it outlines follow-up responsibilities to ensure obligated funds are used promptly, including periodic project checks and required action if construction does not begin or falls significantly behind schedule within 12 months.

nodes.title: Increasing the Loan Amount
nodes.summary: The partial document covers procedures for managing and monitoring USDA Rural Development loan obligations. It explains when and how a loan amount may be increased, noting that increases are rare and require justification, fiscal-year considerations, and approval from the National Office or use of a subsequent loan. It also describes ordering loan checks, including the expected processing time, how multiple advances are handled in AMAS, the importance of tracking accrued interest and deobligating unused funds, and the screen functions used for these tasks. In addition, it addresses interim financing, requiring a single check to pay off the interim financier. Finally, it discusses follow-up on obligations, emphasizing periodic review of construction progress and requiring documentation and action if a project has not started or is significantly behind schedule after 12 months.

nodes.title: Ordering Checks
nodes.summary: The partial document covers procedures related to Rural Housing loan administration. It explains when and how the Agency may increase a loan amount, including the need for borrower justification, National Office approval, and the difference between increases within the same fiscal year versus a subsequent loan in a later fiscal year. It then describes check ordering for loan disbursements, noting the expected processing time, the use of AMAS for multiple advances, and the importance of monitoring accrued interest, debt limits, and deobligating unused funds before the final draw. It also mentions interim financing, which requires a single check to pay off the interim lender. Finally, the document begins a section on follow-up to obligations, stating that staff must periodically verify construction progress and document action if a project has not started or is significantly delayed after 12 months.

nodes.title: FOLLOW-UP TO OBLIGATIONS
nodes.summary:
The partial document discusses procedures and conditions for managing Multi-Family Housing loan obligations. It explains when and how a loan amount may be increased, including the need for National Office approval and use of a subsequent loan if the need arises in a later fiscal year. It also covers ordering loan checks, especially for multiple advances and interim financing, emphasizing timing, monitoring disbursements, accrued interest, and use of specific AMAS screens.

The document then outlines follow-up requirements for obligated funds, stating that staff must periodically verify construction progress and take action if a project has not started or is significantly delayed after 12 months. Possible actions include canceling and deobligating funds or allowing an additional 12 months for progress. Finally, it describes when obligation transfers are permitted, such as when the organizational entity stays the same, the entity changes but ownership interests remain identical, or there is a monetary default. It also lists conditions that must be met for such transfers, including lien removal, adherence to approved plans, agency approval, and future ineligibility of the original parties for a period of at least five years.

nodes.title: TRANSFER OF FUND OBLIGATION
nodes.summary: The partial document covers procedures for managing Multifamily Housing loan obligations. It explains when an obligation may be transferred, including cases where the organizational entity stays the same, the legal entity changes, or there is a monetary default by the original applicant. It also outlines when and how an obligation can be canceled before closing, including required notifications, deobligation steps in AMAS, completion of the appropriate form, and voiding any issued check if needed. Finally, it describes the Letter of Conditions that is sent after loan approval, which sets out the loan terms, program requirements, construction conditions, and other items the applicant must satisfy before closing.

nodes.title: CANCELING AN OBLIGATION
nodes.summary:
The partial document covers two main topics related to loan processing in HB-1-3560:

1. **Canceling an Obligation**
   - Explains when and why a loan obligation may be canceled before closing, such as when the borrower withdraws or new disqualifying information is received.
   - Outlines the Loan Originator’s responsibilities: notifying the applicant and closing agent, informing the borrower about correction or appeal options if applicable, notifying the National Office, deobligating the loan in AMAS, completing the required cancellation form, and voiding any check if necessary.
   - States that the cancellation decision must be documented in the running record or project case file.

2. **Letter of Conditions**
   - Describes the requirement for the Loan Approval Official to send the applicant a Letter of Conditions after loan approval.
   - Explains that the letter outlines the approved loan terms, program requirements, construction requirements, and any conditions that must be met before closing.
   - Notes that the applicant must sign and return the letter within 5 days, and that a copy must be sent to the Loan Originator.
   - Mentions that the specific contents of the letter are listed in a referenced exhibit.

nodes.title: LETTER OF CONDITIONS
nodes.summary:
The partial document covers two main topics in the loan approval and closing process for USDA multifamily housing:

1. **Canceling an Obligation**
   - Explains when and why a loan obligation may be canceled before closing, such as borrower withdrawal or new disqualifying information.
   - Outlines the Loan Originator’s responsibilities: notifying the applicant and closing agent, informing the borrower about possible correction or appeal options when applicable, notifying the National Office, deobligating the loan in AMAS, completing the required cancellation form, and voiding and returning the check if one has already been issued.
   - Emphasizes that the cancellation decision must be documented in the running record or project case file.

2. **Letter of Conditions**
   - Describes the required letter sent after loan approval, which sets out the terms and conditions that must be met before loan closing.
   - States that the letter must be signed and returned by the applicant within 5 days, and a copy must be sent to the Loan Originator.
   - Lists the information that must be included in the letter, such as loan terms, equity contribution, operating capital requirement, cost overrun responsibility, security requirements, environmental mitigation measures, allowed rents, rental assistance, and any management changes.
   - Summarizes key program requirements that must be referenced, including tenant eligibility, accounting, financial examination, reporting, civil rights compliance, insurance, and other obligations like recordkeeping and Agency access.
   - Includes required civil rights covenant language for the deed or conveyance instrument.
   - Identifies construction and development requirements such as public bidding, construction accounting, and cost certification.
   - Provides space for the applicant’s signature and return address to accept the letter of conditions.

nodes.title: INSURANCE REQUIREMENTS
nodes.summary: The partial document covers two main topics from HB-1-3560. First, it explains insurance requirements that loan applicants must have in place at loan closing, including fire and extended coverage, worker’s compensation, flood insurance in special flood or mudslide-prone areas, and liability insurance. Second, it describes how case numbers, project numbers, and loan codes are assigned, including the use of state and county codes, tax identification numbers or temporary numbers, AMAS-assigned project numbers, and how initial loans are coded when all units are financed at one site and time.

nodes.title: ASSIGNING CASE NUMBERS, PROJECT NUMBERS, AND LOAN CODES
nodes.summary:
The partial document outlines two main topics from HB-1-3560:

1. **Insurance requirements at loan closing**  
   It explains that loan applicants must have required insurance in place before closing and be notified of these requirements during loan approval. The minimum required coverages include:
   - Fire and extended coverage for all buildings securing the loan
   - Workers’ compensation insurance for all employees
   - Flood insurance for buildings in special flood or mudslide-prone areas
   - Liability insurance  
   It also notes that Chapter 3 of HB-2-3560 provides more detail.

2. **Assignment of case numbers, project numbers, and loan codes**  
   This section describes how the Loan Approval Official assigns a borrower’s case number using state and county codes plus the borrower’s tax identification number, with temporary numbers used if no tax ID exists. It explains that temporary numbers must later be replaced when a tax ID is obtained. It also states that AMAS assigns project numbers, and that loans for all units built or purchased at one location at one time are coded as the initial loan on the project.

The document then begins **Attachment 5-A, Agency Docket Checklist**, which lists required forms and documents for the application docket, along with who is responsible for each item and its docket position.

nodes.title: ATTACHMENT 5 -A
nodes.summary: The partial document only contains an instruction requesting a direct description of the document’s main points, but it does not include any substantive content to summarize.

nodes.title: Agency Docket Checklist
nodes.summary:
The partial document is from USDA Rural Development’s HB-1-3560 guidance and covers two main sections:

1. Agency Docket Checklist (Attachment 5-A)
   - A detailed checklist of documents required for a multifamily housing loan/application docket.
   - It lists forms and supporting materials that must be provided by either the agency or the applicant, along with docket position numbers and spaces for dates due/received.
   - Items include financing and certification forms, environmental and flood-related documents, organizational documents, market feasibility materials, appraisal reports, title insurance, management plans, deeds, leases, and ownership records.
   - Notes clarify that some documents apply only when the applicant is an organization or when a contractor copy is needed.

2. Loan Calculation Worksheets (Attachment 5-B)
   - A worksheet used to calculate loan amounts for housing projects.
   - It explains how to determine Total Development Cost, Security Value, Adjusted Total Development Cost, and Loan Basis Value.
   - It then shows how to compute the Maximum Loan, Applicant Contribution, and Return on Investment.
   - The calculations differentiate between LIHTC and non-LIHTC proposals and exclude certain proposal types such as donated land, applicant-owned sites, or 102 percent loans.

nodes.title: ATTACHMENT 5 -B
nodes.summary:
This document is a loan calculation worksheet attachment used for Rural Development housing loans, specifically for proposals that are not using donated land, applicant-owned sites, or 102 percent loans. It outlines how to compute key financing figures for a housing project, including Total Development Cost (TDC), Security Value (SV), Adjusted Total Development Cost (ATDC), and Loan Basis Value (LBV).

The worksheet provides step-by-step formulas to determine the maximum loan amount, applicant contribution, and return on investment for both LIHTC and non-LIHTC proposals. It references specific forms and line items used in the calculations and is intended to be completed by a Rural Development employee.

nodes.title: Loan Calculation Worksheets
nodes.summary:
The partial document is a set of Rural Development loan calculation worksheets and examples for housing projects, followed by the start of Chapter 6 on participation with other funding sources.

Main points covered:
- How to calculate loan amounts using key values such as Total Development Cost (TDC), Security Value (SV), Adjusted Total Development Cost (ATDC), and Loan Basis Value (LBV).
- Different worksheet formats for:
  - standard projects,
  - donated land,
  - applicant-owned sites,
  - and 102 percent loans.
- Special handling for LIHTC projects versus non-LIHTC projects, including different loan percentages.
- Step-by-step examples showing how maximum loan amount, applicant contribution, and return on investment are computed under different site and appraisal scenarios.
- Guidance on how donated land or applicant-owned sites affect the loan calculation, including when the site value may count toward the applicant’s required contribution.
- Examples where the site value is less than, equal to, or greater than the required applicant contribution, including cases where the loan amount must be reduced.
- Introduction to participation loans in Chapter 6, explaining that the Agency encourages loans with multiple funding sources to stretch resources and support more affordable housing.
- The chapter notes underwriting concerns in participation loans, such as protecting the Agency’s security interest, coordinating with other funding programs, and ensuring no more subsidy is provided than necessary.
- It also mentions coordination with LIHTC allocators and State housing finance agencies.

nodes.title: INTRODUCTION
nodes.summary: The partial document introduces Chapter 6 on participation with other funding sources, focusing on participation loans. It explains that the Agency encourages combining its financing with other lenders or grantors to maximize the number of affordable housing units and stretch limited resources. The section notes that loans leveraging outside funding get priority in project selection. It also explains that participation loans require special processing and underwriting to protect the Agency’s security, manage differing program requirements, and avoid providing excessive subsidy. The chapter outlines the conditions for making participation loans, how security interests and loan limits are set, and procedures for coordinating with the State housing finance agency on low-income housing tax credits. It further states that participation loans benefit both the Agency and borrowers, and that if Agency rental assistance is involved, the Agency’s loan participation generally must be at least 25 percent of total development cost unless waived, while rents must not exceed what they would be under full Agency financing.

nodes.title: OVERVIEW OF PARTICIPATION LOANS
nodes.summary:
This partial document introduces Chapter 6 on participation loans, explaining that the Agency encourages using loans with one or more additional funding sources to maximize the number of affordable housing units. It notes that such loans require special processing and underwriting to protect the Agency’s security, manage differing program requirements, and avoid providing more subsidy than needed.

The section also summarizes the benefits of participation loans for both the Agency and borrowers, including stretching limited resources and increasing housing assistance. It explains that the Agency may participate with loan funds only or with both rental assistance and loan funds. If rental assistance is included, the Agency’s participation must generally be at least 25% of total development cost unless an exception is approved, and rents cannot exceed levels that would apply under full Agency financing.

nodes.title: GENERAL
nodes.summary: The partial document explains the rules for participation loans in multi-family housing projects under the USDA Rural Housing Service program. It covers how these loans are processed through the NOFA process and standard program procedures, with additional coordination needed when other funding sources are involved. It emphasizes key eligibility conditions: the number of Agency-eligible tenant units must be at least equal to the number of units financed by the Agency, and the project must have a participation agreement, intercreditor agreement, or MOU with other lenders. It also notes that mixed-income projects are allowed, but they require extra oversight to ensure compliance. Finally, it outlines design requirements for participation loan projects, stating that construction must follow applicable RD instructions while allowing some flexibility in design, nonessential facilities, and documentation, depending on partner agency requirements.

nodes.title: DESIGN REQUIREMENTS
nodes.summary:
The partial document explains the requirements and procedures for participation loans in multi-family housing projects under HB-1-3560. It covers how participation loans are processed through the NOFA process, with regular program requirements and some added coordination with other funding sources. The document highlights key eligibility conditions, including ensuring that the number of income-eligible units is at least equal to the number of units financed by the Agency, and that the Agency must have a participation agreement, intercreditor agreement, or MOU with other lenders.

It also describes design requirements for these projects, noting that while RD construction instructions still apply, participation loans allow some flexibility in design and in the use of nonessential common facilities, as long as quality and safety standards are met. The document emphasizes that all units must be of equal quality in mixed-income projects and that optional features may be allowed if they are customary and support marketability.

Finally, the section outlines security requirements, including the Agency’s preference for a first or parity lien on the property and project revenues, though a junior lien may be accepted if adequately secured. It explains the need for a formal security agreement to define lien positions, responsibilities, and relationships among the Agency and other lenders.

nodes.title: Composition and Quality of Units
nodes.summary: The document explains requirements for mixed-income housing units and nonessential common facilities in participation loan projects, emphasizing that all units must be equal in quality and that amenities may be included if customary and marketable. It then outlines the Agency’s security arrangements for participation loans, including seeking a first or parity lien, allowing a junior lien only when adequately secured, and requiring formal agreements with other lenders to define liens, responsibilities, and relationships.

nodes.title: nullssential Common Facilities [7 CFR 3560.66(e)]
nodes.summary: The partial document explains requirements for project composition, quality, and security in participation loans. It states that all units in mixed-income or Agency-funded projects must be equal in quality, with no lower standard for lower-income units, and allows certain marketable features such as patios, balconies, washers/dryers, and garbage disposals when customary for the area. It also covers nonessential common facilities, which may be included only if they are safely operated, not funded by Agency money, supported by resident user fees, and separated by parcel and lien when appropriate. The document then describes security agreements for participation loans, including the Agency’s goal of obtaining a first or parity lien on property and revenues, the possibility of accepting a junior lien if adequately protected, and the need for a participation agreement, intercreditor agreement, or MOU that defines the roles and responsibilities of the Agency and other lenders.

nodes.title: SECURITY AGREEMENTS
nodes.summary:
The partial document explains requirements for mixed-income housing projects financed with participation loans, emphasizing that all units must be equal in quality regardless of resident income level. It allows certain marketable design features if they are customary for the area. It also describes rules for including nonessential common facilities, such as the need for health and safety safeguards, no Agency funding for the facilities, financial self-support through user fees, and separate parcels/liens when appropriate.

The document then outlines security agreement requirements for participation loans. The Agency generally seeks a first or parity lien on the property and project revenues, but may accept a junior lien if its interests are adequately protected. It defines adequate security as sufficient property value to ensure repayment of the loan principal after senior liens are satisfied. Finally, it states that the Loan Originator must prepare and negotiate a participation agreement, intercreditor agreement, or memorandum of understanding to clearly set out lien positions and each lender’s responsibilities.

nodes.title: Lien Position
nodes.summary: The partial document covers standards for mixed-income project units and the Agency’s security requirements for participation loans. It explains that all units must be equal in quality regardless of income level, and that certain marketable design features may be included if customary for the area. It also describes when nonessential common facilities are allowed, including safeguards and funding limitations. The document then outlines how the Agency secures its interest through lien position and security agreements, including first or parity liens, possible junior liens if adequately protected, and the use of participation agreements, intercreditor agreements, or MOUs to define responsibilities among lenders.

nodes.title: Security Agreement
nodes.summary: The partial document covers requirements for participation loans in mixed-income projects, including standards for unit quality, allowable design features, and rules for nonessential common facilities. It explains how the Agency secures its interest through liens and security agreements, including the need for participation, intercreditor, or memorandum of understanding documents that define lien position, defaults, notices, payment handling, construction fund advances, reserves, and foreclosure procedures. It also introduces subsidy layering, stating that total project funding from all sources must not exceed what is necessary for project feasibility, and notes the use of SAUCE software to evaluate whether subsidy is excessive.

nodes.title: Sample Agreements
nodes.summary:
The partial document explains required provisions for participation agreements, intercreditor agreements, or memoranda of understanding between USDA and other financing sources during loan origination. It covers key terms such as lien position and collateral, cross-default provisions, notice requirements, how payments are applied, rules for using loan proceeds during construction, reserve requirements, and foreclosure coordination. It also notes that sample agreements are provided in an attachment.

The document then introduces subsidy layering, emphasizing USDA’s legal obligation to ensure total project funding does not exceed the minimum needed for feasibility. It mentions the SAUCE software as the tool used to analyze subsidy levels and prevent excess subsidy.

nodes.title: SUBSIDY LAYERING
nodes.summary:
The partial document explains requirements for participation loans involving the Agency and other lenders. It covers the key terms that must be included in participation, intercreditor, or memorandum agreements, such as lien position, collateral, cross-default provisions, notice obligations, payment allocation, construction fund advances, reserve requirements, and foreclosure consultation. It also notes the availability of sample agreements.

The document then addresses subsidy layering, stating that the Agency must ensure total project funding does not exceed the minimum needed for feasibility, using SAUCE software to perform the analysis.

Finally, it discusses rents, rental assistance, management, and reserve requirements for projects funded through participation loans. It explains how rents must be set, when Agency-funded rental assistance may be allowed, how management standards are applied when Agency and lender requirements differ, and that reserve funding levels are determined case by case based on project needs and other lenders’ requirements.

nodes.title: RENTS, RENTAL ASSISTANCE, AND MANAGEMENT
nodes.summary: The partial document explains rules for rents, rental assistance, management, and reserve requirements in Agency-funded participation loan projects. It states that rents for Agency-financed units must follow Agency rent-setting rules under 7 CFR part 3560. It also describes when Agency-funded rental assistance may be provided, including a requirement that the Agency loan be at least 25% of the project’s total development cost unless an exception is approved, and that assistance be limited to units whose basic rents do not exceed what they would have been under full Agency financing. The management section says participation loan projects may serve either lower-income households only or mixed-income households, but income-eligible units must meet the stricter of Agency or lender management standards, and the management plan must explain how those standards will be met. Finally, the reserve requirements section says replacement reserves are intended to cover major capital needs over the project’s life, and for participation loans the reserve level is set case by case based on the requirements of all participating lenders.

nodes.title: Establishing Rents
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses requirements for rents, rental assistance, management, and reserve funding in Agency-funded participation loan projects. It explains how rents must be established in compliance with Agency rules, when Agency-funded rental assistance may be provided and the conditions that must be met, and how management standards are handled for projects serving lower-income or mixed-income households. It also outlines reserve requirements, stating that replacement reserve funding is intended to cover major capital needs over a project’s life and that, for participation loans, reserve levels are set case by case in coordination with other lenders.

nodes.title: Rental Assistance [7 CFR 3560.66(b)]
nodes.summary: The partial document explains rules for rents, rental assistance, management, and reserve requirements in multifamily housing projects financed with participation loans. It states that Agency-financed units must follow Agency rent-setting rules, and that rental assistance may be considered only if the Agency loan is at least 25% of total development cost unless an exception is approved, and only for units whose basic rents do not exceed what they would be under full Agency financing. It also covers management expectations, noting that projects may serve low-income or mixed-income households, but income-eligible units must meet the stricter of Agency or lender management requirements and the management plan must describe how those requirements will be met. Finally, it outlines reserve requirements, emphasizing that reserve funding should be sufficient for long-term capital needs and will be set case by case for participation loans based on the requirements of all participating lenders.

nodes.title: Management
nodes.summary: The document section covers how rents, rental assistance, management, and reserve requirements are handled for multifamily housing projects financed through participation loans. It explains that rents for Agency-financed units must follow Agency rules and applicable regulations, and that Agency-funded rental assistance may be available only if the Agency loan is at least 25% of total development cost and assistance is limited to units whose basic rents do not exceed what they would be under full Agency financing. It also states that projects may serve either low-income households only or mixed-income households, but units for income-eligible tenants must meet the stricter of Agency or lender management standards, with the management plan required to describe compliance procedures. Finally, it notes that reserve funding for participation loans is set case by case based on the needs and requirements of all participating lenders.

nodes.title: RESERVE REQUIREMENTS [7 CFR 3560.65]
nodes.summary: The partial document explains key rules for participation loan projects under HB-1-3560. It covers how rents must be established for Agency-financed units, when Agency-funded rental assistance may be provided, and the management requirements for multi-family housing serving either lower-income or mixed-income households. It also details reserve requirements, including how reserve levels are set on a case-by-case basis, minimum funding standards, and when additional borrower contributions may be required. Finally, it begins a section on processing applications involving participation loans, noting that the Loan Originator generally follows standard procedures but must also address special issues such as underwriting, Agency security position, design and management review, and loan limits, working in coordination with other funding sources.

nodes.title: OVERVIEW
nodes.summary:
The partial document discusses reserve funding requirements and the processing of participation loans under HB-1-3560. It explains that reserve contributions should generally cover replacement costs based on life-cycle analysis, but rents must remain within Comparable Rents for Conventional Units (CRCU) limits. At a minimum, reserves must equal 10 percent of the project’s Rural Development-eligible development cost or appraised value, with guidance on when higher reserves may be needed. It also states that reserve terms and withdrawal procedures must be agreed to by all lenders and included in participation or intercreditor agreements.

The document then introduces Section 2 on processing applications involving participation loans, noting that loan originators follow the standard loan processing procedures but must also address underwriting, security position, design and management features, and loan limits. It emphasizes coordination with other funding sources, such as private lenders, Federal agencies, and state housing finance agencies, to ensure funding is secured and that there is no excess subsidy. If funding sources fail to materialize, the project must be reevaluated and the National Office contacted.

Finally, the underwriting section outlines special considerations for participation loans, including applicant and project eligibility and project feasibility. It stresses that market studies must show clear need and demand for all units, including mixed-income developments, and that if LIHTC-supported units exceed the need for unsubsidized units, the application must be rejected.

nodes.title: UNDERWRITING
nodes.summary: The partial document explains the procedures and underwriting requirements for Rural Development participation loans. It emphasizes coordination with state housing finance agencies and other funding partners to confirm external financing, avoid excess subsidy, and reassess the project if expected funding does not materialize. It then outlines underwriting rules, noting that applicant and project eligibility follow the same standards as Agency-only multifamily housing loans. The section also focuses on project feasibility, especially market need and demand for all unit types and income levels, and requires rejection if the market analysis does not support the number of deeply subsidized units when LIHTCs exceed rental assistance levels. It concludes with key underwriting criteria such as applicant eligibility, project eligibility, economic feasibility, reasonable project costs, and protection of the Agency’s interests.

nodes.title: Applicant and Project Eligibility
nodes.summary:
The partial document explains procedures for processing and underwriting participation loans for multi-family housing projects. It emphasizes coordination with state housing finance agencies and other funding partners, especially when LIHTCs and HOME funds are involved, to avoid excess subsidy and to confirm funding availability. It also requires the Loan Originator to reevaluate project feasibility and notify the National Office if expected funding does not materialize.

The underwriting section states that participation loans must be underwritten like other multi-family housing loans, but with special considerations. It covers applicant and project eligibility, project feasibility, and market need. In particular, the document says market feasibility must show clear demand for all proposed units and income groups, and if LIHTCs are being used for more units than are supported by rental assistance or similar subsidy, the documentation must still show demand for unsubsidized units or the application must be rejected. It concludes with key underwriting questions: whether the applicant and project are eligible, whether the project is economically feasible, whether costs are reasonable, and whether the Agency’s interests are protected.

nodes.title: Project Feasibility
nodes.summary: The partial document explains how participation loan applications should be coordinated and underwritten for multifamily housing projects. It emphasizes early coordination with state housing finance agencies and other funding partners to verify likely funding sources such as LIHTCs and HOME funds, avoid excess subsidy, and reevaluate the project if expected funding does not materialize. It then outlines underwriting requirements, stating that applicant and project eligibility standards are the same as for Agency-only financing, and that the loan originator must assess project feasibility. A key focus is market feasibility: the documentation must show clear need and demand for all proposed units, including all income groups in mixed-income projects. It also notes that if LIHTCs cover more units than those receiving rental assistance or similar subsidy, the market study must demonstrate demand for unsubsidized units or the application must be rejected. The section ends with a summary of core underwriting questions, including eligibility, economic feasibility, reasonableness of costs, and protection of the Agency’s interests.

nodes.title: Project Need
nodes.summary: The partial document explains requirements for Rural Development participation loans, focusing on coordination with other funding partners, especially state housing finance agencies that provide LIHTCs and HOME funds, to avoid excess subsidy. It says the Loan Originator must monitor whether expected financing sources are secured, and if funding changes, must reassess project feasibility, rescore the application, and contact the National Office. The section on underwriting states that participation loans must be underwritten like other multifamily housing loans, with standard eligibility and feasibility checks applying. It emphasizes applicant and project eligibility, market need and demand for all unit types and income levels, and special review when LIHTC-supported units exceed the share of subsidized units, requiring rejection if the market does not support the number of units without deep subsidy.

nodes.title: Financial Feasibility
nodes.summary: The partial document focuses on underwriting and approval requirements for a project loan, especially for mixed-income developments. It explains that the Loan Originator must assess financial feasibility by reviewing project income and expenses and confirming that funding sources and uses align. It also requires verification of other financing sources, including documentation of loan applications or grant requests, confirmation of eligibility, and reassessment of funding likelihood if no firm commitment exists. The document then addresses reasonable project costs, requiring the Loan Originator to ensure costs are justified and that any nonessential common facilities are not paid for with Agency funds, while still protecting tenant health and safety. Finally, it covers management requirements, stating that the applicant must demonstrate adequate management systems and provide a management plan showing how Agency program requirements will be met for eligible units.

nodes.title: Viability of Other Funding/Financing Sources
nodes.summary: This excerpt covers key underwriting and approval considerations for a mixed-income project financed in part by the Agency. It explains that the Loan Originator must assess the project’s financial feasibility by reviewing projected income, expenses, and whether sources and uses of funds balance. It also requires verification that other financing sources are likely to be secured, including documentation of applications or grant requests and follow-up with funding sources; if outside financing appears unlikely, the loan may be rejected and the National Office must be notified. The section then addresses reasonable project costs, requiring use of underwriting tools to confirm costs are appropriate and that any nonessential common facilities are not financed with Agency funds, while still ensuring tenant health and safety. Finally, it emphasizes management requirements, stating that applicants must demonstrate adequate management systems and provide a management plan showing how Agency program requirements will be met for eligible tenants.

nodes.title: Reasonable Project Costs
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines key underwriting and approval requirements for a mixed-income or Agency-financed project. It explains that the Loan Originator must assess financial feasibility by reviewing projected income, expenses, and whether sources and uses align. It also requires verification of other funding and financing sources, including documentation of applications or grant requests, confirmation that applicants are eligible, and reassessment if funding is not yet firmly committed. The document then covers reasonable project costs, emphasizing use of underwriting tools to ensure costs are appropriate and that nonessential common facilities are not paid for with Agency funds, while still protecting tenant health and safety. Finally, it addresses management requirements, stating that the applicant must demonstrate adequate management systems and provide a management plan showing how Agency program requirements will be met for eligible tenants.

nodes.title: Management
nodes.summary:
The partial document explains key underwriting and approval requirements for participation loans. It covers how the Loan Originator must evaluate financial feasibility by reviewing project income, expenses, and the match between sources and uses of funds. It also describes how to assess the reliability of other financing sources by requiring documentation, verifying applications with lenders or grantors, and rejecting the loan if outside funding is unlikely. 

The document then addresses reasonable project costs, including special review of nonessential common facilities in mixed-income projects to ensure Agency funds are not used for those facilities and that health and safety protections are in place. It outlines management requirements, emphasizing that applicants must demonstrate adequate management systems and explain how Agency program rules and any user fees will be handled. 

Finally, it summarizes environmental review requirements, noting that participation loans follow the same process as nonparticipation loans and that the review covers the entire project. It also covers subsidy review using SAUCE software and the process for establishing loan amount, terms, and conditions, including how grants and other funding sources affect the maximum Agency loan amount.

nodes.title: ENVIRONMENTAL REQUIREMENTS
nodes.summary: The partial document covers key procedures for USDA/Rural Development housing participation loans. It explains that management plans must address user fees for nonessential facilities, describes environmental review requirements and notes that the review applies to the entire project, and outlines subsidy review procedures using the SAUCE software with supporting cost and budget forms. It also begins the section on establishing loan amounts, terms, and conditions, emphasizing that loan amounts must account for all funding sources and eligible costs, and that grants are treated differently depending on when they are received.

nodes.title: SUBSIDY REVIEWS
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines key procedures for USDA Rural Development multifamily housing participation loans. It explains that the management plan must address how user fees will cover operating and maintenance costs of nonessential facilities. It then describes environmental requirements, stating that participation loans undergo the same environmental review process as nonparticipation loans and that the review covers the entire project; if a state agency is the financial partner, the Loan Originator should coordinate with the State Environmental Coordinator for a joint review. The document also covers subsidy reviews, requiring the Loan Originator to use SAUCE software and related forms to analyze all loan requests before approval. Finally, it begins the section on establishing loan amount, terms, and conditions, explaining that Agency loans are limited to eligible costs and all funding sources are considered, while certain grants do not count as borrower equity and later grants for disallowed costs or overruns do not change the Agency loan amount.

nodes.title: ESTABLISHING LOAN AMOUNT, TERMS, AND CONDITIONS
nodes.summary: The partial document covers several sections of USDA Rural Development loan processing and project requirements. It explains that management plans must address how user fees will fund operating and maintenance costs for nonessential facilities. It then outlines environmental review requirements for participation loans, emphasizing that the review must cover the whole project and may involve coordination with a State Environmental Coordinator for joint reviews with state agencies. Next, it describes subsidy review procedures, stating that Loan Originators must use the SAUCE software to analyze loan requests using applicant-provided cost and budget data. Finally, it discusses how to establish loan amounts, terms, and conditions, noting that Agency loans must reflect all funding sources and eligible costs, and that grants used later for disallowed costs or overruns do not change the Agency’s loan amount.

nodes.title: Establishing Loan Amounts
nodes.summary: The partial document covers key procedures and requirements for participation loans in Agency housing financing. It explains that management plans must address user fees for nonessential facilities, then outlines environmental review requirements, emphasizing that participation loans undergo the same environmental review as nonparticipation loans and that the review must cover the entire project, with special coordination when a State agency is involved. It also describes subsidy reviews, including the use of SAUCE software and supporting cost/budget forms to analyze loan requests before approval. The document then discusses how to establish loan amounts, terms, and conditions, including how all funding sources and eligible costs are considered, how grants and tax credits affect loan calculations, and how borrower equity contributions are determined. Finally, it addresses working with State housing finance agencies, stressing the need for Field Offices to create procedures and partnerships to coordinate affordable housing financing resources and information exchange.

nodes.title: Borrower Contribution Requirement
nodes.summary:
The partial document explains how the Agency determines loan amounts for participation loans using appraised value, total development cost, and other leveraged funding such as LIHTCs. It gives an example showing how tax credits are deducted from the appraised value, how the Agency then funds 95 percent of the remaining balance, and how the borrower must provide a minimum equity contribution and obtain any additional funding needed to cover total development costs. It also notes that grants received later do not affect the Agency’s point score if they were not counted in loan prioritization.

The document further describes borrower contribution requirements, including how minimum borrower equity is calculated and when a limited-profit borrower’s own additional funds or loans from the borrower may count toward initial investment, subject to rent limitations and other rules. Finally, it introduces the Agency’s coordination with State housing finance agencies, emphasizing partnerships, especially with LIHTC-allocating agencies, and requiring Field Offices to develop procedures for sharing information and coordinating participation loan processing.

nodes.title: WORKING WITH STATE HOUSING FINANCE AGENCIES
nodes.summary:
The partial document explains how the Agency determines loan amounts for participation loans when LIHTCs and other funds are involved. It gives an example showing that the loan is based on the lower of total development cost or appraised value, subtracts tax credit proceeds as available funding, and then requires the borrower to contribute a minimum equity percentage. It also notes that additional grants can be used to cover remaining development costs without affecting the Agency’s point score if they were not considered in the original ranking.

The document then discusses borrower contribution requirements, including how the minimum contribution is calculated and when a borrower’s own funds, borrower loans, or LIHTC proceeds may count as part of the borrower’s investment.

Finally, it introduces coordination with State housing finance agencies. It emphasizes that the Agency should work closely with these agencies to align resources, share project and funding information, avoid excess government assistance, and monitor compliance after loan closing. It also requires State Offices to establish a Memorandum of Understanding that defines the working relationship and information-sharing procedures.

nodes.title: Coordinating Resources
nodes.summary: The document section explains how Rural Development State Offices should coordinate with State housing finance agencies to maximize affordable housing production. It emphasizes participation in the State’s Consolidated Plan, sharing applicant and project information, and trying to secure preference for RHS applicants in LIHTC allocations. It also describes the need to coordinate during loan application review to avoid excessive government assistance and during asset management to ensure compliance with tax credit rules. Finally, it states that State Offices must establish a formal working relationship with the State housing finance agency through a Memorandum of Understanding, which sets out how information will be exchanged and supports the required subsidy review process.

nodes.title: Entering into a Memorandum of Understanding
nodes.summary: The partial document explains how the Agency coordinates with State housing finance agencies to maximize affordable housing production and avoid excessive government assistance. It describes the need to work with State agencies on LIHTC and other funding, both during loan application review and later asset management, sharing information about financing, costs, occupancy, tenant incomes, and rents. It also requires State Offices to establish a Memorandum of Understanding that defines roles, information-sharing procedures, fee norms, cost formulas, excess assistance rules, and confidentiality. Finally, it outlines the loan originator’s responsibilities to contact the State agency, verify funding applications, exchange project information, and compare data for consistency as part of the loan origination process.

nodes.title: Working with the State Agency
nodes.summary:
The partial document covers several related Rural Housing Service procedures and sample agreements for multi-family housing financing.

Main points include:

- **Working with State Housing Finance Agencies:**  
  It explains how the Loan Originator and Agency coordinate with a State housing finance agency when a borrower is also seeking LIHTC or other funding. The section emphasizes verifying applications, sharing project information, and comparing data for consistency.

- **Memorandum of Understanding (MOU) contents:**  
  Exhibit 6-3 outlines what an MOU with a State housing finance agency should cover, including:
  - fee norms for construction and developer costs,
  - rules for deviations from those norms,
  - formulas for determining costs and assistance,
  - handling excess assistance,
  - information-sharing limits and confidentiality,
  - and regular exchange of project and tax credit data between agencies.

- **Sample intercreditor, parity, and subordination agreements:**  
  Attachment 6-A provides sample legal documents showing how USDA Rural Development and a bank can structure financing relationships for shared projects. These samples address:
  - lien priority and co-equal or subordinate positions,
  - default notifications,
  - foreclosure procedures,
  - pro rata sharing of proceeds,
  - borrower obligations,
  - and restrictions on refinancing, assignments, or future advances.

- **Processing MOU sample with State Housing Credit Agencies:**  
  Attachment 6-B gives a sample MOU between Rural Housing Service and a State Housing Credit Agency. It focuses on cooperation in analyzing project costs, setting fee norms, preventing excess assistance, sharing data on tax credit and Section 515 projects, and coordinating reviews of funding requests.

- **Chapter 7: Security Requirements:**  
  The document then begins Chapter 7, which explains how Multi-Family Housing loans must be secured to protect the government’s financial interest. It introduces:
  - land control requirements,
  - adequacy of collateral,
  - liability,
  - acceptable lien position,
  - and the role of participation loans.

- **Appraisal requirements and property valuation:**  
  The chapter also discusses how appraisals are used to determine security value. It covers:
  - when appraisals are required,
  - qualifications of appraisers,
  - USPAP and nondiscrimination standards,
  - acceptable appraisal forms and narrative reports,
  - valuation methods such as cost, sales comparison, and income approaches,
  - reconciliation of value estimates,
  - and valuation of favorable financing such as interest credit subsidies, tax credits, and grants.

- **Appraisal process roles and responsibilities:**  
  The final portion begins outlining the appraisal workflow, including who requests the appraisal, who prepares the Statement of Work and Request for Quote, and how the process fits into loan origination.

Overall, the partial document focuses on **coordination with state agencies, legal financing agreements, and security/appraisal rules for Rural Development multi-family housing loans**.

nodes.title: THE APPRAISAL PROCESS —ROLES AND RESPONSIBILITIES
nodes.summary: The partial document focuses on appraisal requirements for affordable housing, especially how to handle favorable financing and how the appraisal process is managed. It explains that when market value is assessed with restricted rents, any favorable financing in place must be valued separately from the real property and fully described in the appraisal report, including each financing source, its terms, and its individual value. It also notes that interest credit subsidy from Section 514 and 515 direct loans must be valued using a specific guide, and similar methods can apply to low-interest non-Agency loans. In addition, the document begins a section on the appraisal process, outlining roles and responsibilities and describing the initial step where the Loan Originator requests the appraisal and the State Contracting Officer prepares the Statement of Work and Request for Quote.

nodes.title: The Appraisal Process
nodes.summary:
The partial document focuses on two main areas: how favorable financing is valued in affordable housing appraisals, and how the appraisal process is organized and managed within the Agency.

First, it explains that affordable housing developments often use layered financing sources such as direct loans, tax-exempt bonds, tax credits, grants, and other low-interest or subsidized financing. When appraising properties with restricted rents, these favorable financing sources must be valued separately from the real property itself. The appraisal report must identify each financing source, describe its terms and amount, and include separate valuations for each type. It also notes that valuing interest credit subsidy must follow a specific guide, and that similar methods may be used for low-interest non-Agency loans.

Second, the document outlines the appraisal workflow and the roles involved. The process includes requesting the appraisal, selecting an appraiser, discussing the statement of work, performing the appraisal, reviewing the appraisal, and analyzing the results. It describes responsibilities of key staff such as the Loan Originator, Senior Appraiser, and Staff Appraiser, emphasizing appraisal quality, compliance with Agency and USPAP standards, and the need for review and oversight.

nodes.title: Roles and Responsibilities
nodes.summary: The partial document explains the appraisal review and appraisal selection process for Rural Development Multi-Family Housing projects. It outlines Step 5, where a State-designated Appraiser/Reviewer performs a technical review of the appraisal and prepares a USPAP-compliant review report, and Step 6, where the Loan Originator analyzes the appraisal results to make value and loan decision determinations. It then describes the roles and responsibilities of key staff involved, including the Loan Originator, Senior Appraiser, Staff Appraiser, State Appraiser, Contracting Officer, and National Office, with emphasis on appraisal quality, oversight, reviews, training, and contract management. Finally, it covers Section 7.11 on selecting an appraiser, explaining that selection must follow Agency contracting requirements, often through a Blanket Purchase Agreement, and notes the Contracting Officer’s role in maintaining the approved appraiser list while staff verify pricing and turnaround time.

nodes.title: SELECTING AN APPRAISER
nodes.summary: The excerpt explains roles and responsibilities in the appraisal process and the procedures for selecting an appraiser. It describes qualifications and duties for Staff Appraisers, the supervision and review requirements for their work, and the distinctions between Staff Appraisers, Senior Appraisers, State Appraisers, Contracting Officers, and the National Office. It then introduces Section 7.11 on selecting an appraiser, stating that selection must follow Agency contracting requirements and may use a Blanket Purchase Agreement (BPA) or a request for contract services. The BPA process is outlined, including maintaining a list of approved appraisers, developing the list according to contracting procedures, and checking that an appraiser’s price and completion timeframe are acceptable before engagement.

nodes.title: Blanket Purchase Agreement
nodes.summary: The partial document explains USDA/Rural Development appraisal procedures for multi-unit housing, focusing on who may serve as appraisers and how appraisals are assigned and managed. It describes roles such as Staff Appraiser, State Appraiser, Contracting Officer, and National Office oversight responsibilities. It then outlines methods for selecting an appraiser, including use of a Blanket Purchase Agreement list or a request for contract services, with attention to price, availability, and rotating appraisers fairly. The section also emphasizes the required completion timeframe for appraisals—generally within 45 days, with possible extensions up to 30 days—and describes the post-award conference process, where the Contracting Officer’s Representative reviews the Statement of Work with the appraiser and provides required forms and assignment details.

nodes.title: Request for Contract Services
nodes.summary: The partial document explains procedures for obtaining and managing appraisal services for contract work. It covers how appraisal staff should use the approved appraiser list, rotate among appraisers, and move to the next appraiser if requirements cannot be met. It also describes an alternative process for requesting contract services through competitive bids, where the lowest responsive bid is selected based on a Statement of Work prepared by appraisal staff. The document emphasizes the required time frame for completing appraisals, generally within 45 days, with possible extensions of up to 30 days under limited circumstances. Finally, it outlines post-award responsibilities, including a conference between the Contracting Officer’s Representative and the appraiser to review the scope of work and provide necessary forms and the Statement of Work before work begins.

nodes.title: Time frame for Completion of Appraisals
nodes.summary: The passage explains how appraisal services are obtained and managed under contract. It covers selecting appraisers from a BPA list, including rotating through available names and moving to the next appraiser if criteria are not met. It also describes requesting contract services through bidding, where the lowest responsive bid is selected based on a Statement of Work prepared by appraisal staff. The document sets a completion timeframe for appraisals of 45 days, with possible extensions up to 30 days under certain circumstances. Finally, it outlines the post-award conference process, where the Contracting Officer’s Representative reviews the work with the appraiser and provides necessary forms and the Statement of Work so the appraiser understands the assignment.

nodes.title: WORKING WITH THE APPRAISER
nodes.summary: The passage explains how appraisal services are obtained and managed under HB-1-3560. It covers using the BPA list of appraisers, emphasizing that staff should use all names on the list and rotate appraisers fairly. It then describes an alternative method: requesting contract services, where contracting staff solicit bids and award the contract to the lowest responsive bidder using the appraisal staff’s Statement of Work as the basis. The document also sets a completion timeframe for appraisals, requiring them to be finished within 45 days unless an extension of up to 30 days is granted for reasons beyond control. Finally, it outlines post-award procedures for working with the selected appraiser, including a conference with the COR and providing the appraiser with necessary forms and the Statement of Work to ensure the assignment is clearly understood.

nodes.title: Post -Award Conference with the Appraiser
nodes.summary:
The partial document explains procedures for obtaining contract appraisal services and working with appraisers for multi-family housing appraisals.
