It covers how appraisal staff should use a BPA list or request for contract services, including selecting appraisers, rotating through the list, and choosing the lowest responsive bid. It also sets the expected appraisal completion timeframe of 45 days, with possible extensions of up to 30 days in limited cases.

The document then describes the post-award conference process, where the Contracting Officer’s Representative reviews the scope of work with the appraiser before work begins and provides required forms, the statement of work, applicable regulations and instructions, an appraisal data package, and the notice to proceed/task order letter.

Finally, it details what the Statement of Work must include so the appraiser can produce a high-quality appraisal: the intended use of the appraisal, the required value types, required submission details such as copies, delivery addresses, and deadlines, and background information about the appraisal and property, including report type, format, property contacts, status, ownership, and related project details.

nodes.title: The Statement of Work
nodes.summary:
The partial document outlines procedures for obtaining, managing, paying for, and reviewing appraisals for Multi-Family Housing projects. It explains what information should be given to the appraiser, including agency appraisal regulations, an appraisal data package, and a notice to proceed or task order letter. It then details the required contents of the Statement of Work, such as the intended use of the appraisal, the types of value needed, required report submissions, and background property and appraisal information.

The document also covers payment procedures, stating that a Staff Appraiser must first review the appraisal for compliance with the Statement of Work, USPAP, and agency requirements before payment is approved. If deficiencies are found, they must be corrected before payment, and contract issues must be handled through the Contracting Officer. Finally, it introduces the agency review process for appraisals not written by an Agency certified general appraiser, including a technical review report and review steps focused on completeness, reasoning, and proper use of data such as rents and value assumptions.

nodes.title: Paying for Appraisals
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines appraisal-related procedures for multifamily housing projects under HB-1-3560. It explains how appraisal information sheets should be completed and provided to the appraiser, then details the payment process for contract appraisers, including required staff review for compliance with the statement of work, USPAP, and agency rules. It also describes what happens if an appraisal is deficient, including returning it for correction and involving the contracting officer for contract issues. The section on agency review states that all appraisals not prepared by an Agency certified general appraiser must be reviewed by one, with a technical review report filed. Finally, it lists review steps such as checking completeness and reasoning, including whether the appraiser used the correct rents and properly supported conclusions.

nodes.title: AGENCY REVIEW OF THE APPRAISAL
nodes.summary: The partial document focuses on USDA Rural Development appraisal procedures for Multi-Family Housing projects. It explains how appraisals must be reviewed before payment, including checks for consistency with the Statement of Work, USPAP, and Agency requirements, and outlines what to do if an appraisal is deficient. It then describes the formal Agency review process for appraisals not prepared by an Agency-certified general appraiser, including review for completeness, reasoning, favorable financing valuations, and overall reliability of value conclusions. The section also specifies possible review outcomes, record retention requirements, and filing the review report in the loan docket. Finally, it covers the release of appraisals to owners/applicants and notes that FOIA does not prevent disclosure of appraisals from their own files so they can identify errors or deficiencies.

nodes.title: RELEASE OF APPRAISALS
nodes.summary: The partial document explains appraisal review procedures for subsidized Multi-Family Housing properties, focusing on key factors that affect value, such as development, environmental, zoning, encroachment, market, demographic, and future development information. It outlines the need to review and separately value favorable financing sources like USDA interest credit subsidies, low-interest loans, tax-exempt bonds, tax credits, and grants. It also describes how reviewers should evaluate the overall reliability of the appraisal’s final value conclusion, reconcile different valuation approaches, and determine one of four review dispositions: accepted, accepted as revised, accepted as modified, or rejected. In addition, it covers recordkeeping requirements for appraisal reviews and explains that appraisals ordered by the Agency may be released to owners/applicants from their files, including under FOIA, so they can review and identify errors or deficiencies.

nodes.title: Freedom of Information Act
nodes.summary: The document covers appraisal review procedures for subsidized Multi-Family Housing properties, focusing on how reviewers should evaluate site and market factors, value favorable financing sources separately, and assess the overall reliability of the appraisal conclusion. It explains the possible review outcomes and recordkeeping requirements. The text also details how Rural Development appraisals are released to owners/applicants, including FOIA considerations, preparing a cover letter, redacting proprietary expense comparable information, and delivering the redacted appraisal. Finally, it notes that Rural Development must be identified as the client and that both Rural Development and the owner/applicant should be listed as intended users in fee appraisals, while the internal review report remains confidential.

nodes.title: Steps in Release of Appraisals
nodes.summary: The document explains how Rural Development handles release of multi-family housing appraisals from an owner/applicant’s file, including when the request is not treated as a FOIA request, how to redact proprietary expense comparable information, and how to provide the appraisal with a cover letter. It also clarifies that appraisal review reports are confidential and generally not releasable, with FOIA Exemption 5 cited for denied requests. The second main section discusses how loan staff should evaluate appraisal results in underwriting, focusing on project eligibility, economic feasibility, site suitability, rent comparisons to market rents, and whether project costs and Agency interests are adequately protected.

nodes.title: EVALUATING THE RESULTS OF THE APPRAISAL
nodes.summary:
The partial document explains how appraisal information is handled and how it should be used in underwriting decisions for a housing or loan program. It states that owners or applicants may receive appraisals from their own files upon request, but appraisal review reports are not released, and FOIA requests for those reports should be denied under Exemption 5 because of deliberative process privilege.

The main section focuses on evaluating appraisal results. It describes the appraisal as a critical underwriting document that helps the Loan Originator assess the property’s region, neighborhood, market, and value. The document then ties appraisal review back to key underwriting questions: whether the project is eligible, economically feasible, has reasonable costs, and adequately protects the Agency’s interests.

It specifically discusses project eligibility by looking at site suitability and rents. The appraisal is used to confirm boundary lines, zoning, and other site or market conditions, and to compare proposed rents with market rents. If proposed rents exceed acceptable market standards, the proposal must justify the exception or obtain additional financing.

Finally, the text begins to address economic feasibility, explaining that the appraisal helps determine demand for affordable units and the likelihood that the project will be financially successful.

nodes.title: Is the Project Eligible?
nodes.summary: The document section explains that appraisal reports may be released to owners/applicants upon request, but appraisal review reports are confidential and should be denied under FOIA Exemption 5. It then describes how loan originators use the appraisal in underwriting, focusing on key questions such as project eligibility, economic feasibility, reasonable costs, and protection of the Agency’s interests. The text outlines how to evaluate eligibility by reviewing site suitability, zoning, market conditions, and rent comparisons against market rents/CRCU, noting that higher rents require justification or additional financing. It also begins discussing how appraisals help assess whether the project is economically feasible by indicating demand for affordable units and likely financial success.

nodes.title: Is the Project Economically Feasible?
nodes.summary:
The partial document explains rules for releasing appraisal-related records, stating that appraisals may be shared with owners/applicants from their own files on request, but appraisal review reports are not releasable and should be denied under FOIA Exemption 5.

It then outlines how the results of an appraisal should be used in underwriting decisions. The Loan Originator is instructed to assess the appraisal against key questions: whether the project is eligible, economically feasible, reasonably costed, and whether the Agency’s interests are protected.

For project eligibility, the appraisal is used to review site suitability, zoning, boundary lines, neighborhood characteristics, and whether proposed rents are within allowed market limits or need justification for an exception. For economic feasibility, the document highlights factors such as vacancy rate, community growth, local industry strength, market rents, and capitalization rate as indicators of demand, financial viability, and investor risk.

For cost reasonableness, the appraisal’s cost approach is used to confirm highest and best use, and the income approach is used to compare projected operations and maintenance costs with those of similar subsidized properties. It notes that high operating expense ratios may mean inefficient management for conventional properties, but for subsidized housing they may also reflect restricted rental income.

nodes.title: Are the Costs Reasonable?
nodes.summary: The partial document explains key factors a Loan Originator should review when evaluating the feasibility and cost reasonableness of a proposed subsidized housing project. It focuses on market demand indicators such as vacancy rates, population growth, local industry strength, market rents, and capitalization rates to determine whether enough affordable housing is needed and whether the project carries significant risk. It also covers cost-related appraisal checks, including whether the property’s highest and best use is subsidized housing and whether projected operations and maintenance expenses are reasonable compared with similar projects.

nodes.title: Are the Agency’s Interests Secure?
nodes.summary: The partial document explains how the Agency evaluates whether its interests are secure in a multifamily housing project by comparing appraised value to development costs, checking land value consistency, determining insurable value, and ensuring the improvements’ remaining economic life is long enough for the loan term. It also introduces Attachment 7-A, which defines key value types used in RHS multifamily appraisals, focusing especially on market value. The document clarifies the federal definition of market value, notes that it should be described as “market value, as conventional or unrestricted” to avoid confusion, and explains that appraisals for subsidized Section 514/515 properties rely on a hypothetical condition that treats the property as conventional market housing.

nodes.title: V ALUE TYPES USED IN MULTI -FAMILY APPRAISALS
nodes.summary:
This attachment explains the main value types used in Rural Housing Service multi-family appraisals and how each should be defined, requested, reported, and supported. It focuses on several appraisal value concepts, including market value, market value subject to restricted rents, as-is value, prospective value, value-in-use, liquidation value, and insurable value.

A major emphasis is on market value subject to restricted rents for subsidized housing projects. The document explains that this value must reflect rent restrictions, subsidies, operating conditions, and rental assistance, but must exclude favorable financing. It outlines how the income approach should usually be the primary valuation method, how direct capitalization and discounted cash flow should be used, and why the sales comparison approach is often not appropriate for these properties. It also discusses the role of cost approach support and the need for strong support for income, vacancy, expense, and capitalization rate analyses.

The attachment also explains how rental assistance should be incorporated into value, how favorable financing must be valued separately from the real estate, and how interest credit subsidy should be handled. It stresses that appraisers should report real estate value separately from intangible financing-related value and not combine them into one market value conclusion.

In addition, the document covers special value types for other property situations: as-is value for existing legal and physical conditions, prospective value for proposed or under-construction properties, value-in-use for farm labor housing where market value is not the appropriate basis, liquidation value for quick sale or foreclosure-related situations, and insurable value for estimating insurance coverage based on replacement cost of destructible improvements.

nodes.title: PROPERTY RIGHTS V ALUED
nodes.summary: The document explains how appraisers should identify and describe the property rights being valued for Section 514/515 affordable housing projects. It emphasizes that, under USPAP, the real property interest must be clearly stated, and for market value appraisals this is generally the fee simple estate subject to short-term leases, but with important restrictions. The text discusses USDA Rural Development restrictive-use agreements, rent and tenant-income limits, and possible LIHTC deed restrictions as encumbrances that affect property rights and value. It also clarifies the distinction between fee simple estate, leased fee estate, and leasehold estate, and notes that because these properties are encumbered, the interest should be described as fee simple estate as restricted, subject to short-term leases, with a thorough explanation of the restrictions involved.

nodes.title: SPECIFIC VALUE TYPES TO BE REQUESTED AND REPORTED BASED ON THE INTENDED USE OF THE APPRAISAL
nodes.summary:
This document provides guidance for Rural Development appraisals on which value types should be requested and reported based on the intended use of the appraisal. It explains that the intended use of the appraisal drives the value type needed, and that multiple values may sometimes be required in one report, including separate treatment of intangible assets such as financing, tax credits, grants, and subsidies.

The document outlines general appraisal rules and expectations, including:
- Rural Development’s main value types: Market Value, Market Value subject to restricted rents, Liquidation Value, and their prospective versions.
- The need to follow 7 CFR Part 3560 and USPAP requirements.
- Highest and best use analysis requirements.
- Proper use of hypothetical conditions and extraordinary assumptions.
- The requirement to report intangible assets separately from real estate, especially in restricted-rent situations.
- Clarification that security value is an underwriter tool, not something the appraiser combines with real estate value.
- Guidance that appraisers should keep the subject in current physical condition except for prospective valuations.

It then gives a quick reference and detailed guidance for six common appraisal scenarios:
1. New funds or subsequent loans, such as new construction or rehab, requiring prospective market value with restricted rents and possibly a hypothetical conventional-housing value.
2. Existing loans, such as transfers or assumptions, requiring market value subject to restricted rents.
3. Property value for items not qualifying for prepayment incentives, requiring market value with all restrictions considered.
4. Property value for items that do qualify for prepayment incentives, requiring market value based on a hypothetical unsubsidized conventional housing condition.
5. Sale of eligible program property, such as REO or foreclosure, requiring both market value as conventional housing and market value subject to restricted rents.
6. Sale of non-program property, requiring market value as a conventional market property and possibly liquidation value based on a prescribed marketing period.

For each scenario, the document explains the intended use of the appraisal, what value to request, and key notes such as whether hypothetical conditions are allowed, when current physical condition must be used, how equity is determined, and when liquidation value or prospective value may be appropriate.

nodes.title: STANDARD STATEMENT OF WORK (SOW) FOR MULTI -FAMILY HOUSING CONTRACT APPRAISALS
nodes.summary:
The partial document is a standard statement of work for USDA Rural Development multi-family housing contract appraisals. It explains the program background and sets out the rules appraisers must follow, including USPAP compliance, RHS supplemental standards, and the need for qualified State Certified General Appraisers with experience in subsidized housing.

Main points covered include:
- How appraisal assignments are initiated through a Request for Quote (RFQ) and finalized through a Contract for Appraisal Services (CAS)
- Requirements for communication, post-award conference, property access, and confidentiality
- Prohibition on subcontracting without written approval
- Appraiser responsibilities regarding conflicts of interest, client identification, and property contact
- Nondiscrimination rules and consideration of ADA and environmental hazards
- Guidance on assumptions, hypothetical conditions, limiting conditions, appraisal formats, and valuation approaches
- Required content for complete appraisals, including USPAP items, executive summary, maps, photos, exhibits, legal description, market rent analysis, deferred maintenance, remaining economic life, and treatment of favorable financing
- Specific rules for income approach support, restricted rents, and acceptable valuation methods
- Requirement to include operating statements, budgets, rehab cost forms, and the CAS in the report addenda
- Review and accountability expectations, including the appraiser’s responsibility to defend the appraisal
- Payment terms, late delivery penalties, and possible cancellation for excessive delay

The attachment also includes an example Appraisal Information Sheet showing how assignment details are documented, such as appraisal type, client, intended users, property description, and financing sources.

nodes.title: APPRAISAL INFORMATION SHEET (EXAMPLE), ATTACHMENT TO SOW
nodes.summary:
The partial document contains USDA Rural Development appraisal forms and checklists. It first provides an example “Appraisal Information Sheet” for a proposed off-farm labor housing complex, identifying the client, property contact, intended users and use, appraisal purpose, property type, ownership, address, and detailed property description. It also summarizes project financing sources, including USDA Section 514 loan and Section 516 grant, a county HOME grant, and tax credits. The sheet specifies the required value conclusions, such as prospective market value, subsidy and grant values, and insurable value, and notes that the Cost Approach and Income Approach are required while the Sales Comparison Approach is not. It also addresses rental assistance, utility responsibilities, assumptions and limiting conditions, and report distribution instructions.

The second part is an “Appraisal Data Package Checklist” listing documents and information that should be included in the appraisal package, such as contracts, legal description, mortgage or deed of trust, HAP contract, restrictive use provisions, environmental and lead-based paint reports, inspection reports, market study, prior appraisal, survey, site and floor plans, unit mix, rent roll, waiting list, operating statements, proposed budget, construction plans and cost estimates, ownership history, and detailed financing information.

nodes.title: APPRAISAL DATA PACKAGE CHECKLIST
nodes.summary:
The partial document contains two main sections from USDA Rural Development handbook attachments related to multi-family housing appraisals. The first section is an appraisal data package checklist, listing the documents and information that should be included for an appraisal, such as sales contracts, legal descriptions, mortgage documents, HAP contracts, environmental and lead-based paint reports, inspections, market studies, prior appraisals, surveys, site plans, floor plans, rent rolls, tenant waiting lists, operating statements, budgets, construction plans, rehabilitation cost certifications, ownership history, and financing details.

The second section is a sample transmittal letter used to engage an appraisal services firm. It outlines how Rural Development communicates the appraisal assignment, including the property details, the expected valuation, the purchase order reference, submission deadlines, number of copies required, review period, invoice instructions, and enclosed contract documents. It also notes that the letter serves to confirm oral discussions and establish contract performance dates.

nodes.title: SAMPLE TRANSMITTAL LETTER
nodes.summary:
The partial document contains two main sections. First, it provides a sample transmittal letter used by Rural Development to engage an appraisal firm for a multi-family housing property appraisal. The letter outlines the property details, expected appraisal value type, purchase order reference, submission deadline, number of copies required, invoice instructions, payment process, and included enclosures such as the purchase order, COR designation form, and contract terms. It also notes that the letter confirms prior oral discussions and sets the contract performance dates.

Second, it begins a guide for valuing interest credit subsidy in USDA Rural Housing Service programs. This section explains what interest credit subsidy is, how it lowers the effective interest rate for eligible borrowers, and why it must be considered in appraisals for Section 514 and 515 properties when valuing restricted-rent housing. It introduces the valuation methodology, describing the subsidy as a present value calculation based on the monthly payment savings, discounted at a conventional mortgage rate over the loan term.

nodes.title: GUIDE FOR V ALUATION OF INTEREST CREDIT SUBSIDY
nodes.summary:
The partial document is a valuation guide for USDA Rural Housing Service interest credit subsidies in Section 514 and Section 515 multifamily housing appraisals. It explains what interest credit subsidy is, how it lowers the borrower’s effective mortgage payment, and why it must be valued separately from the property’s restricted-rent market value.

The main method covered is a discounted cash flow approach using three inputs: the monthly payment savings from favorable financing, the conventional market interest rate as the discount rate, and the loan term or remaining loan term as the period. It distinguishes between existing properties and new construction, noting that new construction loans may require subtracting the present value of a balloon payment when the amortization period is longer than the loan term.

The document includes a detailed example showing how to calculate the subsidy value for a Section 515 loan by comparing conventional loan payments to subsidized loan payments, discounting the savings, valuing the balloon payment, and arriving at a final subsidy value. It concludes with guidance that for existing properties, the subsidy value usually involves only the income stream from payment savings, using current loan balance and remaining term, and that original and subsequent RHS loan subsidies should be valued separately.

nodes.title: USDA Rural Development Insurable Value Calculation
nodes.summary: The partial document contains two USDA Rural Development attachment forms. One is an “Insurable Value Calculation” worksheet used to determine a property’s insurable value by listing property details, base cost per square foot, building area, replacement cost new, exclusions, inclusions, and the final concluded insurable value. The second is a “Cover Letter for Released Appraisal,” a template letter from USDA Rural Development to accompany a released property appraisal, explaining that the recipient can contact the State Appraiser with concerns, and that the appraisal may be accepted, revised, modified, or rejected during review.

nodes.title: COVER LETTER FOR RELEASED APPRAISAL
nodes.summary:
The partial document covers a USDA Rural Development appraisal release cover letter and the beginning of Chapter 8 on loan closing and project lease-up. It explains that a copy of the appraisal report is being provided, gives the borrower or applicant a contact for reviewing errors or deficiencies, and outlines the possible outcomes of the appraisal review: accepted, accepted with appraiser revisions, accepted with reviewer modifications, or rejected. It also notes that the review report is for internal Agency use and is not released.

The chapter introduction then outlines the overall loan closing process after loan approval, including preparing for closing, conducting closing, and completing post-closing tasks. It explains that documents are prepared before closing, executed at closing when ownership transfers to the borrower, and then filed and forwarded to loan servicing afterward. The section also introduces project lease-up responsibilities, stating that once construction is complete and the loan is closed, the Agency monitors the borrower’s compliance with rent and occupancy requirements through meetings and site visits. It begins the discussion of loan closing timing, noting the importance of moving quickly from approval to closing to reduce costs and use program funds efficiently.

nodes.title: INTRODUCTION
nodes.summary:
The passage introduces Chapter 8 of HB-1-3560, which explains the loan closing and project lease-up process for multi-family housing projects after a loan has been approved. It outlines the overall structure of the chapter, including: (1) loan closing activities, broken into preparation, the closing itself, and post-closing steps, and (2) Agency responsibilities during project lease-up, when the borrower begins renting units and the Agency monitors compliance with rent and occupancy requirements.

The excerpt also gives an overview of Section 1 on loan closing, noting that it covers timing, general requirements, and the roles of the borrower, the Agency, the closing agent, and Loan Processing Staff. It emphasizes that loan closing timing depends on the project’s financing method, such as interim financing or multiple advances. Finally, it stresses the importance of moving quickly from loan approval to loan closing because once a loan is obligated, interest costs begin to accrue, so construction and disbursement should proceed as efficiently as possible.

nodes.title: OVERVIEW
nodes.summary:
The partial document covers Chapter 8 of HB-1-3560, which explains the loan closing process and project lease-up for multi-family housing loans. It introduces the sequence of closing activities—preparing for closing, attending closing, and completing post-closing tasks—and explains that after construction is complete and the loan is closed, the borrower begins leasing up the project while the Agency monitors compliance with rent and occupancy requirements. The document also outlines the timing of loan closing based on financing type: interim financing closes after construction is finished, while multiple advances close before construction begins.

It further describes the main loan closing requirements, including the need for a borrower-approved and Agency-approved closing agent, execution of all required documents, and verification that all conditions from the loan approval or obligation letter have been satisfied. Key requirements include secure financing, completion of construction or sufficient funds for completion, adequate property security and lien position, and compliance with program and civil rights requirements.

nodes.title: TIMING OF LOAN CLOSING
nodes.summary: The partial document explains the timing and requirements for Agency loan closing in a housing or project financing context. It distinguishes between interim financing, where loan closing occurs after construction is complete, and multiple advances, where closing happens before construction begins. It then outlines loan closing requirements, stating that the Agency and borrower execute necessary documents, and a borrower-selected but Agency-approved closing agent must be present. Before closing, the borrower must prove that all program and property conditions have been met, including secured financing, completed construction or sufficient funds for completion, adequate property security and lien position, and compliance with program and civil rights requirements.

nodes.title: LOAN CLOSING REQUIREMENTS
nodes.summary: The partial document explains loan closing procedures for USDA Rural Development housing loans, focusing on the timing of closing relative to construction, the requirements that must be met before closing, and the roles of agency staff and closing agents. It describes how interim financing closes after construction is complete, while multiple-advance financing closes before construction begins. The document outlines loan closing requirements such as secured financing, completed or adequately secured construction, proper property security, and compliance with program and civil rights rules. It also summarizes pre-closing, closing, and post-closing activities, including document preparation, title review, funding disbursement, recording of the mortgage or deed of trust, and forwarding final files for servicing. Finally, it covers how a closing agent or title company is selected and details the closing agent’s responsibilities, including disclosure of section 514 loan and section 516 grant recipients and amounts on required settlement forms.

nodes.title: CHOOSING THE CLOSING AGENT AND TITLE COMPANY
nodes.summary: The partial document explains how a closing agent or title company is chosen for Agency real estate loans and outlines the closing agent’s responsibilities. It states that the borrower selects an attorney or title company, which must be approved by the Agency and chosen according to RD Instruction 1927-B. It also describes disclosure requirements intended to reduce fraud, waste, abuse, kickbacks, referral fees, and equity skimming: closing agents must identify the recipients and amounts of section 514 loans and section 516 grants on the HUD-1 Settlement Statement and on Form RD 3550-25, with attachments allowed if needed. Finally, it notes that the closing agent handles pre-closing and post-closing duties, with the full task list referenced in Exhibit 8-2.

nodes.title: CLOSING AGENT RESPONSIBILITIES
nodes.summary:
The partial document explains the loan closing process for Agency real estate loans, focusing on who may serve as the closing agent, what that agent must do, and the responsibilities of loan processing staff.

Main points covered:
- How a borrower selects a closing agent or title company, with Agency approval and compliance requirements.
- Closing agent responsibilities, including title clearance, preparation of settlement documentation, reporting section 514 loan and section 516 grant payments, and completing pre-closing, closing-day, and post-closing tasks.
- Specific pre-closing duties such as providing a title insurance binder, checking insurance, taxes, lien position, flood insurance, and ensuring required affidavits and closing conditions are met.
- Closing-day duties such as completing the closing statement, checking for judgments, reviewing and signing documents, and disbursing funds.
- Post-closing duties such as recording security documents, returning executed documents to the Agency, and obtaining title insurance.
- Loan processing staff responsibilities, especially the Loan Originator’s role in coordinating closing, working with applicants and the closing agent, obtaining closing instructions, and ensuring all loan conditions are satisfied before closing.
- Guidance for working with applicants so they understand closing requirements, documents needed, insurance obligations, and responsibilities at closing.
- Review of the title insurance binder to confirm ownership transfer, removal of prior liens, and resolution of judgments.

nodes.title: LOAN PROCESSING STAFF RESPONSIBILITIES
nodes.summary:
The partial document describes the responsibilities of loan processing staff, especially the Loan Originator, in preparing for and carrying out loan closing. It explains that the Loan Originator must coordinate closing so all conditions are met, work with the applicant and closing agent, and obtain closing instructions from OGC before closing can occur. It outlines key tasks such as educating the applicant about closing requirements, reviewing the title insurance binder to confirm ownership and clear title, preparing the loan docket for OGC review, and sending closing instructions and supporting documents to the closing agent.

The document also covers how to obtain the loan check through AMAS, including verifying loan balances and debt limits, and notes differences between interim financing and multiple advances. In addition, it explains establishing the borrower’s payment schedule and initial disbursement amount. Finally, it introduces the actions required at loan closing, emphasizing execution of all necessary documents to transfer ownership and secure the loan.

nodes.title: ACTIONS AT LOAN CLOSING
nodes.summary: The partial document explains procedures for loan disbursement, closing, and postclosing administration. It covers how loan checks are requested and issued for interim financing versus multiple advances, including use of AMAS screens to verify disbursed amounts, accrued interest, and the Maximum Debt Limit. It describes establishing the borrower’s monthly payment schedule and the initial disbursement process, including handling of retained funds in supervised accounts. The document then outlines required actions at loan closing, such as reviewing and executing documents and making any approved corrections. Finally, it details postclosing tasks: reviewing and filing closing documents, recording the mortgage or deed of trust, obtaining title insurance, and setting the amortization effective date that determines when interest accrual and repayments begin.

nodes.title: POSTCLOSING ACTIVITIES
nodes.summary: The document describes required loan closing and postclosing procedures. It covers reviewing and executing closing documents, making and initialing any changes to the security instrument or promissory note, and disbursing funds at closing. After closing, it explains how documents must be collected, reviewed, recorded, and filed. It also outlines the responsibilities of the closing agent and loan originator for obtaining proper lien priority, recording the mortgage or deed of trust, securing title insurance within 14 days, and establishing the amortization effective date that determines when interest and payment schedules begin.

nodes.title: Obtain and Review Closing Documents
nodes.summary: The partial document covers loan closing and post-closing procedures. It explains the required review and execution of closing documents, how corrections to security instruments or promissory notes must be made and initialed, and the disbursement of funds. It then outlines post-closing responsibilities, including obtaining and reviewing all closing documents, recording the mortgage or deed of trust, securing and delivering the title insurance policy, and establishing the amortization effective date (AED), which determines when interest accrues and the payment schedule begins.

nodes.title: Record Mortgage or Deed
nodes.summary: The partial document covers loan closing and postclosing procedures. It explains that at closing, all required documents must be reviewed and executed, any changes to security instruments or promissory notes must be clearly marked and initialed, and funds are disbursed as appropriate. After closing, it describes the required postclosing tasks: collecting and reviewing closing documents, confirming proper mortgage lien priority and execution, recording the mortgage or deed of trust, securing and delivering the title insurance policy, and establishing the Amortization Effective Date, which determines when interest accrues and the repayment schedule begins.

nodes.title: Secure Title
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines loan closing and postclosing procedures. It explains what must be reviewed and executed at closing, how changes to the promissory note or security instrument must be marked and initialed, and that funds are disbursed as appropriate. It then details postclosing responsibilities, including obtaining and reviewing all closing documents, confirming proper lien priority and execution, recording the mortgage or deed of trust, securing and delivering the title insurance policy, and establishing the Amortization Effective Date (AED), which determines when interest accrual and payment scheduling begin.

nodes.title: Establish the Amortization Effective Date
nodes.summary: The partial document explains the loan closing and postclosing procedures for HB-1-3560. It covers the required actions at closing, including reviewing and executing listed documents, handling any changes to security instruments or promissory notes, and disbursing funds. It then outlines postclosing responsibilities such as collecting and reviewing closing documents, ensuring the mortgage is properly recorded, securing and delivering the title insurance policy, and establishing the Amortization Effective Date (AED). The document also describes entering closing information into AMAS, assembling the loan docket for servicing staff, and updating the Multi-Family Housing Information System (MFIS).

nodes.title: Put Closing Information into AMAS
nodes.summary: This section outlines post-closing procedures for a loan. It explains that once all closing documents are complete, the Loan Originator must enter the closing information into AMAS using the Loan Closing screen. After the information is entered and documents are filed, the Loan Originator must assemble the loan closing documents into the loan docket and forward it to the appropriate servicing staff. It also states that the Loan Originator must enter the required information into the Multi-Family Housing Information System (MFIS).

nodes.title: Complete Loan Docket and Transfer to Servicing Staff
nodes.summary: This section outlines the post-closing workflow for a loan. It explains that once all closing documents are complete, the Loan Originator must enter closing information into AMAS using the Loan Closing screen. After that, the originator should assemble the full loan docket, file all necessary closing documents, and transfer the docket to the appropriate servicing staff for дальнейшее loan servicing. It also states that the Loan Originator must enter the relevant loan information into the Multi-Family Housing Information System (MFIS).

nodes.title: Populate Multi -Family Housing Information System (MFIS)
nodes.summary: The partial document describes post-closing procedures and early project lease-up requirements for a multifamily housing loan program. It explains that once closing documents are complete, the Loan Originator must enter closing information into AMAS, assemble the loan docket, forward it to servicing staff, and populate the MFIS system. It then shifts to Section 2 on project lease-up, stating that after loan closing the applicant becomes a borrower subject to management, occupancy, rent, subsidy, and financial management rules. The borrower must begin affirmative fair housing marketing at least 90 days before rental activity and comply with program requirements as units are leased. The document also outlines monitoring responsibilities during lease-up, including coordination between Loan Processing and Loan Servicing Staff to ensure affirmative marketing, occupancy eligibility, and correct rents, with field staff reviewing project worksheets and tenant certifications to verify compliance.

nodes.title: PROJECT LEASE -UP REQUIREMENTS
nodes.summary: This partial document explains the project lease-up phase for a housing loan program. It states that once the loan is closed, the applicant becomes a borrower and must follow requirements for project management, occupancy, rents, subsidies, financial management, and affirmative fair housing marketing. Marketing must begin at least 90 days before rental activity, and borrowers must carefully ensure compliance with rent and occupancy rules as units are leased. The Agency provides guidance, closely monitors early lease-up, and refers readers to HB-2-3560 and the relevant regulations in 7 CFR part 3560. The document also outlines monitoring responsibilities during lease-up, including coordination between loan processing and servicing staff, review of project worksheets and tenant certifications, and verification that affirmative marketing, occupancy eligibility, and correct rent charges are being properly followed.

nodes.title: MONITORING PROJECT LEASE -UP
nodes.summary:
The partial document explains the project lease-up phase for a housing loan program and the borrower’s responsibilities after loan closing. It focuses on complying with rent, occupancy, subsidy, financial management, and affirmative marketing requirements, including starting marketing at least 90 days before rental activity. It also describes how the Agency monitors lease-up through project worksheets, tenant certifications, and close coordination between loan processing and servicing staff.  

A major section covers early project oversight activities, especially the pre-occupancy conference, the 90-day visit, and the 11-month warranty inspection. The pre-occupancy conference is used to review management documents, initial operating capital requirements, tenant eligibility and grievance procedures, and civil rights/affirmative fair housing obligations. The text also lists key regulations and forms used in managing and monitoring project operations.

nodes.title: Pre- Occupancy Conference
nodes.summary:
The partial document describes early project monitoring requirements for housing projects, focusing on three key events: the pre-occupancy conference, the 90-day visit, and the 11-month warranty inspection. It explains that the pre-occupancy conference, held before lease-up begins, is used to review management documents, initial operating capital, tenant eligibility and grievance procedures, and civil rights/affirmative fair housing obligations. It emphasizes required forms, posters, and language-access responsibilities.

The document then outlines the 90-day visit, which occurs shortly after occupancy begins and is meant to verify that lease-up is progressing properly and that program rules are being followed. During this visit, the Loan Originator should review tenant files, the marketing plan, the waiting list, use of the borrower’s capital contribution, and financial management systems to ensure compliance and proper project operation.

nodes.title: The 90- Day Visit
nodes.summary: The partial document describes post-occupancy oversight requirements for rural housing projects, focusing on fair housing and civil rights compliance, borrower obligations, and early project monitoring. It explains that required posters and the approved marketing plan must be displayed and provided to the borrower, and that the borrower must collect demographic data and make reasonable efforts to provide materials in other languages when needed. It then outlines the 90-day visit, which assesses lease-up progress and compliance with program rules by reviewing tenant files, the marketing plan, the waiting list, borrower operating capital use, financial management, and project maintenance. Finally, it covers the 11-month warranty inspection, a physical inspection conducted before the builder’s warranty expires to identify defects, maintenance issues, and other property condition concerns, with monitoring tracked through MFIS.

nodes.title: The 11- Month Warranty Inspection
nodes.summary:
The partial document covers two main topics related to rural housing project administration and loan closing.

First, it discusses the 11-month warranty inspection. This inspection is conducted about eleven months after project completion to verify the condition of items covered by the builder’s one-year warranty, ensure repairs are completed before the warranty expires, and review the property’s overall physical and maintenance condition. It also notes that this inspection should be tracked through the MFIS.

Second, it provides an attachment listing the loan closing documents required before and at closing. These include the promissory note, interest credit and rental assistance agreement, mortgage, financing statements or security agreements if needed, title insurance, organizational papers, affidavits, evidence of tax payment, required insurance policies, deeds, and loan closing instructions. It also lists additional items that must be completed by loan closing, such as meeting conditions from approval letters, depositing initial operating capital, making the initial equity contribution, and certifying the availability or unavailability of other government assistance.

nodes.title: LOAN CLOSING DOCUMENTS
nodes.summary: The partial document covers two main topics from the USDA Rural Development handbook. First, it lists the loan closing documents and items that must be prepared and submitted before or at closing, including the promissory note, mortgage, insurance, title evidence, organizational papers, deeds, and required certifications and financial evidence. Second, it begins Chapter 9 on project construction, explaining that construction management is the borrower’s responsibility but the Agency has an oversight role. It outlines the Agency’s construction review process, including review of plans and specifications, coordination on environmental mitigation, approval or concurrence on contractors and contracts, required preconstruction conferences, inspections during construction, change orders, contractor payments, and final close-out procedures.

nodes.title: INTRODUCTION
nodes.summary:
This partial document outlines Chapter 9 of HB-1-3560, which covers project construction. It explains that while the borrower is responsible for managing construction, the Agency oversees the process to ensure compliance with project plans, specifications, and government security interests. The chapter is organized into three main sections: construction requirements, contracting procedures, and the Agency’s responsibilities during construction and close-out.

It also describes the Agency’s role in the construction process, beginning with review of planning documents and extending through final inspection. The State Architect reviews construction documents, advises on design, specifications, and cost estimates, and coordinates on environmental mitigation measures. The Agency also concurs in contractor selection and the construction contract, requires a preconstruction conference, and during construction reviews inspections, change orders, and contractor payments before final contract close-out.

nodes.title: OVERVIEW OF THE AGENCY’S ROLE IN THE CONSTRUCTION PROCESS
nodes.summary:
The partial document covers Chapter 9 on project construction, focusing on the Agency’s role in overseeing construction for rural development housing projects. It explains that construction management is the borrower’s responsibility, but the Agency monitors compliance with plans and specifications and protects the government’s security interest through review, inspection, and approval processes.

Main points include:
- An overview of the Agency’s construction oversight role, from initial application through final inspection and closeout.
- The State Architect’s responsibilities in reviewing construction documents, advising on design/specification acceptability, and coordinating with environmental requirements.
- The requirement for a preconstruction conference and the Agency’s concurrence in contractor selection, construction contracts, change orders, and contractor payments.
- A reminder that the borrower’s project architect remains responsible for ensuring construction follows approved plans and specifications.
- Construction financing methods, including interim financing and multiple advances, with preference for interim financing when possible.
- Requirements related to identity-of-interest relationships, including disclosure, cost comparison, and contractor cost certification.

nodes.title: CONSTRUCTION FINANCING
nodes.summary: The partial document explains key aspects of construction financing and oversight for a project. It first notes the architect’s role in monitoring construction to ensure work matches approved plans and specifications, including inspections, payment certifications, and change order approvals. It then describes two construction financing methods: interim financing, where a lender funds construction and the Agency closes its loan after completion, and multiple advances, where the Agency releases funds as work progresses and the borrower must show interim financing is not available on acceptable terms. Finally, it addresses identity-of-interest relationships in the construction process, requiring disclosure to the Agency, cost comparisons to similar non-IOI projects, and contractor certification of actual costs.

nodes.title: IDENTIFY- OF-INTEREST RELATIONSHIPS
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines key construction requirements for USDA Rural Development multifamily housing projects. It explains two construction financing options: interim financing, where an outside lender funds construction before the Agency closes its loan, and multiple advances, where the Agency disburses funds as work progresses. It also covers identity-of-interest relationships, requiring disclosure, favorable cost comparison, and contractor cost certification when such relationships exist. The section then describes construction standards, including compliance with RD instructions, state and local codes, thermal performance, land/site development standards, and accessibility requirements such as UFAS, FHA/AG, and ADA/AG. Finally, it introduces methods of construction, emphasizing competitive bid contracts as the default approach, with contracts awarded to the lowest responsible bidder, subject to Agency approval, contractor licensing, debarment checks, and bonding requirements.

nodes.title: CONSTRUCTION STANDARDS
nodes.summary: The document section outlines construction requirements for Rural Development multi-family housing projects. It explains the required construction standards, including compliance with Agency standards, thermal performance requirements, state and local codes, land and site development rules, and accessibility standards such as UFAS, FHA/AG, ADA/AG, and any applicable local requirements. It also states that the project architect must certify final plans and specifications using Form RD 1924-25 before loan approval. The section then introduces methods of construction, identifying competitive bid, negotiated bid, and owner-builder as the three options. It describes the competitive bid process in detail, noting that it is the default method unless an exception is granted, that contracts are awarded to the lowest responsible bidder after public notice, and that the contractor, contract price, licensing, debarment status, and bonding requirements must all meet Agency approval.

nodes.title: METHODS OF CONSTRUCTION
nodes.summary: The document section outlines construction requirements for Multi-Family Housing projects under HB-1-3560. It explains the applicable construction standards, including compliance with USDA RD instructions, agency-adopted state development standards, thermal performance standards, and all relevant state and local codes. It also emphasizes that site and land development must meet RD Instruction 1924-C requirements and that the project architect must certify compliance with construction and accessibility standards before loan approval using Form RD 1924-25. The section further describes the allowed methods of construction—competitive bid, negotiated bid, and owner-builder—with a detailed focus on competitive bidding. It states that construction contracts are generally awarded through competitive bid to the lowest responsible bidder, subject to Agency concurrence, contractor licensing, debarment restrictions, and bonding requirements.

nodes.title: Competitive Bid
nodes.summary: The partial document explains construction requirements for Multi-Family Housing projects, including the required construction standards, compliance with federal, state, and local codes, land and site development rules, and architect certification of plans and accessibility compliance. It also outlines the three approved methods of construction—competitive bid, negotiated bid, and owner-builder—and details the competitive bid process, including public notice, award to the lowest responsible bidder, Agency concurrence, contractor licensing and debarment checks, and bonding requirements.

nodes.title: Negotiated Contract
nodes.summary: The partial document explains two alternative construction contracting arrangements for borrowers: negotiated contracts and owner-builder construction. It states that borrowers may request an exception from competitive bidding for a negotiated contract when they want a specific contractor or when bidding does not produce an acceptable result, but approval depends on conditions such as fair cost compared with local market construction, contractor reliability and licensing, and proper documentation of the request. It also describes owner-builder construction, where the borrower and construction company are the same entity, noting that the owner-builder must be qualified and licensed, there is no overall construction contract, and separate contracts may still be used for trade work like plumbing and electrical. The section further notes that agency staff review owner-builder requests using drawings, specifications, and cost estimates, and that borrowers must certify final construction costs. Finally, it introduces contractor requirements and briefly contrasts competitive bidding and negotiated contracts based on market conditions.

nodes.title: Owner -Builder [RD Instruction 1924.13(e)(2)]
nodes.summary: The partial document explains construction contracting options and requirements for borrowers under HB-1-3560. It covers when a negotiated contract may be approved as an exception to competitive bidding, including the need for favorable cost comparison, a licensed and reliable contractor, and documented reasons for the exception, with requests following RD Instruction 1924-A. It also describes the owner-builder method, where the borrower and construction company are the same entity, requiring the borrower to be qualified and properly licensed, with no main construction contract but separate subcontract agreements for trades like plumbing and electrical work. The section notes that the Loan Originator and State Architect/Engineer must review owner-builder requests and that final construction costs must be certified. It begins a broader contractor requirements section and briefly discusses how market conditions can affect whether negotiated bids or competitive bids are more cost-effective.

nodes.title: CONTRACTOR REQUIREMENTS
nodes.summary:
The excerpt outlines construction contracting requirements and exceptions under HB-1-3560. It explains when borrowers may use a negotiated contract instead of competitive bidding, including the need for favorable pricing, a qualified and licensed contractor, and documented reasons for the exception. It also describes the owner-builder arrangement, where the borrower and construction entity are the same, requiring borrower qualification, local licensing, agency review, and certification of final costs.

The document then details contractor requirements the Agency must verify, including debarment/suspension certification for bids over $25,000, required property and liability insurance, and payment and performance bonds equal to 100% of the contract amount. It notes that interim lenders may waive bonding in writing and that an irrevocable letter of credit may be used instead of bonds, with specific conditions to secure completion of the construction.

nodes.title: Debarment and Suspensions
nodes.summary:
The document outlines contractor requirements for construction projects. It covers:

- **Debarment and suspension checks:** Contractors bidding over $25,000 must certify they are not debarred or suspended from federal programs, and the agency must independently verify this.
- **Insurance requirements:** Contractors must carry property and liability insurance, and the contract is not valid until proof of insurance is received.
- **Payment and performance bonds:** Before construction begins, contractors must provide bonds equal to 100% of the construction contract, obtained from approved surety companies; interim lenders may waive this in writing.
- **Letters of credit as an alternative:** An unconditional, irrevocable letter of credit may be accepted instead of bonds, provided it matches the contract amount and remains valid through final acceptance of the work.

nodes.title: Insurance
nodes.summary: The document section outlines contractor requirements for construction projects, covering four main areas: debarment and suspension certification, insurance, payment and performance bonds, and letters of credit. It explains that contractors bidding over $25,000 must certify they are not debarred or suspended, and agencies must verify this independently. It also states that contractors must provide proper property and liability insurance before a construction contract becomes valid. In addition, it details the requirement for payment and performance bonds equal to 100% of the contract value, including approved surety sources and a possible written waiver by an interim lender. Finally, it describes how an unconditional, irrevocable letter of credit can be used instead of bonds, with requirements for amount, duration, and coverage of contractor defaults and completion costs.

nodes.title: Payment and Performance Bonds
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines contractor financial and compliance requirements for construction contracts. It covers debarment and suspension certification for bids over $25,000, including the Agency’s duty to verify contractor status. It explains insurance requirements for property and liability coverage before a contract becomes valid. It details payment and performance bond requirements, including bond amount, approved sureties, and a possible written waiver by an interim lender. Finally, it describes the use of an unconditional, irrevocable letter of credit as an alternative to bonds, including required terms and duration.

nodes.title: Letters Of Credit
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines key construction contracting requirements for a project. It explains that bidders must certify they are not debarred or suspended from federal programs, and the agency must verify contractor eligibility. It then covers insurance requirements, stating that property and liability insurance must be in place before a construction contract becomes valid. The document also discusses payment and performance bonds, including their required coverage, acceptable surety sources, and possible waiver if interim financing is involved. As an alternative to bonds, it describes conditions for using an irrevocable letter of credit, including credit quality standards, beneficiary requirements, term length, and agency approval. Finally, it addresses contractor warranties and compliance with civil rights laws, including accessibility and disability-related requirements.

nodes.title: Warranties
nodes.summary: The partial document describes requirements related to construction project financing and compliance under HB-1-3560. It explains the standards for an irrevocable letter of credit, including credit quality ratings, required amount coverage, term length, beneficiary designation, and approval of the form by the Agency. It also lists acceptable credit ratings from different rating agencies in Exhibit 9-1. In addition, it covers warranty obligations, stating that the contractor must provide a 12-month builder’s warranty using Form RD 1924-19 when the project is ready for occupancy. Finally, it outlines civil rights compliance requirements that the applicant or borrower must follow, including laws such as the Architectural Barriers Act, Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act, and the Americans with Disabilities Act.

nodes.title: Compliance with Civil Rights Requirements
nodes.summary: The document section covers construction project requirements related to letters of credit, warranties, civil rights compliance, and cost certification. It explains that an irrevocable letter of credit must meet specific credit quality standards, cover 100% of the construction contract, name Rural Development, USDA as sole beneficiary, last at least one year, and be approved in form by the Agency. It also outlines warranty requirements, including a 12-month builder’s warranty at occupancy, and lists key civil rights laws and regulations the applicant/borrower must follow. Finally, it describes when cost certification is required, especially in cases involving an identity-of-interest, and notes that actual construction costs must be certified and audited according to agency and government standards.

nodes.title: COST CERTIFICATION
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses housing and construction compliance requirements, including references to the Fair Housing Act, FHA/AG, Executive Order 11246, and state or local requirements. It then focuses on Section 9.8, Cost Certification, explaining when certifications of actual construction costs are required, especially in cases involving an IOI or when deemed necessary by the Agency. The section notes that cost certification must follow a specific regulation, requires reporting direct costs on Form RD 1924-13, and may involve CPA audit under Government audit standards. It also outlines the Agency’s ongoing review of construction costs from initial estimates through final payment, and summarizes the loan processing staff’s role in comparing estimates, reviewing Form RD 1924-13, checking payment requests and change orders, and verifying final payments and cost certification.

nodes.title: COMPETITIVE BIDDING PROCEDURES
nodes.summary: The partial document explains the rules and process for competitive bidding in contracting procedures, emphasizing that the borrower is responsible for proper bidding under RD Instruction 1924-A, while the Agency only provides oversight. It describes the purpose of competitive bidding as achieving the lowest acceptable construction cost through open and fair competition, and notes that if no acceptable bids are received, the contract may be rebid or negotiated with Agency approval. The document also covers what must be included in the bidding package, how invitations to bid should be distributed publicly to ensure broad contractor access, and the Agency’s role at bid openings. Finally, it outlines the required materials the borrower must submit for Agency review, including a bid summary and a revised final cost breakdown.

nodes.title: Contents of the Bidding Package
nodes.summary: This section explains the competitive bidding procedures for construction contracts, including the borrower’s responsibility to follow RD Instruction 1924-A and the architect’s role in managing the bid process. It emphasizes that the Agency only provides oversight to ensure proper procedures and that bidding must be open, fair, and competitive to achieve the lowest acceptable construction cost. It also covers what must be included in the bidding package, how invitations to bid should be distributed to interested parties and the public, and what happens if no acceptable bids are received. Finally, it outlines the bid opening process and the Agency’s concurrence requirements, including the documents the borrower must submit for review, such as the bid summary and revised final cost breakdown.

nodes.title: Distribution of the Bidding Packages
nodes.summary: The partial document explains USDA Rural Development contracting procedures for competitive bidding and Agency concurrence in contracts. It covers the borrower’s responsibility to follow proper bidding rules, the project architect’s role in managing the bid process, and the Agency’s oversight function. It describes the purpose of competitive bidding as achieving the lowest acceptable construction cost through open and fair competition, with options to rebid or negotiate if no acceptable bids are received. The document also outlines what must be included in the bidding package, how bid invitations must be distributed publicly, and the process for bid openings and Agency review, including submission of a bid summary and revised final cost breakdown.

nodes.title: AGENCY CONCURRENCE IN THE CONTRACT
nodes.summary:
This excerpt describes the competitive bidding and contract approval procedures for a project. It explains that the borrower is responsible for proper bidding under RD Instruction 1924-A, while the project architect typically manages the bid invitation and selection process and the Agency only provides oversight. The document emphasizes that competitive bidding must promote open and free competition to secure the lowest acceptable construction cost, and if no acceptable bids are received, the borrower may rebid or seek a negotiated bid with Agency approval.

It also outlines what must be included in the bidding package, which is prepared by the project architect, and explains how bid invitations must be distributed to ensure all interested parties have an opportunity to bid, including public notice through newspapers or plan services. Finally, it covers Agency concurrence in the contract, noting that an RD representative attends bid openings as an observer only, and lists the materials the borrower must submit for Agency review, including a bid summary and a revised final cost breakdown on Form RD 1924-13.

nodes.title: Review of the Bid
nodes.summary:
The partial document explains the Agency’s rules for competitive bidding and contract approval in housing construction projects. It covers the borrower’s responsibility to follow RD bidding procedures, the architect’s role in preparing and managing bid documents, and the Agency’s oversight role. It explains that competitive bidding is meant to secure the lowest acceptable construction cost through open competition, and describes what happens if no acceptable bids are received.

It also outlines what must be included in the bidding package and how bid invitations must be distributed to ensure public notice and fair access to bidders. For Agency concurrence in the contract, it describes the bid-opening process, the role of the RD representative, and the specific documents the borrower must submit for review, including bid summaries, cost breakdowns, architect recommendations, and bid selection documentation. The staff review focuses on bid completeness, cost sufficiency, and justification for the selected bid.

Finally, it discusses acceptable construction contract forms, required nondiscrimination language, contract execution and concurrence procedures, and the need to retain all construction-related documents in the borrower’s file.

nodes.title: Contract Documents
nodes.summary: The partial document explains the review and approval process for construction bid awards and contract documents in a loan or project file. It covers what bid documentation must include, how staff should verify that the selected bid is properly completed and financially supported, and how the architect must justify any decision not to choose the lowest bid. It also describes acceptable construction contract forms, including AIA and agency-approved forms, the required nondiscrimination language, and the process for signing and concurring in the contract. Finally, it notes that the agency is not a party to the contract but must ensure compliance, and that all construction-related documents must be kept in the borrower’s construction file.

nodes.title: PRECONSTRUCTION CONFERENCE
nodes.summary:
The partial document covers two main construction administration topics for USDA Rural Development projects:

1. **Preconstruction Conference**
   - Requires all interested parties to attend after the contract is awarded and executed.
   - Explains that the meeting is used to clarify responsibilities and expectations before construction begins.
   - Identifies who should attend, including the borrower, contractor, architect, lender representatives, and an Agency representative, with the project architect leading.
   - Lists key agenda items such as party responsibilities, scope of work, wage and EEO/OSHA requirements, site use rules, material protection, environmental mitigation, inspection and payment procedures, schedule requirements, contract documents, dispute resolution, and closeout procedures.
   - Includes an exhibit listing the standard contract documents that may be part of the project package.

2. **Notice to Proceed**
   - Defines the Notice to Proceed as the document that authorizes the contractor to start construction.
   - States that it marks the official start date for the contract timeline.
   - Explains that it is issued by the borrower after the contract is executed and the preconstruction conference is complete.
   - Notes that it must wait until the Agency loan is closed or interim financing has been approved by the interim lender.

nodes.title: NOTICE TO PROCEED
nodes.summary: The document section explains the purpose and issuance of the Notice to Proceed, which authorizes construction to begin, sets the contract start date, and is issued only after the contract is executed and the preconstruction conference is completed. It also covers construction oversight through progress inspections, emphasizing that while the borrower is responsible for construction quality, the Agency must inspect regularly to protect its investment. The section describes inspection timing, including monthly inspections and three key milestone inspections: before or during footings and slabs, when the building is enclosed but structural and rough-in work is still visible, and at final completion before occupancy. It also identifies who performs inspections, including the project architect, the Agency representative, and optionally the interim lender.

nodes.title: PROGRESS INSPECTIONS
nodes.summary: The passage explains the Agency’s construction oversight role during project development, focusing on progress inspections. It states that although the borrower is responsible for construction quality, the Agency must inspect regularly to protect its investment. Inspections are required before partial payments, typically monthly, and include three key stages: an initial inspection during footings/slab work, an inspection when the building is enclosed but structural and mechanical rough-ins remain visible, and a final inspection when the project is complete and ready for occupancy. It also describes who performs inspections: the project architect as the primary inspector, an Agency representative with MFH construction knowledge, and the interim lender when applicable. The section emphasizes coordination among these parties, especially around partial payment requests.

nodes.title: Timing of Inspections
nodes.summary: The document section explains construction oversight for multi-family housing projects, focusing on progress inspections. It states that the borrower is responsible for construction quality, but the Agency must conduct periodic inspections to protect its investment. It outlines when inspections must occur, including monthly inspections and three key milestone inspections: before concrete is placed, when the building is enclosed with rough-ins visible, and at final completion before occupancy. It also describes who performs inspections, emphasizing the project architect’s primary role, with participation from the Agency representative and sometimes the interim lender. Finally, it details inspection procedures, including using the official inspection form, documenting discrepancies, verifying work completed before payments, checking environmental mitigation compliance, conducting pre-final inspections for occupancy readiness, issuing a Certificate of Substantial Completion and punch list, and keeping signed copies of all inspection reports in the construction file.

nodes.title: Inspection Procedures
nodes.summary:
The partial document covers construction inspection procedures and change order management for Agency-financed projects. It explains that actual work must be inspected against signed plans and specifications, with discrepancies documented, corrected, or otherwise resolved. The Agency inspector is required to use Form RD 1924-12 and record conditions, work completed, stored materials, environmental mitigation compliance, and pre-final occupancy readiness; all inspection reports must be signed and retained in the construction file.

The document also outlines how change orders are handled. It states that any post-contract changes affecting design, materials, cost, scope, or schedule must be documented using Form RD 1924-7 or an equivalent, and Agency written concurrence must be obtained before work proceeds. It notes that changes generally should not exceed 20 percent of the original contract cost, may require bond increases if they do, and must include cost breakdowns. Finally, it describes possible ways to fund added costs, including borrower funds, deductive change orders, or in rare cases a subsequent Agency loan, along with related restrictions and limits.

nodes.title: CHANGE ORDERS
nodes.summary: The partial document explains procedures for managing construction contract changes and interim payments in Rural Development projects. It covers when change orders are required, who must approve them, documentation and cost breakdown requirements, limits on the size of changes, how cost increases or decreases are handled, and when environmental review documents are or are not needed. It also describes partial payment procedures, including the need to ensure contractor payments are properly applied to labor and materials, required forms and certifications, and the process for submitting payment requests.

nodes.title: PARTIAL PAYMENTS
nodes.summary: The document section covers rules for construction cost increases and decreases, including when change orders can be approved and how funding must be handled. It then explains procedures for partial payments to contractors, including required forms, review and approval steps, retainage, and treatment of approved change orders. It also describes how multiple advance checks are obtained and deposited, the need to monitor the maximum debt limit, and the process for contract close-out after work is completed and all claims are released.

nodes.title: OBTAINING THE CHECK FOR MULTIPLE ADVANCES
nodes.summary: The partial document explains procedures for construction payment requests and contract close-out in an Agency-financed project. It emphasizes that payment request cost breakdowns must match the approved cost estimate, must be reviewed and verified by the architect and borrower, and may include change orders only if Agency-approved. It also describes retainage requirements, including withholding enough funds until work is complete and obtaining surety consent before releasing retainage. The document then outlines how the Agency processes approved payment requests, including ordering checks, depositing them in a supervised account, releasing funds as work is completed, and monitoring the maximum debt limit. Finally, it covers contract close-out, stating that all work must be completed to acceptable standards and all payments and claims must be settled before the contract is closed.

nodes.title: CONTRACT CLOSE -OUT
nodes.summary: The partial document explains procedures for construction payment requests, including matching cost breakdowns to the approved estimate, verifying requests through site inspection by the architect and borrower, handling approved change orders, and allowing the Agency to approve or revise partial payments. It also covers retainage requirements, including withholding a percentage until work is completed and accepted, and the need for surety consent before release of retainage. In addition, it describes how to obtain checks for multiple advances after Agency approval, how funds are deposited and released, the need to monitor the loan’s maximum debt limit, and the contract close-out process, where all work, payments, and claims must be fully resolved and inspected for compliance with the contract.

nodes.title: Final Inspection
nodes.summary:
The partial document explains the procedures and requirements for final inspection and final payment on a construction project. It says the project architect should coordinate a final inspection with all relevant parties, including the Agency and interim lender inspector if applicable, and that the inspection must occur before final payment and, if interim financing is used, before the Agency loan is closed. The document describes when a final inspection is considered complete, including completion of all punch-list items, correction of all deficiencies, and acceptance by local authorities when required.

It also outlines the documents needed before final payment can be made, such as contractor and claimant release forms, builder’s warranty, acceptance by public authority, final certificate of payment, and cost certification if applicable. Finally, it notes that if some work cannot be completed due to weather or other extenuating circumstances, funds may be withheld to ensure completion of the remaining work.

nodes.title: Final Payment
nodes.summary:
The excerpt covers two main topics in HB-1-3560:

1. Final inspection and final payment procedures for construction projects
