It explains that, beyond standard underwriting procedures, the Loan Originator must assess the market for this type of housing, the adequacy and long-term availability of tenant support services, and the quality of project management. It emphasizes market feasibility documentation, especially whether there is demand for the specific services offered and for meal plans, since poor planning can harm financial viability. It also details tenant service requirements, stating that services must be needed, affordable, and sustainable, and that service costs must be reasonable compared with the market. If costs are too high and cannot be reduced or substituted with community or volunteer options, the project should be rejected as infeasible.

nodes.title: B. Tenant Services
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses underwriting and design requirements for congregate housing projects under HB-1-3560. It explains that loan originators must underwrite these loans using standard procedures while also evaluating the special market for congregate housing, the availability and adequacy of tenant support services, and the quality of project management. It emphasizes that market feasibility documentation must show demand for the specific services offered, including meal plans, and that these services must be well planned so facilities are used effectively. The document also requires tenant services to be needed, affordable, and sustainable, with costs reduced or the project rejected if services are not feasible. In addition, it addresses management expectations, stating that the proposed management company should have experience in special senior housing or obtain added expertise if needed. Finally, it covers design considerations, requiring project plans to meet program criteria, support tenant use and efficient operations, and be reviewed by the State Architect.

nodes.title: C. Management
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses requirements for managing and designing special senior housing projects, especially congregate housing. It explains that the Loan Originator must verify that the proposed management company has relevant experience, and if not, the applicant must obtain additional management expertise, such as a consultant. It also covers design review responsibilities, stating that project plans must meet specified design criteria, be practical and accessible for tenants, and support efficient operations. Examples include ensuring common facilities are easy to reach and service areas are separated from public spaces. Finally, it notes that plans should be reviewed by a State Architect to confirm compliance with program requirements.

nodes.title: 11.13 DESIGN CONSIDERATIONS
nodes.summary:
The partial document covers management and design requirements for congregate housing and then introduces rules for group homes under multi-family housing loans. It explains that the loan originator must verify that the management company has experience with special senior housing or require additional expertise, such as a consultant, if needed. It also states that project plans must meet design criteria, be convenient and functional for tenants, and be reviewed by the State Architect.

The document then outlines group homes, describing them as shared living arrangements for elderly or disabled individuals who can manage basic daily living but need support from a resident assistant. It emphasizes that group homes are not healthcare facilities, though limited medical assistance may be allowed. The section also covers eligibility rules, including tenant qualification, the use of legal guardians to sign leases when necessary, compliance with all applicable laws, and the requirement that any affiliated group home remain a separate, independent entity. Finally, it notes that applicants must demonstrate that needed support services will be available on a continuous, long-term basis, whether provided by the project, a public agency, or a nonprofit organization.

nodes.title: SECTION 2: GROUP HOMES [7 CFR 3560.69]
nodes.summary: The partial document explains the rules and procedures for Section 2 group homes under HB-1-3560. It defines group homes as shared living arrangements for individuals who can manage basic daily living but need assistance from a trained resident assistant, and notes they are not healthcare facilities. It covers project eligibility, including tenant restrictions for specific groups such as elderly, developmentally disabled, or mentally impaired tenants, as well as the need to verify tenant eligibility and allow legal guardians to sign leases when necessary. It also states that group homes must comply with all applicable federal, state, and local laws, and may be affiliated with another organization only if they remain a separate, independent entity. Finally, it describes support service requirements, emphasizing that applicants must demonstrate the availability of adequate long-term support services through the project, public agencies, or qualified nonprofit organizations.

nodes.title: 11.14 OVERVIEW
nodes.summary:
The partial document explains the rules for Section 2 on group homes under HB-1-3560. It defines what a group home is, emphasizing that it provides shared living for people who can manage basic daily living but need guidance or assistance from a trained resident assistant. It notes that group homes may serve elderly or disabled individuals, may restrict occupancy to certain eligible groups, and are not intended to function as healthcare facilities even though limited medical assistance may be provided.

The document then covers project eligibility requirements. It explains that group homes may limit tenancy to specific populations such as elderly, developmentally disabled, or mentally impaired tenants, and that prospective tenants must be evaluated for eligibility. It also states that a legal guardian may sign a lease on behalf of a tenant who cannot legally enter a contract, that all applicable federal, state, and local laws must be followed, and that a group home may be affiliated with another organization only if it remains a separate, independent entity.

Finally, it describes support service requirements. Loan applicants must demonstrate that needed support services will be available on a continuous and long-term basis. These services may be provided by the project itself, a public agency, or an established nonprofit organization.

nodes.title: 11.15 PROJECT ELIGIBILITY
nodes.summary: The partial document explains the rules for group homes under HB-1-3560. It defines group homes as shared living arrangements for people who can handle basic self-care but need a trained resident assistant, and notes they are intended for elderly or disabled residents rather than as healthcare facilities. It then outlines project eligibility requirements, including tenant restrictions, evaluation of prospective tenants, the role of legal guardians in signing leases, and the need to comply with all applicable laws and regulations. It also explains that a group home may be affiliated with another organization but must remain a separate, independent entity. Finally, it covers support service requirements, stating that applicants must demonstrate the long-term availability of adequate services, which may be provided by the project, a public agency, or a qualified nonprofit organization.

nodes.title: A. Tenancy
nodes.summary:
The partial document explains the rules and procedures for Group Homes under HB-1-3560. It begins with an overview of what group homes are: shared living arrangements in rental projects for people who can live independently in basic daily activities but need a trained resident assistant for direction or limited help. It notes that group homes may serve elderly or disabled individuals and are not intended to operate as healthcare facilities.

The document then covers project eligibility requirements. It explains that a group home may restrict occupancy to certain tenant groups, such as elderly, developmentally disabled, or mentally impaired tenants. Applicants must assess whether prospective tenants meet the eligibility criteria, and legal guardians may sign leases for tenants who cannot legally contract themselves. It also states that group homes must comply with all applicable federal, state, and local laws. In addition, a group home may be affiliated with another organization, but it must remain a separate, independently functioning entity.

Finally, the section addresses support services. It requires loan applicants to demonstrate that the necessary support services for tenants will be available on a continuous, long-term basis. These services may be provided by the project itself, a public agency, or an established nonprofit organization.

nodes.title: B. Applicable Laws
C. Association with Another Organization
nodes.summary:
The document section explains the rules and procedures for group homes under HB-1-3560, including how they differ from other multifamily housing loans. It defines group homes as shared living arrangements for individuals who can manage basic daily living but need assistance from a trained resident assistant, often for elderly or disabled tenants. It emphasizes that group homes are not healthcare facilities, though limited medical help like medication assistance may be provided.

The section also covers project eligibility, including tenancy restrictions for specific groups, tenant qualification requirements, and the role of legal guardians in signing leases when tenants lack legal capacity. It states that group homes must comply with all applicable federal, state, and local laws and may be affiliated with another organization only if they remain independent. Finally, it explains that applicants must demonstrate the availability of adequate support services on a long-term basis, which may be provided by the project, a public agency, or an established nonprofit organization.

nodes.title: 11.16 SUPPORT SERVICES
nodes.summary: This section explains the rules for group homes under the rural housing loan program. It defines group homes as shared housing for elderly or disabled residents who can care for themselves but need some direction or assistance from a trained resident assistant, and notes that they are not healthcare facilities. It covers project eligibility, including tenant restrictions, tenant screening, legal guardians signing leases when needed, compliance with applicable laws, and requirements for being a separate entity if affiliated with another organization. It then describes support service requirements, emphasizing that applicants must demonstrate long-term availability of needed services and provide a service plan covering meals, transportation, social/recreational activities, and access to medical care. The section also outlines design requirements for group homes, such as residential character, common areas, accessibility, emergency lighting, and compliance with accessibility standards and other regulations. Finally, it notes that rent and rental assistance for group homes are determined according to the referenced attachment.

nodes.title: 11.17 DESIGN REQUIREMENTS
nodes.summary: The partial document explains requirements for group home loan projects under HB-1-3560. It covers the need for a detailed service plan showing how residents will receive affordable support services for independent living, including meals, transportation, recreation, and medical access, along with a government agency statement supporting tenant service needs. It then outlines design requirements for group homes, emphasizing residential character, common areas, accessibility, emergency lighting, and compliance with accessibility and disability standards. The section also describes how rents and rental assistance are determined for group homes, including rules for shared units and shared bedrooms. Finally, it details application and processing requirements, stating that applicants must submit standard multifamily housing documentation plus the service plan and agency statement, and it explains underwriting considerations such as verifying market demand, application completeness, and the group home’s ability to operate independently if affiliated with another organization.

nodes.title: 11.18 RENTS AND RENTAL ASSISTANCE
nodes.summary: The excerpt explains rental assistance rules for group home units, including shared bedrooms and rent limits. It then outlines application requirements and processing for group home loans, stating that applicants must provide standard Multi-Family Housing documentation plus a service plan and a government support narrative. The section also describes the review and approval workflow from the Loan Originator to the National Office and State level. Finally, it covers underwriting considerations, emphasizing that loans must meet general lending standards and that the lender must verify complete documentation, the group home’s independence if affiliated with another organization, and the existence of a viable market for the services offered.

nodes.title: 11.19 APPLICATION REQUIREMENTS AND PROCESSING
nodes.summary: The document explains group home rental assistance and space definitions, including how shared bedrooms are treated and the condition that total rent must stay within comparable local rents. It then outlines application requirements and processing for group home loans, stating that applicants must provide standard Multi-Family Housing documentation plus a service plan and a government support narrative. The processing steps are described from initial review by the Loan Originator through National Office concurrence and State-level completion, with special approval required if the loan exceeds delegated authority. Finally, it covers underwriting considerations, emphasizing that loans must meet standard underwriting rules and that the Loan Originator must verify complete documentation, the group home’s independence if affiliated with another organization, and the existence of a market for the services offered.

nodes.title: 11.20 UNDERWRITING CONSIDERATIONS
nodes.summary:
The passage explains key rules for group home housing units and then focuses on loan application and underwriting requirements. It defines what constitutes a unit in a group home and describes when Agency rental assistance may be provided to tenants sharing a unit, including limits in shared-bedroom situations based on comparable local rents.

It then outlines the application requirements for loans to complete a group home, stating that applicants must submit the standard Multi-Family Housing documentation plus a service plan and a government narrative support statement. The section also describes the processing steps, including initial review by the Loan Originator, possible rejection if the proposal is not eligible or feasible, and escalation to the National Office and State level for concurrence and final processing in certain cases.

Finally, the text covers underwriting considerations, emphasizing that group home loans must meet general loan standards while also being evaluated for market demand, tenant/service needs, organizational independence, required documentation, experienced management, and whether the group home design meets specified criteria.

nodes.title: SECTION 3: MARKET FEASIBILITY CONSIDERATIONS FOR CONGREGATE HOUSING AND GROUP HOMES
nodes.summary: This section explains the additional market feasibility documentation required for congregate housing and group homes. It says the market feasibility study must demonstrate the need for both the housing and the specific services offered, especially for elderly tenants. The document should gather information from local aging agencies or surveys if needed, and it may use an expanded market area only under limited rural conditions, with clear evidence that enough tenants can be drawn from that area. It also requires income data from the Social Security Office, demographic information for older age groups, discussion of alternative service providers, and an analysis of demand for meal services since meal service is a key part of congregate housing feasibility.

nodes.title: 11.21 ADDITIONAL MARKET FEASIBILITY DOCUMENTATION REQUIREMENTS [7 CFR 3560.69(f )]
nodes.summary:
The partial document covers two main topics from HB-1-3560:

1. **Additional market feasibility documentation for congregate housing and group homes**
   - Explains what the market feasibility document (MFD) must include for projects serving specific tenant groups, especially elderly tenants.
   - Requires documentation of the need for both housing and the proposed services.
   - Discusses when an expanded market area may be used and what proof is needed to show enough tenants will support the project.
   - Calls for income data from Social Security sources, demographic data for older age groups, analysis of alternative service providers, and evidence of demand for meal services.

2. **Manufactured housing program requirements**
   - Introduces manufactured housing as a type of multifamily housing eligible for Agency loans.
   - Describes manufactured housing characteristics and notes that these loans are underwritten at the State level with final National Office approval.
   - Outlines applicant and project eligibility rules, including that the applicant must be the first owner, units must be new and unoccupied, projects must have at least two units, refinancing is generally not allowed, and rehabilitation is limited to Agency-financed units and should be performed by experienced individuals.

nodes.title: SECTION 4: MANUFACTURED HOUSING [7 CFR 3560.70]
nodes.summary: The partial document contains only an instruction requesting a description of the document’s main points, but it does not include any substantive content to summarize.

nodes.title: 11.22 OVERVIEW
nodes.summary: The excerpt covers the USDA Multi-Family Housing rules for manufactured housing projects. It explains what qualifies as manufactured housing, how these loans are handled and approved, and notes that they are underwritten at the State level but require final National Office approval. It also outlines key eligibility requirements for both applicants and projects, including that the buyer must be the first owner, the homes must be new and unused, the project must contain at least two units, refinancing is generally not allowed except for construction loan takeout, and rehabilitation is only permitted for Agency-financed units and by experienced professionals.

nodes.title: 11.23 ELIGIBILITY REQUIREMENTS
nodes.summary: This section explains USDA Multi-Family Housing loans for manufactured housing projects. It defines manufactured housing, notes that these loans compete with other multi-family housing projects and are underwritten at the State level with final National Office approval, and emphasizes that offsite construction creates extra design and warranty requirements. It also covers eligibility rules: the applicant must be the first owner buying the homes for purposes other than resale, and the project must meet specific conditions such as having at least two units, using homes that are new and less than one year old, and not being for refinancing except to replace a construction loan. The section also states that rehabilitation loans are allowed only for Agency-financed manufactured home projects and must be handled by experienced professionals to protect structural integrity.

nodes.title: A. Applicant Eligibility
nodes.summary: This section explains USDA/Rural Development rules for Multi-Family Housing loans involving manufactured housing. It defines manufactured housing, notes that loans compete for Multi-Family Housing funding and are underwritten at the state level with final approval from the National Office, and emphasizes that while most underwriting rules are the same as for other projects, additional offsite-construction design and warranty requirements apply. It also outlines eligibility requirements: the applicant must be the first owner buying the homes for use, not resale, and the project must meet specific conditions such as using new, unoccupied units less than one year old, containing at least two housing units, not being for refinancing except to replace a construction loan, and allowing rehabilitation only for existing Agency-financed projects and by experienced rehabbers.

nodes.title: B. Project Eligibility
nodes.summary:
This section explains the USDA Rural Development rules for multifamily loan projects involving manufactured housing. It defines manufactured housing, notes that these loans are underwritten at the State level but require National Office final approval, and says most processing rules are similar to other multifamily projects with added design and warranty requirements.

It then covers eligibility requirements, including that the borrower must be the first purchaser, the homes must be new and unoccupied, the project must include at least two units, refinancing is generally not allowed except to replace construction financing, and rehabilitation is only allowed under certain conditions for existing Agency-financed projects.

The section also outlines design standards, such as minimum size and width, permanent foundation and anchoring requirements, thermal performance standards, compliance with HUD manufactured home safety standards, and the need for a well-planned residential site layout with adequate recreational space for families.

Finally, it states the special warranty requirement that the seller must provide the manufacturer’s warranty to the borrower and identify each unit by serial number.

nodes.title: 11.24 DESIGN REQUIREMENTS
nodes.summary: The partial document covers requirements for manufactured housing projects, including the possibility of redeveloping existing manufactured housing property by replacing it with new manufactured homes. It then outlines design standards for manufactured housing, such as minimum floor space and width, permanent foundations, anchoring, thermal performance, and compliance with HUD and Agency construction standards. The document also emphasizes good site planning, attractive and functional residential design, and the need for adequate recreational space for family-oriented projects. Finally, it states special warranty requirements, requiring sellers to provide the manufacturer’s warranty to the borrower and identify the units by serial number.

nodes.title: 11.25 SPECIAL WARRANTY REQUIREMENTS
nodes.summary:
The partial document outlines USDA Rural Development requirements for manufactured housing projects. It explains that proposals may involve purchasing existing manufactured housing property for redevelopment, then details design requirements such as minimum unit size, permanent foundations, anchoring, thermal performance, and compliance with HUD and Agency standards. It emphasizes good site planning, attractive and functional residential design, and adequate recreational space for family use.

The document also covers special warranty requirements, stating that sellers and contractors must provide warranties covering the manufactured home, foundation, installation, transportation damage, and proper joining of double-wide units. It then explains loan terms, limiting amortization to the lesser of the security’s economic life or 30 years. Finally, it describes processing, underwriting, and closing requirements, including verifying eligibility, design compliance, warranties, and taking a mortgage or deed of trust on the entire property.

nodes.title: 11.26 RATES AND TERMS
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines requirements for manufactured housing loans, including warranty obligations, loan terms, underwriting, and closing procedures. It explains that the contractor or dealer-contractor must provide a warranty confirming the manufactured home, foundation, anchoring, and improvements comply with approved plans, were not damaged in transit, and that the manufacturer’s warranty supplements other borrower protections. It also states that loan amortization and term cannot exceed the lesser of the security’s economic life or 30 years. The section on loan processing and underwriting requires lenders to follow general guidance, verify project eligibility, construction/design compliance, and required warranties before approval. Finally, the loan closing section begins by requiring a mortgage or deed of trust on the entire property purchased or improved with the loan.

nodes.title: 11.27 LOAN PROCESSING AND UNDERWRITING
nodes.summary: The partial document covers requirements for manufactured housing loans and projects, including warranty standards, loan term limits, underwriting steps, and closing conditions. It explains that the contractor or dealer-contractor must provide a warranty covering conformity with approved plans, absence of hidden transportation damage, proper joining of double-wide units, and preservation of other borrower rights. It also states that loan terms cannot exceed the lesser of the economic life of the security or 30 years. For loan processing and underwriting, the loan originator must verify project eligibility, confirm home age, coordinate with the State Architect on construction/design compliance, and ensure required warranties are available before final approval. Finally, it notes that loan closing must follow chapter guidance and requires a mortgage or deed of trust on the entire property.

nodes.title: 11.28 LOAN CLOSING
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines USDA Rural Development loan requirements for manufactured housing and rural cooperative housing. It explains warranty obligations for manufactured homes, including conformity with plans, absence of transport damage, and warranty protections. It also covers loan rates and terms, stating that amortization cannot exceed the lesser of the security’s economic life or 30 years. The section on loan processing and underwriting describes additional eligibility, design, and warranty verification steps needed before approval. Loan closing requirements include securing a mortgage or deed of trust on the full property, obtaining appropriate title insurance or attorney opinion, and ensuring the property is taxed as real estate when permitted. The document then introduces rural cooperative housing, describing its ownership and operation by a consumer cooperative, limited equity structure through patronage capital accounts, and the joint State Office and National Office underwriting process. Finally, it explains cooperative housing eligibility and organizational rules, including the role of the Board of Directors and membership protections for eligible residents.

nodes.title: SECTION 5: RURAL COOPERATIVE HOUSING
nodes.summary: The partial document explains the basics of Rural Cooperative Housing under HB-1-3560, including how consumer cooperatives own, operate, and manage the housing at cost. It describes the limited-equity structure, where members do not build home equity but instead accumulate funds in patronage capital accounts. The section also outlines the funding and underwriting process, noting that cooperative projects are funded from National Office resources and reviewed jointly by the State and National Offices, with final approval made by the National Office. In addition, it covers eligibility and organizational requirements, stating that the consumer cooperative is the loan applicant, that it must have a board of directors elected from members, and that members who qualify initially cannot lose membership or tenancy later if their income changes.

nodes.title: 11.29 OVERVIEW
nodes.summary: The document section provides an overview of rural cooperative housing and explains how it differs from other multi-family housing programs. It describes cooperative housing as multi-family housing owned, operated, and managed by a consumer cooperative made up of eligible members who live in the housing at actual cost. Any excess income is credited to members’ patronage capital accounts, giving members limited equity rather than ownership equity in their homes. It also explains the funding and underwriting process, noting that cooperative housing projects are financed from funds held at the National Office and reviewed jointly by the State Office and National Office, with the National Office making the final approval decision. The section then covers eligibility and organizational requirements, stating that the consumer cooperative is the loan applicant, that it has a Board of Directors elected from its members, and that all members must meet specified organizational and eligibility standards. It also notes that once admitted, a member cannot lose membership or tenancy solely because they later no longer meet the original income eligibility requirements.

nodes.title: 11.30 ELIGIBILITY AND ORGANIZATIONAL REQUIREMENTS
nodes.summary:
The partial document explains USDA Rural Development rules for rural cooperative housing. It defines cooperative housing as multi-family housing owned and managed by a consumer cooperative, with members operating the project at cost and building limited equity through patronage capital accounts rather than home equity. It describes the unique funding and underwriting process, where cooperative projects are financed from National Office funds and reviewed jointly by the State Office and National Office.

The section also covers eligibility and organizational requirements for the consumer cooperative, its members, and the Board of Directors. It states that the cooperative must be legally organized, operate solely for members’ benefit, restrict membership to eligible persons, and have a board elected from the membership. Members must generally be very low, low, or moderate income, live in the cooperative, pay a membership fee, and agree to uphold the cooperative concept. Once admitted, members cannot lose tenancy just because their income later exceeds original eligibility limits.

Finally, the text addresses market feasibility and application requirements. Market feasibility is demonstrated through a list of prospective members, and a professional market study is not required. It also notes that loan applicants must provide additional documentation beyond standard multifamily housing application requirements.

nodes.title: 11.31 MARKET FEASIBILITY
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines eligibility and organizational requirements for a consumer cooperative loan applicant, its members, and its board of directors. It explains that a cooperative must be legally organized under state or federally recognized tribal cooperative laws, operate housing on a cooperative, at-cost basis for members, and limit membership to eligible persons with some flexibility in special circumstances. Members must live in the cooperative housing, meet income requirements when admitted, pay and maintain a membership fee, and sign documents supporting the cooperative structure. The board of directors must come from the membership, include at least five members, maintain a membership list, and oversee the cooperative’s rules and management. The document also covers market feasibility, stating that feasibility is demonstrated by confirmed prospective members rather than a professional market study, and notes that applicants must submit additional application requirements beyond those for other multifamily housing projects.

nodes.title: 11.32 APPLICATION REQUIREMENTS
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines eligibility, organizational, market feasibility, application, and management requirements for consumer cooperative housing loan applicants. It explains who can form a cooperative, who may be members, and the responsibilities and composition of the Board of Directors. It also describes how market feasibility is demonstrated through interested prospective members rather than a full market study. In addition, it lists the documents and certifications required at the initial and final application stages, including budgets, member interest statements, income and expense statements, fair housing marketing plans, limited equity agreements, and subscription agreements. Finally, it emphasizes that the cooperative board has ultimate responsibility for management and that special management considerations apply when underwriting cooperative housing loans.

nodes.title: 11.33 MANAGEMENT REQUIREMENTS
nodes.summary:
The partial document outlines key requirements for cooperative housing applications and management. It first describes what must be provided at the initial application stage, including prospective member information, a management reserve budget, certification of member interest and understanding of cooperative responsibilities, and financial statements proving ability to meet occupancy and debt obligations. It then lists final application requirements such as an Affirmative Fair Housing Marketing Plan, a contingency plan for professional management if the cooperative cannot self-manage, and executed limited equity and subscription agreements.

The document then shifts to management requirements for cooperative housing loans. It explains that the cooperative’s Board of Directors has ultimate responsibility for governance, policy enforcement, and decisions affecting occupancy rates and membership rights. The board must also provide prospective members with information about the cooperative’s structure, financial obligations, and limited equity arrangements.

Finally, it describes the role of an adviser to the Board during formation and early operation, including selection criteria, Agency approval, compensation considerations, and ongoing monitoring. It also notes the importance of committees, requiring the cooperative to define committee responsibilities, functions, and provide this information to prospective members.

nodes.title: A. Cooperative Board of Directors
nodes.summary: The partial document explains the governance structure of a cooperative housing project. It covers the role and responsibilities of the Cooperative Board of Directors, including setting and enforcing policies, managing rule violations, and informing prospective members about the cooperative’s obligations and limited equity arrangement. It also describes the role of an external adviser to the Board during formation and early operation, including selection criteria, Agency approval, monitoring duties, and possible compensation. Finally, it discusses the need for committees within the cooperative, outlining that responsibilities, committee structure, and functions must be defined and shared with prospective members.

nodes.title: B. Adviser to the Board
nodes.summary: The document explains the governance structure and early management of a cooperative housing project. It covers the role of the Cooperative Board of Directors, including setting and enforcing policies, making key decisions such as occupancy rate increases or termination of membership, and providing prospective members with information about the cooperative’s obligations and limited equity arrangements. It also describes the role of an outside adviser to the Board during formation and the transition to self-management, including selection criteria, Agency approval, monitoring responsibilities, and possible compensation. Finally, it discusses the use of committees, noting that the cooperative must define committee responsibilities, list the committees, and explain their functions to prospective members.

nodes.title: C. Committees
nodes.summary: The document section explains how a cooperative housing project should be governed and managed. It describes the role of the Cooperative Board of Directors, including setting and enforcing policies, approving member obligations, and informing prospective members about the cooperative and limited equity requirements. It then outlines the role of an outside adviser to the board during formation and early operations, including selection criteria, agency approval, and possible compensation. The section also discusses the use of committees, encouraging member participation and board training as key to successful self-management. Finally, it introduces acceptable management options for cooperatives, including self-management, partial self-management, and contract management, emphasizing ongoing education and the possible need for outside services or professional management if self-management is not successful.

nodes.title: D. Acceptable Forms of Management
nodes.summary: The passage explains that a cooperative’s board must be trained to understand how the cooperative operates before it can effectively manage the organization. It emphasizes board member education, committee participation by members, and the need for strong member involvement to support self-management. It also lists examples of possible committees, such as maintenance, groundskeeping, communications, budget and finance, rules, recreation, and home service. The text then introduces acceptable management structures for cooperative housing: self-management, partial self-management, and contract management. It further describes self-management as the preferred goal for many cooperatives, requiring ongoing education, budgeted training expenses, and sometimes outside help such as bookkeeping support during the early stages.

nodes.title: 1. Self-Management
nodes.summary:
The excerpt explains how a cooperative should be prepared to manage itself and what management options are acceptable. It emphasizes that prospective Board members need education and training before taking responsibility, and that members should voluntarily serve on committees such as maintenance, groundskeeping, communications, budget and finance, rules, recreation, and home service, with additional participation expected if needed. It then outlines three management models for cooperative housing: self-management, partial self-management, and contract management. The text notes that self-management is the preferred goal, but outside assistance such as a bookkeeper or adviser may be needed during the cooperative’s early years. If self-management is not working, the cooperative may use partial self-management or hire professional contract management, with the Agency involved in deciding when this is necessary.

The passage also describes how management costs are handled through a management reserve and patronage account system. Monthly management charges are collected as part of occupancy costs and held in reserve; if the cooperative successfully manages itself, unused funds are later transferred to patronage accounts and credited to members. If professional management is hired because self-management fails, reserve funds are used to pay for it and patronage accruals are suspended, with occupancy charges increased if needed. It also states that rental assistance cannot be used for members’ contributions to the management reserve, though full-time professional management may qualify as a shelter cost eligible for rental assistance.

nodes.title: 2. Partial Self-Management
nodes.summary: The passage explains management options for a cooperative, including partial self-management and full contract management, and notes that the Agency may decide if professional management is needed. It then describes how management reserves and patronage accounts are funded and used: monthly management charges are set aside in a reserve, can be transferred to members’ patronage accounts if the cooperative manages successfully, and are redirected to pay for professional management if needed. It also states that member contributions to the management reserve cannot come from Agency rental assistance unless full-time professional management makes the fee part of eligible shelter cost.

nodes.title: 3. Contract Management
nodes.summary:
The passage explains cooperative management options and how management costs are handled financially. It covers three management arrangements: full self-management with an adviser, partial self-management using outside technical help, and contract management with professional day-to-day supervision. It also explains that the Agency may decide when professional management is necessary if agreement cannot be reached.

The second main topic is the management reserve and patronage account system. A monthly management fee is built into occupancy charges and held in a reserve account. If the cooperative successfully manages itself, unused reserve funds are transferred annually to members’ patronage accounts. If professional management is needed, reserve funds pay for it and patronage accruals are suspended. The text also notes that rental assistance cannot be used for members’ contributions to the management reserve, though full-time professional management fees may become eligible as part of shelter cost.

nodes.title: E. Management Reserves and Patronage Accounts
nodes.summary:
The partial document explains management options for a cooperative housing project, focusing on how self-management may be supported, replaced, or supplemented by professional management. It outlines three levels of management: full self-management with adviser assistance, partial self-management using contracted technical or supervisory services, and full contract management with a professional management agent. It also describes how the Board of Directors and the Agency decide when professional management is needed.

The document then discusses management reserves and patronage accounts. It explains that a monthly management fee is built into occupancy charges and held in a reserve account. If the cooperative manages successfully, unused funds are transferred at year-end to members’ patronage accounts as credits. If professional management becomes necessary because of poor self-management, reserve funds are used to pay for it and patronage accruals are suspended. It also states that members must pay their share of the management reserve from their own resources, not rental assistance, unless full-time professional management is required, in which case the fee may be counted as part of shelter cost and eligible for Agency rental assistance.

nodes.title: 1. Paying for Management
nodes.summary: The partial document covers cooperative housing management and membership requirements, including options for self-management, partial self-management, and contract management when professional oversight is needed. It explains how management reserves and patronage capital accounts are funded, tracked, and distributed, and notes that rental assistance cannot be used for the management reserve unless full-time professional management becomes a shelter cost. It also describes the occupancy agreement that all members must sign, the cooperative membership fee requirements and payment terms, and the loan processing procedures for cooperative housing applications, including eligibility review, funding availability checks, and referral for authorization or rejection with appeal rights.

nodes.title: 2. Patronage Capital Accounts
nodes.summary: The partial document describes key rules and procedures for cooperative housing programs. It explains how patronage capital accounts and management reserves are handled, including recordkeeping, IRS-compliant distribution, and treatment of inflation equity when a member leaves. It also outlines the occupancy agreement that all cooperative members must sign, defining rights, obligations, monthly charges, and other occupancy terms. In addition, it covers the cooperative membership fee, requiring it to equal one month’s occupancy charge, be applied uniformly, and allow installment payments under specified conditions. Finally, it summarizes the loan processing process for cooperative housing applications, including how applications are solicited, reviewed for funding availability, evaluated for eligibility and feasibility, and forwarded for further approval or rejected with appeal rights.

nodes.title: F. Occupancy Agreement
nodes.summary: This partial document describes key requirements for cooperative housing programs, focusing on how financial and membership matters are handled. It explains patronage capital accounts, including how year-end income and unused management reserves are assigned, recordkeeping for each member’s share, IRS-compliant patronage distributions, and treatment of inflation equity. It also outlines the occupancy agreement that every cooperative member must sign, covering member rights, responsibilities, monthly charges, and housing terms. In addition, it sets rules for the cooperative membership fee, which must equal one month’s occupancy charge, be applied uniformly, and may be paid over time with a minimum initial payment. Finally, it summarizes the loan processing procedure for cooperative housing, including solicitation through a NOFA, funding from an administrative reserve, application review order, eligibility and feasibility checks, and forwarding acceptable proposals to the National Office.

nodes.title: 11.34 COOPERATIVE MEMBERSHIP FEE
nodes.summary: The partial document covers key rules and procedures for cooperative housing programs. It explains how patronage capital accounts are handled, including assigning unused year-end income and management reserves as patronage capital, keeping records of member contributions, and following IRS rules for patronage distributions. It also describes occupancy agreements, which all cooperative members must sign to define rights, obligations, and occupancy charges. In addition, it outlines the cooperative membership fee requirement, stating that the fee must equal one month’s occupancy charge, be applied uniformly, and may be paid over time with no interest under certain conditions. Finally, it describes the loan processing procedure for cooperative housing applications, including solicitation through a NOFA, review order, funding availability checks, eligibility and feasibility review, and forwarding acceptable proposals to the National Office.

nodes.title: 11.35 LOAN PROCESSING
nodes.summary: The partial document explains key rules and procedures for cooperative housing projects. It covers patronage capital accounts, including how year-end income and management reserves are assigned, recordkeeping requirements, and how inflation equity is treated when a member leaves. It also describes the occupancy agreement that all cooperative members must sign, outlining member and cooperative rights, obligations, and monthly charges. In addition, it sets the cooperative membership fee policy, requiring a fee equal to one month’s occupancy charge with installment options under certain conditions. Finally, it outlines the loan processing, closing, and post-closing procedures for cooperative housing loans, including application submission, eligibility and feasibility review, National Office authorization, and use of standard loan closing and construction monitoring procedures.

nodes.title: 11.36 LOAN CLOSING AND POST -CLOSING ACTIVITIES
nodes.summary: The partial document explains the steps that follow an eligibility and feasibility determination in the loan process. It states that, if the National Office authorizes it, the Leadership Designee informs the applicant that the application appears eligible and feasible, after which the Loan Originator invites the applicant to submit a final application. It also notes that final applications are processed and loan approval is obtained using Chapter 5 procedures. The section then introduces loan closing and post-closing activities, stating that Loan Originators must follow Chapters 8 and 9 for closing the loan, monitoring construction, and overseeing initial project occupancy.

nodes.title: A. Cooperative Board of Directors
nodes.summary: The partial document explains the final steps in the loan application and approval process. It states that if the National Office authorizes it, the Leadership Designee informs the applicant that the application appears eligible and feasible, after which the Loan Originator invites submission of a final application. It also notes that Loan Originators must follow Chapter 5 procedures to process final applications and secure loan approval. The section then introduces loan closing and post-closing activities, directing Loan Originators to Chapters 8 and 9 for closing the loan, monitoring construction, and overseeing initial project occupancy.

nodes.title: B. Adviser to the Board
nodes.summary: This excerpt outlines the steps that follow an initial determination of eligibility for a loan application. If the National Office authorizes it, the Leadership Designee informs the applicant that the application appears eligible and feasible, after which the Loan Originator invites the applicant to submit a final application. It also notes that final applications are processed under Chapter 5 procedures to obtain loan approval. The section then introduces loan closing and post-closing activities, stating that Loan Originators must follow Chapters 8 and 9 for closing the loan, monitoring construction, and overseeing initial project occupancy.

nodes.title: C. Committees
nodes.summary: The partial document explains loan processing and closing steps, then introduces an attachment focused on congregate housing and group homes. It outlines the purpose and objective of this housing guidance, which is to provide affordable shelter plus separately managed services based on demonstrated market need. It defines key terms such as congregate housing, group home, service agreement, service contract, service plan, and tenant base. It also notes that subsidy cannot be used to pay for services in congregate housing or group homes.

nodes.title: ATTACHMENT 11 -A MANAGEMENT OF CONGREGATE HOUSING AND GROUP HOMES
nodes.summary: The document is an attachment to USDA HB-1-3560 that sets additional rules for managing congregate housing and group homes. It explains the purpose and objective of these housing types, emphasizing that housing and services must be treated as separate, affordable components based on market need and resident resources. The document defines key terms such as congregate housing, group home, service agreement, service contract, service plan, and tenant base. It also notes that rent subsidy cannot be used to pay for services in congregate housing or group homes.

nodes.title: I. PURPOSE:
nodes.summary:
The document is an attachment from HB-1-3560 that sets additional rules for managing congregate housing and group homes under 7 CFR Part 3560. It explains the purpose and objective of these housing types: to provide shelter and separately identified supportive services that are affordable to tenants, while allowing residents to meet any additional medical, personal, or service needs on their own through their own resources.

It defines key terms such as congregate housing, group home, service agreement, service contract, service plan, and tenant base. These definitions clarify the types of housing, the role of service providers, and the written documents required to describe services and responsibilities. The document also notes that rent subsidy cannot be used to pay for services in congregate housing or group homes.

nodes.title: II. OBJECTIVE:
nodes.summary: The document is an attachment to HB-1-3560 that sets additional management requirements for congregate housing and group homes. It explains the purpose and objective of these housing types, emphasizing that housing and supportive services must be treated as separate components and kept affordable for the tenant base. It defines key terms such as congregate housing, group home, service agreement, service contract, service plan, and tenant base. It also states that the rent subsidy described in the referenced paragraph cannot be used to pay for services in congregate housing or group homes.

nodes.title: III. DEFINITIONS:
nodes.summary: The document is an attachment to HB-1-3560 that sets additional management requirements for congregate housing and group homes under USDA rural housing rules. It explains the purpose and objective of these housing types: to provide shelter and separately arranged supportive services that are affordable to tenants, while allowing residents to meet any additional medical, discretionary, or personal service needs through their own resources. The document defines key terms such as congregate housing, group home, service agreement, service contract, service plan, and tenant base. It also states that certain rent subsidies cannot be used to pay for services in congregate housing or group homes.

nodes.title: IV. RENT SUBSIDY OPPORTUNITIES:
nodes.summary:
The document outlines special management requirements for congregate housing and group homes under HB-1-3560. It explains the purpose and objective of these housing types: to provide affordable shelter plus separately arranged supportive services based on identified market need, while allowing residents to meet any additional medical or personal service needs on their own.

It defines key terms such as congregate housing, group home, service agreement, service contract, service plan, and tenant base. It also states that rent subsidies cannot be used to pay for services in these housing types.

The main operational section requires borrowers to follow general USDA housing rules and submit a service plan describing how services will be provided. It emphasizes documenting borrower and management experience, especially if the provider is inexperienced and needs outside assistance.

The management plan must address several specific areas unique to congregate and group home housing, including:
- tenant mix,
- marketing plan,
- service packages,
- referral services,
- tenant consultation,
- and emergency evacuation planning.

Overall, the partial document focuses on how these housing projects should be planned, managed, and operated to ensure proper service delivery, tenant support, and safety.

nodes.title: V. MANAGEMENT OPERATIONS:
nodes.summary: The document section focuses on management operations requirements for congregate housing and group homes under 7 CFR Part 3560. It explains that borrowers must submit a service plan describing how services will be provided and document relevant experience, especially if they are unfamiliar with congregate or group home settings. It also details what a management plan must include for these types of housing, such as the intended tenant mix, marketing approach, service packages offered, referral services and community linkages, tenant consultation procedures, and emergency evacuation planning.

nodes.title: A. Borrower's experience.
nodes.summary: This section explains management requirements for congregate housing and group homes under 7 CFR Part 3560. It says borrowers must submit a service plan showing how services will be delivered and, if they lack experience, must seek help from qualified organizations or individuals. The management plan must address the unique operation of these housing types, including tenant mix, marketing strategies, service packages, referral services, tenant consultation, and emergency evacuation planning.

nodes.title: B. Management plan.
nodes.summary: The partial document describes management requirements for congregate housing and group homes under 7 CFR Part 3560. It explains that borrowers must submit a service plan and outlines what the management plan should cover, including the borrower’s or management agent’s experience, the need for outside assistance if they lack experience, and documentation of service provider capability. It also details key plan elements such as tenant mix, marketing strategy, service packages, referral services, tenant consultation, and emergency evacuation planning, emphasizing how the project will support and coordinate services for tenants.

nodes.title: 1. Tenant mix.
nodes.summary: The document section outlines management requirements for congregate housing and group homes under 7 CFR Part 3560. It says borrowers must submit a service plan explaining how services will be provided and document the experience and capability of themselves or their management agents. It also details what the management plan must cover, including tenant mix, marketing strategies, service packages, referral services, tenant consultation procedures, and emergency evacuation planning.

nodes.title: 2. Marketing plan.
nodes.summary: This excerpt explains management operation requirements for congregate housing and group homes under 7 CFR Part 3560. It says borrowers must submit a service plan and demonstrate experience or obtain expert assistance if they are new to these housing types. The management plan must address the unique needs of congregate/group home projects, including tenant mix, marketing, service packages, referral services, tenant consultation, and emergency evacuation planning.

nodes.title: 3. Service package.
nodes.summary: The document section outlines management operations requirements for congregate housing and group homes under 7 CFR Part 3560. It explains that borrowers must submit a service plan describing how services will be provided and document relevant experience in operating such housing. The management plan must cover tenant mix, marketing strategies, service packages, referral services, tenant consultation procedures, and emergency evacuation planning, with emphasis on how the project will manage the unique needs of congregate or group home residents.

nodes.title: 4. Referral service.
nodes.summary: The document section outlines management operation requirements for congregate housing and group homes under 7 CFR Part 3560. It explains that borrowers must submit a service plan showing how services will be provided, and it requires borrowers or management agents to demonstrate relevant experience or obtain assistance if they are new to this type of housing. It also details what the management plan must cover for these specialized housing projects, including tenant mix, marketing strategy, service packages, referral services, tenant consultation, and emergency evacuation planning.

nodes.title: 5. Tenant consultation.
nodes.summary: The partial document explains management operations requirements for congregate housing and group homes under 7 CFR Part 3560. It says borrowers must submit a service plan showing how services will be provided, and it emphasizes documenting the borrower’s or management agent’s experience with these types of housing. The management plan must address several key areas specific to congregate/group home projects: tenant mix, marketing strategies, service packages, referral services, tenant consultation, and emergency evacuation planning.

nodes.title: 6. Emergency evacuation plan.
nodes.summary:
This excerpt outlines requirements for managing and leasing congregate housing and group homes under USDA Rural Development rules. It covers:

- **Management operations**: Borrowers must follow 7 CFR Part 3560 and submit a service plan describing how services will be delivered.
- **Borrower experience**: The management plan must document the borrower’s or management agent’s experience with congregate/group home housing, or show that outside experienced assistance was obtained.
- **Management plan content**: The plan must address key project-specific items such as:
  - tenant mix,
  - marketing strategy,
  - service package options,
  - referral services,
  - tenant consultation,
  - emergency evacuation planning.
- **Service plan and agreements**: Borrowers must provide a service plan, and separate service agreements for any services not directly provided by the borrower. These agreements must specify the service, cost, and duration.
- **Service contract review**: A sample service contract must be submitted for review and must comply with Fair Housing and Agency requirements; it cannot be required as a condition of occupancy and must be separate from the lease.
- **Renting procedure**: The section also explains tenant eligibility and selection rules, including compliance with general occupancy rules and special guidance for choosing tenants in congregate housing, especially regarding the intended tenant mix and service needs.

nodes.title: C. Service plan.
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines requirements for congregate housing and group home projects, focusing on service-related documentation and tenant selection procedures. It explains that borrowers must submit a service plan, service agreements for services not provided directly, and sample service contracts for Agency review, with specific rules about duration, authorization, and separation from occupancy leases. It then covers renting procedures, including eligibility standards for tenants, compliance with federal housing regulations, and guidance for tenant selection. A key point is the congregate housing tenant mix: projects should primarily serve tenants needing service packages, but may include tenants with fewer or no services if necessary, with the project management plan specifying the proportion and Rural Development approval required.

nodes.title: D. Service agreements.
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines requirements for congregate housing and group home projects, focusing on service documentation and tenant admission procedures. It explains that borrowers must submit a service plan, service agreements for any services not provided directly, and sample service contracts for Agency review, with requirements that these documents comply with management plan authorization rules, Fair Housing Act standards, and occupancy restrictions. It then begins the renting procedure section, stating that tenants must meet eligibility and housing provisions under federal regulations, and that borrowers must follow proper tenant selection rules when offering congregate housing or group homes. The document also discusses congregate housing tenant mix planning, emphasizing that projects should be designed to serve tenants needing varying levels of service packages, with the proposed mix described in the management plan and subject to Rural Development approval.

nodes.title: E. Service contract.
nodes.summary: The document outlines requirements for congregate housing and group home borrowers related to service planning, service agreements, and service contracts. It explains that borrowers must submit a service plan, with specific rules about how it relates to the management plan and signatures when the service provider is not the borrower or agent. It also requires separate service agreements for services not provided directly, including details such as the service, cost, and duration, and specifies that these agreements must last at least one year. In addition, borrowers must submit a sample service contract for Agency review to ensure compliance with Fair Housing Act and Agency requirements, and the contract cannot be tied to occupancy and must be separate from the lease. The document then begins the renting procedure section, covering eligible tenants and tenant selection rules, including how borrowers must follow general occupancy provisions and evaluate applicants for congregate or group home housing. It further discusses tenant selection for congregate housing, especially tenant mix, emphasizing that projects should serve tenants needing service packages, how service-package capacity should be described in the management plan, and that Rural Development must approve the proposed tenant mix.

It explains that borrowers must submit a service plan, service agreements for any services not directly provided, and sample service contracts for agency review, with clear rules about signatures, authorization, duration, and separation from lease agreements. It then begins the “Renting Procedure” section, describing who is eligible to be a tenant, how borrowers must follow federal housing provisions when assessing applicants, and how tenant selection should work for congregate housing, including guidance on tenant mix, service-package planning, and Rural Development approval.

nodes.title: A. Eligible tenants.
nodes.summary: The document section outlines requirements for congregate housing and group home projects, focusing on service planning and tenant procedures. It explains that borrowers must submit a service plan and service agreements for services not directly provided, with rules on duration, authorization, and how these documents relate to management plans and agreements. It also requires a sample service contract for agency review, ensuring compliance with Fair Housing Act and Agency rules, and states that the service contract cannot be required for occupancy or included with the lease. The section then covers renting procedures, including eligibility and tenant selection standards for congregate housing and group homes. For congregate housing, it emphasizes tenant mix planning, describing how projects should balance tenants needing varying levels of service packages, with the project management plan specifying the service distribution and Rural Development approving the proposed mix.

nodes.title: B. Tenant selection.
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines requirements for congregate housing and group home projects, focusing on service arrangements and tenant selection procedures. It explains that borrowers must submit a service plan and service agreements for services not directly provided, including requirements for documentation, duration, and authorization. nodes.title: 1. Congregate housing.
nodes.summary: This excerpt outlines requirements for congregate housing and group home projects, focusing on service-related documentation and tenant procedures. It explains that borrowers must submit a service plan, service agreements for services not directly provided, and sample service contracts for Agency review, with requirements on duration, authorization, and separation from occupancy leases. For congregate housing, it emphasizes tenant mix planning, including how many tenants will receive varying levels of service packages, the need to consult project management, and the requirement for Rural Development concurrence.

nodes.title: a. Tenant mix.
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines requirements for congregate housing and group home projects, focusing on service-related obligations and tenant admission procedures. It explains that borrowers must submit a service plan, service agreements for any third-party services, and sample service contracts for agency review, with protections to ensure these contracts are separate from occupancy leases and comply with Fair Housing and agency rules. It then covers renting procedures, including eligibility of tenants, borrower responsibilities in informing applicants about available supportive services, and how applicants must request those services. The document also describes tenant selection considerations for congregate housing, such as maintaining an appropriate tenant mix, managing waiting lists for different service packages, and obtaining Rural Development concurrence. For group homes, it notes that occupancy may be limited to specific tenant groups, applicants must demonstrate need, participation in training or rehabilitation programs cannot be required, and local applicants should be considered first. Finally, it explains how to determine per-unit rental rates in group living arrangements by dividing operational costs equally or according to unit size.

nodes.title: b. Selecting services needed or wanted by tenants in congregate housing.
nodes.summary: The passage explains rules for congregate housing and group homes, focusing on how tenants are informed about and select supportive services, how applicants/tenants request those services, and how waiting lists for different service packages may be managed. It also describes group home occupancy rules, including limiting residents to certain eligible groups, requiring applicants to show need for the housing, prohibiting mandatory training or rehabilitation participation, and giving preference to local applicants. Finally, it outlines how per-unit rental rates are calculated in group living arrangements, either by splitting costs equally when units are the same size or allocating costs based on unit size when they differ.

nodes.title: c. Waiting lists.
nodes.summary: The document section explains how congregate housing and group homes should manage tenant services, eligibility, waiting lists, and rent calculation. It states that borrowers must inform applicants about available supportive services, while applicants or tenants must request the services they need. It also describes how only essential information may be collected to assess service needs, including possible professional assessments in group home cases. The section covers waiting lists for different levels or timing of service packages and notes that applicants requesting services now should generally be prioritized over those requesting them later. For group homes, it explains that occupancy may be limited to specific tenant groups, that applicants must show need for the housing, that tenants cannot be required to participate in training or rehabilitation programs, and that local applicants should be considered first. Finally, it outlines how per-unit rental rates in group living arrangements are determined by dividing operational costs equally when units are the same size, or proportionally when unit sizes differ.

nodes.title: 2. Group home.
nodes.summary: The document section explains how congregate housing services and group homes should be managed, focusing on tenant service selection, waiting lists, occupancy rules, and rent calculation. It states that borrowers must inform applicants or tenants about available supportive services, while applicants/tenants must request the services they want or need. It also allows only essential information to be collected to match tenants with services, including possible professional assessments in group homes. The section describes how waiting lists may be used for different service package levels, with priority given to applicants seeking services sooner. For group homes, occupancy may be limited to specific eligible populations, must be justified by need, cannot require participation in ongoing training or rehabilitation programs, and should prioritize local applicants. Finally, it outlines how per-unit rental rates in group living arrangements are determined based on unit size and the allocation of operational costs.

nodes.title: a. Applicants for group home housing must demonstrate their need for such housing.

It describes how essential information may be gathered to match tenants with services and how waiting lists for different service packages can be maintained while prioritizing applicants requesting immediate services. It also outlines group home occupancy rules, such as limiting residents to specific eligible groups, requiring applicants to demonstrate need, prohibiting mandatory participation in training or rehabilitation programs, and preferring local applicants. Finally, it covers how per-unit rental rates are determined in group living arrangements by defining a unit and allocating operational costs equally or proportionally based on unit size.

nodes.title: b. Tenants of group homes cannot be required to be a part of an ongoing training or rehabilitation program sponsored by the applicant or other organization.
nodes.summary: The document section explains how congregate housing services are selected and managed, including the borrower’s duty to inform applicants about available supportive services, the applicant’s responsibility to request needed services, and the use of waiting lists for different service packages. It also outlines special rules for group homes, such as limiting occupancy to certain tenant groups, requiring applicants to show need, prohibiting mandatory training or rehabilitation participation, and giving preference to local applicants. Finally, it describes how to calculate per-unit rental rates in group living arrangements by dividing operational costs equally when units are the same size or proportionally when unit sizes differ.

nodes.title: c. Tenants should be selected from the local area before considering other areas.
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines rules for congregate housing and group homes, focusing on how tenants select needed services, how applicants must be informed about available supportive services, and how requests for services are handled. It explains that management may maintain waiting lists for different service package levels and gives priority to applicants seeking services immediately. It also describes group home occupancy restrictions, including limiting residents to specific eligible groups, requiring applicants to demonstrate need, prohibiting mandatory participation in training or rehabilitation programs, and preferring local applicants. Finally, it explains how to determine per-unit rental rates in group living arrangements by defining what counts as a unit and allocating operating costs equally or based on unit size.

nodes.title: C. Determining per unit rental rates for group living arrangements.
nodes.summary: The document explains rules for congregate housing and group homes, focusing on how tenants are informed about and select supportive services, how applicants or tenants request needed services, and how waiting lists may be managed for different service packages. It also outlines group home policies, including limiting occupancy to specific eligible tenant groups, requiring applicants to show need for the housing, prohibiting mandatory participation in training or rehabilitation programs, and giving preference to local applicants. Finally, it describes how to calculate per-unit rental rates in group living arrangements by dividing operational costs equally when units are the same size or proportionally when unit sizes differ.

nodes.title: 1. When all units are of equal size, divide operational costs equally.
nodes.summary:
The document explains how supportive services are selected and managed in congregate housing, including the borrower’s duty to inform applicants/tenants about available services, the tenant’s responsibility to request desired services, and how only essential information may be collected to determine service needs or accommodations. It also covers the use of waiting lists for different service package levels and notes that selection priority should favor applicants requesting services sooner. 

It then describes rules for group homes, which may restrict occupancy to specific eligible groups, require applicants to show need, prohibit mandatory participation in training or rehabilitation programs, and prefer selecting tenants from the local area. 

Finally, it outlines how to calculate per-unit rental rates in group living arrangements, defining a “unit” as the space occupied by a tenant household and explaining that operational costs should be divided equally when units are the same size or proportionally when they differ in size.

nodes.title: 2. When all units are not of equal size, determine the size of each unit and divide operational costs accordingly.
nodes.summary:
The partial document explains requirements for congregate housing and group homes under Rural Development rules. It covers how tenants are informed about and select supportive services, the use of waiting lists for different service packages, and special occupancy rules for group homes, including eligibility, local preference, and limits on mandatory training or rehabilitation participation. It also describes how to determine per-unit rental rates in group living arrangements, including treatment of traditional and nontraditional units and excluding resident assistant units from rent calculations.  

The document then addresses income/employment verification and ongoing review of support services during recertification. Finally, it outlines lease agreement requirements for tenants who request services, including clauses stating that service use is optional and that lease obligations remain separate from service dissatisfaction. It also begins to discuss conditions for service facilities used by non-tenants, such as meal services, including lease and cost recovery requirements and tenant priority.

nodes.title: a. The size of traditional units is their square footage.
nodes.summary: This excerpt outlines rules for congregate housing under HB-1-3560, focusing on how unit size is determined, how certain units are excluded from rent calculations, and how tenant income and support services must be reviewed and certified. It also details lease requirements for tenants who request services, including required lease language explaining that service use is optional and separate from rent. Finally, it addresses situations where housing services are offered to non-tenants, requiring proper leasing arrangements, cost recovery, and tenant priority for services when Rural Development financing is involved.

nodes.title: b. The size of nontraditional units is the bedroom or portion of bedroom occupied by the household and portion of the common area to be used by all potential units in nontraditional units.
nodes.summary:
This section explains how to determine unit size for rent calculations, including traditional units based on square footage and nontraditional units based on occupied bedroom/common space. It also states that resident assistant units are not revenue-producing and are excluded from rent determination.

The document then covers verification and certification requirements for tenant income, employment, and support services, directing management to recertify income and discuss available services and accommodations with tenants.

Finally, it outlines lease agreement requirements for congregate housing. Leases must include specific clauses when tenants request services, clarifying that service packages are optional and separate from rent, and that service issues cannot be used to withhold rent. It also addresses situations where services are provided to non-tenants, requiring leases with service providers, priority for project tenants, and financial terms that cover operating costs and related expenses.

nodes.title: 3. A unit occupied by a resident assistant is not considered a revenue producing unit and would be excluded from the rent determination.

It then covers verification and recertification procedures for tenant income, employment, and support services, requiring management to review available services with each tenant and determine whether additional accommodations are needed. The final section focuses on lease agreements for congregate housing, specifying required lease language when tenants request services, including that service packages are optional and separate from rent. It also addresses situations where services are provided to non-tenants, requiring appropriate leasing and payment arrangements, priority for project tenants, and compliance conditions when facilities are financed with loan funds.
