nodes.title: B. Services provided to people other than tenants of Rural Development financed congregate housing.
nodes.summary:
The document section outlines rules for congregate housing and related rural development-financed projects. It explains how unit size is determined for traditional and nontraditional units and states that resident assistant units are not counted as revenue-producing for rent calculations. It then covers tenant income verification and service review, requiring management to recertify income and discuss available support services with tenants.

It also details lease agreement requirements, including mandatory clauses when tenants request services, protections ensuring service use is optional, and conditions for services provided to non-tenants, such as priority for project tenants and lease approval requirements. The section explains rent and service payment handling, requiring that rent and service charges be separately accounted for even if paid together.

Finally, it addresses budgeting, accounting, and reporting rules, requiring separation of project operations from service component revenues and expenses, and it concludes with termination and eviction guidance, stating that tenants may remain as long as they remain eligible and can care for themselves, while tenancy termination must follow applicable federal regulations.

nodes.title: IX. RENT COLLECTION:
nodes.summary:

It specifies that any lease agreement must be approved by the appropriate Rural Development official and include a required approval statement. It also explains rent and service payment collection rules, emphasizing that charges may be paid separately or together but must be accounted for separately.

The section further describes budgeting and accounting rules, requiring borrowers to keep project operations and service component finances separate, with project budgets reflecting only project operations and proper proration of employee costs when staff support both areas. It stresses separate financial records and reporting, limits the use of funds between project and service accounts, and notes when detailed service financial reports may be requested.

Finally, it covers termination of tenancy and eviction, stating that residents in congregate or group home housing may remain as long as they remain eligible and can care for themselves with or without services, and that tenancy can only be ended under the applicable federal housing regulations.

structure.title: ATTACHMENT 11-C: OCCUPANCY AGREEMENT
structure.summary:
The partial document contains two related housing documents for a cooperative project.

Main points covered:
- An occupancy agreement between a cooperative and a member for use of a specific dwelling unit as a permanent residence.
- The agreement explains that membership and eligibility requirements, including household income, are essential for continued occupancy.
- The term of occupancy is generally three years, with automatic renewal options unless proper notice is given and conditions for vacating are met.

Key provisions in the occupancy agreement:
- Monthly occupancy charges: how they are calculated, what expenses they cover, when they begin, and that members must provide yearly income certification.
- Patronage refunds: members may receive a share of excess cooperative funds assigned on the books.
- Renewal and termination rules: procedures for renewal, notice requirements, surrender of membership, and removal from the unit.
- Use of premises: unit must be used only as a private residence; rules against nuisance, illegal activity, and actions that raise insurance costs.
- Rights of possession: cooperative guarantees peaceful use if the member complies with the agreement.
- Restrictions on subletting and transfer: no assignment or sublease without written consent from the cooperative and Rural Development.
- Management, taxes, insurance, and utilities: cooperative responsibilities and member payment obligations for separately metered utilities.
- Repairs and maintenance: division of responsibilities between member and cooperative, including the cooperative’s right to make repairs and charge the member.
- Alterations and additions: restrictions on structural changes and installation of certain equipment without consent.
- Default provisions: events that constitute default, notice and eviction procedures, and remedies available to the cooperative.
- Member duties: compliance with bylaws, rules, regulations, and cooperative principles.
- Fire loss and casualty: how occupancy charges and restoration are handled if the unit is damaged.
- Inspection rights: cooperative, mortgagee, and authorized representatives may inspect the unit with notice or in emergencies.
- Subordination: the agreement is subordinate to the Rural Development mortgage and related financing documents.
- Late charges and legal costs: late fees for overdue occupancy charges and responsibility for attorney’s fees in case of default.
- Notices and binding terms: how notices are given and that only written agreement terms, charter, and bylaws are binding.

The second document is a cooperative housing survey:
- It asks prospective members whether they are willing to share cooperative responsibilities, serve on the board or committees, help maintain property, and learn more about cooperative housing.
- It includes a screening question about whether the applicant answered honestly and whether they answered “no” to key participation questions, indicating the housing may not be a good fit.
- It begins a household information section asking how many adults and children live in the household.

structure.title: ATTACHMENT 11-D: COOPERATIVE HOUSING SURVEY
structure.summary:
The partial document includes two cooperative housing forms and their main purposes.

The first form, “Cooperative Housing Survey,” is a questionnaire for people considering joining a housing cooperative. It asks whether applicants are willing to share responsibilities, serve on the board or committees, help maintain property, and learn more about cooperative housing. It also checks if they want to attend an information meeting, whether they answered truthfully, and whether they would be unsuitable if they answered “no” to key responsibility questions. The second part collects household information such as number of adults and children, annual income, whether anyone needs specially designed housing due to a handicap or impairment, and contact details for an informal meeting about a proposed cooperative.

The second form, “Limited Equity Agreement,” is an agreement between the cooperative and the Rural Housing Service (RHS). It explains that the cooperative is intended to provide housing for very-low, low-, and moderate-income households through RHS mortgage financing. It sets rules for limited equity, including assigning cooperative equity to members through patronage accounts, notifying members annually, preventing dissolution just to distribute equity, and requiring that if the cooperative is dissolved, its assets be transferred to another nonprofit or municipal organization to continue serving the same housing purpose.

structure.title: ATTACHMENT 11-E: LIMITED EQUITY AGREEMENT
structure.summary:
The partial document contains two main attachment sections from USDA Rural Housing Service guidance.

The first section is a “Limited Equity Agreement” for a housing cooperative. It explains the purpose of the agreement, which is to support cooperative housing financed under Section 515 of the Housing Act of 1949. It states that the cooperative must operate to preserve housing for very-low, low-, and moderate-income residents. The agreement sets rules for how equity is handled: cooperative earnings are allocated to members as patronage capital, members are notified annually of their account balances, the cooperative cannot dissolve simply to distribute equity, and if dissolution becomes necessary, assets must be transferred to another nonprofit or similar organization for the same purpose. It also specifies that only membership fees and patronage capital may be distributed to members, with deductions allowed for unpaid occupancy charges or damages. The agreement is signed and filed with the member’s record.

The second section, “Qualifications for an Adviser to the Board,” describes the personal and professional qualities expected of someone advising a cooperative board. It emphasizes that the adviser should be friendly, trustworthy, patient, understanding, and supportive of low-income residents. The adviser must be able to explain the cooperative housing concept, listen to members, teach and learn, and work effectively with officials and professionals. It also outlines needed educational and practical background, including experience with low-income groups, administrative skills for managing records and maintenance systems, and training in teaching, learning-by-doing, and group dynamics.

structure.title: ATTACHMENT 11-F: QUALIFICATIONS FOR AN ADVISER TO THE BOARD
structure.summary: The partial document describes the qualifications and role of an adviser to a cooperative housing board, emphasizing that the adviser should be a caring, patient, trustworthy “friend” who can support low-income residents, explain complex housing and legal concepts, and work effectively with both members and officials. It outlines the adviser’s educational and experience background, including experience with low-income populations, administrative and maintenance systems, training in teaching and group dynamics, problem-solving, social casework awareness, resource use, and interpreting funding agency documents. The document also explains the adviser’s relationship to cooperative members: teaching them management and maintenance, helping them gradually assume responsibility, encouraging cooperative values and good governance, and ensuring they understand and follow rules, occupancy agreements, and funding regulations.

structure.title: ATTACHMENT 11-G: RELATIONSHIP OF ADVISER TO MEMBERS
structure.summary: The document outlines the relationship between an adviser and cooperative members, emphasizing that the adviser’s role is to teach members how to manage and maintain the cooperative until they can operate it independently. It explains the skills and knowledge the adviser must help members develop, including management procedures, cooperative principles, board and committee functions, regulatory requirements, and responsibilities such as paying on time, sharing work, participating in decisions, and cooperating with others. The document also stresses that members must understand and follow rules and occupancy agreements, and that the adviser should support early planning, member involvement, problem-solving, and the interpretation of government regulations. In addition, it lists adviser responsibilities to the board, such as acting as a backup manager, helping organize the board and committees, monitoring operations, creating and implementing an educational plan, assisting with recordkeeping and maintenance systems, addressing basic financial questions, and helping evaluate new applicants for membership.

structure.title: ATTACHMENT 11-H: ADVISER RESPONSIBILITIES
structure.summary: The partial document contains two main sections from HB-1-3560 attachments. First, “Attachment 11-H Adviser Responsibilities” lists the duties of an adviser to a board in a cooperative housing setting, including acting as a backup manager while teaching self-management, helping organize the board and committees, monitoring operations, developing and implementing an educational plan, assisting with management and maintenance systems, helping with non-complex financial questions, and advising on evaluating new membership applicants. Second, “Attachment 11-I Subscription Agreement” is a form for a cooperative housing membership subscription. It outlines the subscriber’s agreement to purchase a membership certificate, payment terms, acceptance of cooperative governing documents and Rural Development requirements, subordination to mortgage liens, and the right to occupy a designated dwelling unit if approved, with occupancy charges based on family income.

structure.title: ATTACHMENT 11-I: SUBSCRIPTION AGREEMENT
structure.summary:
This partial document is a Subscription Agreement for a cooperative housing corporation. It outlines the subscriber’s commitment to purchase a membership certificate, including the payment amount and schedule. The agreement states that the subscriber is bound by the cooperative’s articles of incorporation, bylaws, and occupancy agreement, and that all rights are subordinate to the mortgage lien and Rural Development requirements.

It explains that if approved, the subscriber may occupy a designated dwelling unit under an occupancy agreement, with charges initially estimated and later based on family income. The document also describes several cancellation and withdrawal rights: the cooperative may cancel before membership approval, Rural Development eligibility or loan issues can void the agreement, the subscriber has a five-day right to withdraw after signing, and defaults may result in loss of rights and possible retention of payments as liquidated damages. It also states that the agreement is not assignable except under approved conditions and that it contains the full agreement, replacing any prior oral statements or understandings.

structure.title: CHAPTER 12: FARM LABOR HOUSING LOANS AND GRANTS
structure.summary: This partial document outlines Chapter 12 of HB-1-3560, which covers Farm Labor Housing loans and grants. It explains that the chapter supplements the general loan origination procedures from earlier chapters and highlights special requirements for farm labor housing. The chapter is divided into two main parts: Section 1 on Off-Farm Labor Housing and Section 2 on On-Farm Labor Housing. The excerpt focuses on Section 1, describing off-farm labor housing as housing for farm laborers regardless of where they work, which can be financed through loans, grants, or both, and may also include rental assistance and technical assistance grants. It notes that off-farm projects are typically developed by nonprofit organizations or public bodies, can be located in urban or rural areas if need is justified, and receive annual funding allocations announced through a Notice of Funding Availability (NOFA). Applicants then submit applications to Field Offices for review and funding consideration.

nodes.title: INTRODUCTION
nodes.summary:
The partial document introduces Chapter 12 of HB-1-3560, which covers Farm Labor Housing loans and grants. It explains that the chapter focuses on the origination process for farm labor housing projects, noting that although many procedures are similar to Section 515 loans, there are special requirements unique to farm labor housing. The chapter is divided into two sections: one for Off-Farm Labor Housing and one for On-Farm Labor Housing.

The excerpt then begins Section 1, describing Off-Farm Labor Housing projects as housing intended to increase affordable housing for farm laborers regardless of where they work. It explains that these projects may use loans, grants, or both, and may also include rental assistance contracts. The text notes that technical assistance grants may be awarded to eligible nonprofit or public entities to support development. It also highlights that off-farm labor housing is not limited by the designated places requirement and may be built in urban or rural areas if need is justified. Funding is allocated annually through a NOFA, after which applicants submit applications through Field Offices for funding consideration.

nodes.title: SECTION 1: OFF-FARM LABOR HOUSING LOANS AND GRANTS
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines Chapter 12 of HB-1-3560, which covers Farm Labor Housing loans and grants. It explains that the chapter supplements the general loan application procedures from earlier chapters by adding requirements specific to farm labor housing. The text distinguishes between two main categories: off-farm labor housing and on-farm labor housing. It introduces Section 1, which covers off-farm labor housing projects, including how funding is announced through a NOFA, how applications are ranked and funded, and how state offices process them. It also describes off-farm labor housing as affordable housing for farm laborers that can be developed by nonprofit organizations or public bodies in urban or rural areas, with possible loans, grants, rental assistance, and technical assistance grants.

nodes.title: OVERVIEW
nodes.summary:
This partial document is a section from HB-1-3560, Chapter 12, which covers Farm Labor Housing loans and grants. It explains the purpose of the chapter, noting that it supplements the general loan processing procedures in earlier chapters by focusing on the special requirements for farm labor housing projects. The chapter is divided into two main parts: off-farm labor housing and on-farm labor housing.

The excerpt first gives an overview of off-farm labor housing, describing it as housing intended to increase affordable options for farm laborers regardless of where they work. It explains that these projects may be financed with loans, grants, or both, and that developers may also receive rental assistance contracts. It notes that technical assistance grants may be available to nonprofit agencies under Section 516(i). The document also explains that off-farm labor housing is generally developed by broad-based nonprofits or public bodies, is not limited by the “designated places” requirement applicable to Section 515 loans, and may be built in either urban or rural areas if the need is justified. Funding is allocated annually through a Notice of Funding Availability (NOFA), and applications are submitted and processed through Field Offices in a selection and final application process.

The document also defines “farm labor” for the purposes of the Section 514 and 516 programs, with reference to activities involved in cultivating, harvesting, handling, processing, storing, or transporting agricultural and aquacultural commodities.

Another major point covered is applicant eligibility for off-farm labor housing loans and grants. It lists the types of entities that may qualify for Section 514 loans, including broad-based nonprofit organizations, limited partnerships with nonprofit general partners, nonprofit farmworker organizations, federally recognized Indian tribes, and state or local government agencies or subdivisions. For Section 516 grants, the excerpt begins to describe additional eligibility requirements, starting with broad-based nonprofit organizations.

nodes.title: APPLICANT ELIGIBILITY FOR OFF-FARM LABOR HOUSING [7 CFR 3560.555]
nodes.summary: The document excerpt explains eligibility rules for USDA Farm Labor Housing programs under Sections 514 and 516. It defines what counts as farm labor for these programs, including activities related to cultivating, harvesting, handling, packing, storing, and transporting agricultural or aquacultural commodities in unprocessed form. It then describes who may apply for Farm Labor Housing loans and grants, listing eligible applicant types such as broad-based nonprofit organizations, limited partnerships with nonprofit general partners, nonprofit organizations of farmworkers, federally recognized Indian tribes, and state or local government agencies or subdivisions.

nodes.title: Eligible Applicants for Farm Labor Housing Loans
nodes.summary: The partial document explains the definition of farm labor for Sections 514 and 516, including various agricultural and aquaculture activities such as cultivating, harvesting, packing, storing, and transporting commodities. It then outlines applicant eligibility requirements for off-farm labor housing under 7 CFR 3560.555. For farm labor housing loans, eligible applicants include broad-based nonprofit organizations, limited partnerships with a nonprofit general partner, nonprofit organizations of farmworkers, federally recognized Indian tribes, and state or local government agencies or subdivisions. It also begins listing the eligibility requirements for farm labor housing grants, starting with broad-based nonprofit organizations.

nodes.title: Eligible Applicants for Farm Labor Housing Grants
nodes.summary: The partial document explains eligibility and funding rules for USDA Farm Labor Housing programs under Sections 514 and 516. It defines farm labor for these programs, then describes who may apply for off-farm labor housing loans and grants, including nonprofits, nonprofits of farmworkers, Indian tribes, and state or local government entities. It also outlines the maximum grant amount, which is generally capped at 90 percent of total development cost, and requires applicants to show a documented need for the housing project. Finally, it covers technical assistance grants, noting that they may be awarded to eligible nonprofit agencies to help others secure housing loans and grants, with restrictions on duplicate funding and conflicts of interest.

nodes.title: TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE GRANTS
nodes.summary:
The partial document outlines requirements and eligibility rules for Off-Farm Labor Housing grants and loans. It covers who may be eligible applicants, including nonprofit organizations, farmworker nonprofits, Federally recognized Indian tribes, and state or local government entities. It explains the maximum grant amount, limited to 90 percent of total development cost or the amount exceeding the applicant’s own resources and approved loans, and notes that applicants must show a documented pressing need for the housing and that it likely cannot be provided without a grant.

The document also describes technical assistance grants, which may be awarded to eligible nonprofit agencies to help other organizations obtain financing for farm labor housing, with restrictions on duplicate funding and conflicts of interest. Finally, it details project eligibility requirements, including site and design standards, such as compliance with site rules, restrictions when similar housing projects already exist in the market area, and design considerations like interior and exterior washing facilities to protect tenants and the property.

nodes.title: PROJECT ELIGIBILITY
nodes.summary: The partial document explains the project eligibility requirements for Off-Farm Labor Housing under HB-1-3560. It outlines the site requirements and design requirements that applications must meet to qualify for a loan or grant. Key site rules include compliance with 7 CFR 3560.558, with the exception that projects do not have to be in a designated place, and it notes several situations where funding will not be considered, such as when another similar project is already being processed, when existing assisted housing in the area is incomplete or has high vacancy, when a Special Note Rent is pending or in effect, or when the area needs rental assistance rather than new units. The design requirements section references 7 CFR 3560.559 and highlights the need to consider interior and exterior washing facilities to protect tenants from dirt and chemical exposure, with examples such as boot washing stations, hose bibs, or accessible bathrooms. Applicants are expected to document the type of farm work tenants will do and explain what washing facilities are appropriate for the project.

nodes.title: Site Requirements [7 CFR 3560.558]
nodes.summary:
This section explains the eligibility requirements for Off-Farm Labor Housing projects seeking loans or grants. It outlines both site and design criteria that applications must meet.

The site requirements state that projects must comply with 7 CFR 3560.558, except for the rule requiring the property to be in a designated place, since farm labor housing may be developed in both urban and rural areas. It also lists conditions under which funding will not be considered, such as when another similar project is already being processed in the same market area, when a prior assisted project is incomplete or underoccupied, when a Special Note Rent or similar tool is in effect, or when the market need is for rental assistance rather than more housing units.

The design requirements refer to 7 CFR 3560.559 and include guidance on providing interior and exterior washing facilities for tenants when needed. These facilities may include items like boot washing stations, hose bibs, or accessible bathrooms, depending on local farmworker activities. Applicants must document the type of work tenants will do and explain how proposed washing facilities will meet project needs.

nodes.title: Design Requirements [7 CFR 3560.559]
nodes.summary: The partial document explains the project eligibility rules for Off-Farm Labor Housing loans and grants. It covers site requirements, including when projects will not be funded due to existing or pending housing projects, vacancy issues, or unmet demand for rental assistance. It also outlines design requirements such as washing facilities for workers and special provisions for seasonal labor housing. In addition, the document describes eligible and prohibited uses of loan and grant funds, including technical assistance, initial operating expenses, tenant-related facilities, and seasonal-use facilities. Finally, it notes that initial applications must follow the same basic submission requirements as Section 515 project applications.

nodes.title: LOAN AND GRANT FUND USES
nodes.summary: The passage outlines key rules for off-farm and seasonal farm labor housing under HB-1-3560. It explains that seasonal labor housing occupied for 8 months or less by migrant farmworkers may be built under RD Instruction 1924-A, Exhibit I, if the application clearly states the standards being used. It then describes eligible loan and grant fund uses, including reimbursement for technical assistance, limited initial operating expenses, tenant-related facilities such as daycare or computer labs, and seasonal or temporary-use facilities with furnishings and equipment. It also states prohibited fund uses, referencing the restrictions in 7 CFR 3560.554 while noting an exception allowing Section 514/516 funds for housing serving primarily temporary and transient residents. Finally, it notes that initial applications for off-farm labor housing follow the same basic submission requirements as Section 515 project applications.

nodes.title: Eligible Uses of Funds [7 CFR 3560.53(k) and (o)]
nodes.summary: This excerpt from HB-1-3560 covers guidelines for seasonal labor housing and the use of loan and grant funds in off-farm labor housing projects. It explains that seasonal farm labor housing for migrant farmworkers, occupied 8 months or less per year, may be built under RD Instruction 1924-A, Exhibit I, if properly documented in the application. It then outlines eligible uses of loan and grant funds, including reimbursement for technical assistance, limited initial operating expenses, tenant-related facilities such as daycare or computer learning centers, and seasonal or temporary-use facilities with furnishings and equipment. It also notes prohibited uses of funds by referencing applicable federal restrictions, while allowing Section 514/516 funds for housing serving primarily temporary and transient residents. Finally, it states that initial applications for off-farm labor housing must follow the same basic submission requirements as Section 515 project applications.

nodes.title: Prohibited Uses of Funds [7 CFR 3560.554]
nodes.summary: The partial document describes rules for off-farm and seasonal farm labor housing under HB-1-3560. It explains that seasonal labor housing occupied for 8 months or less by migrant farmworkers may be built under RD Instruction 1924-A, Exhibit I, with the application needing to document the standards used. It then outlines eligible uses of loan and grant funds, including reimbursement for nonprofit technical assistance, limited initial operating expenses, tenant-related facilities like daycare or computer centers, and seasonal or temporary-use facilities with furnishings and equipment. The section also states prohibited uses of funds by referencing applicable federal restrictions. Finally, it notes that initial applications for off-farm labor housing follow the same basic submission requirements as Section 515 project applications.

nodes.title: SUBMISSION AND PROCESSING OF INITIAL APPLICATIONS
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses requirements and funding rules for off-farm and seasonal farm labor housing under HB-1-3560. It explains that seasonal labor housing occupied for 8 months or less by migrant farmworkers may be built under RD Instruction 1924-A, Exhibit I, and that applications must document the standards used. It then outlines eligible loan and grant fund uses, including reimbursement for technical assistance, limited initial operating expenses, tenant-related facilities such as daycare or computer learning centers, and seasonal or temporary-use facilities with furnishings and equipment. It also lists prohibited fund uses by reference to 7 CFR 3560.554, noting that certain Section 514/516 funds may be used for housing serving temporary and transient residents. Finally, it states that initial applications for off-farm labor housing must meet the same basic submission requirements as Section 515 project applications.

nodes.title: Content of Initial Applications
nodes.summary: The partial document explains USDA Rural Development rules for off-farm and seasonal farm labor housing, focusing on construction standards, allowable loan and grant fund uses, and how applications are submitted, reviewed, scored, ranked, and selected for funding. It notes that seasonal labor housing occupied 8 months or less may follow a specific RD construction exhibit, and that applications must document the standards used. It also lists eligible fund uses such as technical assistance reimbursement, limited initial operating expenses, tenant-related facilities, and temporary-use facilities, while referencing prohibited uses. Finally, it outlines the application process, including completeness and eligibility review, the fact that designated-place requirements do not apply to farm labor housing, and the state and national scoring, ranking, and selection procedures for funding.

nodes.title: Completeness Review
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines the process for reviewing, scoring, ranking, and selecting Farm Labor Housing loan and grant applications. It explains that loan originators must first check applications for completeness and eligibility, following specific chapter and paragraph requirements, while noting that designated place requirements do not apply to farm labor housing. It then describes how State Offices score and rank initial applications, with a Loan Review Committee required to review the results before they are sent to the National Office. Finally, it covers the national-level ranking and selection process, including submission deadlines, national review criteria, funding awards based on scores, and the use of funds by the next highest-ranked application if an award recipient does not proceed or cannot complete the project.

nodes.title: Eligibility Review
nodes.summary: The partial document explains the process for reviewing, scoring, ranking, and selecting Farm Labor Housing loan and grant applications. It covers completeness review and eligibility review requirements, including a note that designated place requirements do not apply to farm labor housing. It then describes state-level scoring and ranking of initial applications, review by a Loan Review Committee, and forwarding eligible applications to the National Office. Finally, it outlines the national ranking and selection process, where applications are ranked according to NOFA criteria and funding is awarded to the highest-scoring applications, with unused funds reassigned to the next-ranked application if a selected applicant does not proceed.

nodes.title: Scoring and Ranking of Initial Applications
nodes.summary: The partial document describes the process for reviewing and selecting Farm Labor Housing loan and grant applications. It covers completeness review of initial applications, eligibility review requirements, and notes that designated place restrictions do not apply to farm labor housing. It then explains how State Offices score and rank applications under the NOFA and Chapter 4 procedures, with Loan Review Committee review before forwarding to the National Office. Finally, it outlines national-level ranking and selection, including submission deadlines, national review criteria, and funding of the top-ranked applications, with unused funds reassigned to the next highest-ranked applicant if an award does not proceed.

nodes.title: National Ranking and Selection of Initial Applications
nodes.summary: The document section outlines the review and selection process for Farm Labor Housing and Off-Farm Labor Housing initial applications. It explains the required completeness review, eligibility review, scoring and ranking of applications at the State level, and national-level ranking and selection by the Agency. It also covers how applicants are notified of selection or denial through specific handbook letters, including required ECOA notices. Finally, it describes additional processing requirements for Off-Farm Labor Housing applications, focusing on project eligibility factors such as rural location exceptions, exterior washing facilities, and whether the housing is intended for year-round or seasonal use.

nodes.title: Notification of Initial Applications Selection
nodes.summary: The partial document explains the notification and follow-up process for initial application selection in the national competition, including the three standard letters applicants may receive: a notice of pre-application review action, a lack-of-funds notice, or a denial due to ineligibility, with ECOA notice requirements for non-invitation letters. It then begins section 12.8 on further processing of initial applications for the Off-Farm Labor Housing program, noting that while general Chapter 4 and 5 procedures apply, some review steps differ because of program-specific requirements. The section on project eligibility highlights key checks for Loan Originators, such as confirming the project does not need to be in a rural location, ensuring exterior washing facilities are included in the budget, and verifying how the project addresses seasonal versus year-round use and construction standards.

nodes.title: FURTHER PROCESSING OF INITIAL APPLICATIONS
nodes.summary:
The document explains how initial applications are handled after national selection and what notification letters applicants receive. It states that the National Office informs State Offices of selected applications, State Offices notify applicants and invite final applications, and applicants may receive one of three standard letters: a notice of pre-application review action for selected applicants, a lack-of-funds letter for unfunded but eligible applicants, or a denial letter for applicants found ineligible. It also notes that any non-invitation letter must include the standard ECOA notice.

The document then begins Section 12.8 on further processing of initial applications for the Off-Farm Labor Housing program. It explains that although general procedures in Chapters 4 and 5 still apply, some review steps are different because of program-specific requirements. The main project eligibility checks listed are that the project does not need to be in a rural location or designated place, the budget must include exterior washing facilities, and the application must address whether the housing will be used year-round or seasonally and the standards to which it will be built.

nodes.title: Project Eligibility
nodes.summary: The partial document explains the notification process for initial application selections and the subsequent steps for further processing in the Off-Farm Labor Housing program. It describes three possible agency letters sent to applicants: one for those selected for further processing, one for those not funded due to lack of funds, and one for those denied funding because of ineligibility. It also notes that letters other than invitation letters must include the standard ECOA notice. In addition, the document outlines special project eligibility review points for Off-Farm Labor Housing, including that the project does not need to be in a rural location, must include exterior washing facilities in the budget, and must address whether housing will be used year-round or seasonally and the applicable construction standards.

nodes.title: Applicant Eligibility
nodes.summary: The partial document explains key requirements for applicant eligibility, project feasibility, and grant calculation for farm labor housing funding. It states that applicants must be one of the eligible entity types, and if requesting grant funds, they must show they are a permanent organization serving low-income farmworker housing, have secured other development financing, and demonstrate a pressing need that cannot be met without the grant. It also requires applicants to prove a need for affordable farm labor housing in the market area by providing analysis of agricultural activity and eligible farmworker demand, excluding ineligible workers. Finally, it describes how the State Office determines the allowable grant amount using a cash flow analysis and project data, and notes that final loan/grant decisions depend on rent levels, operating costs, rental assistance, applicant resources, and repayment ability, with a special limit that grants cannot exceed the lesser of 90 percent of total development cost or the amount exceeding other contributions.

nodes.title: Project Feasibility
nodes.summary:
The partial document outlines key requirements for applicant eligibility and project feasibility for an Off-Farm Labor Housing loan/grant application, including criteria for organizations requesting grant funding. It explains that applicants must be one of the approved eligible entities and, if seeking grants, must show they are a permanent organization serving low-income farmworker housing, have other funding sources to cover remaining development costs, and can demonstrate a pressing need that cannot be met without grant support.

It also covers the project feasibility analysis required in the initial application, emphasizing the need to demonstrate demand for affordable farm labor housing in the market area through current and projected agricultural activity and farmworker housing needs, while excluding ineligible workers from the market study.

Finally, it describes how the allowable grant amount is determined, using an automated cash flow analysis and applicant-provided economic data, with final loan/grant levels based on factors such as local rents, operating costs, rental assistance, applicant resources, and repayment ability. The beginning of the final application processing section notes that grant amounts cannot exceed certain limits relative to total development cost.

nodes.title: Calculation of the Allowable Grant Amount
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines key requirements for farm labor housing loan and grant applications. It covers applicant eligibility, including qualification as an eligible entity and additional documentation needed when requesting grant funding, such as proof of organizational permanence, sources for non-grant development costs, and evidence of urgent need. It also describes project feasibility requirements, requiring applicants to demonstrate a need for affordable farm labor housing through market-area analysis of agricultural activity and eligible farmworker demand. Finally, it explains how allowable grant amounts are calculated using cash flow analysis and project data, with final funding decisions based on rents, operating costs, rental assistance, applicant resources, and repayment ability, and begins the section on submission and processing of final applications, including a limit on grant amounts.

nodes.title: SUBMISSION AND PROCESSING OF FINAL APPLICATIONS
nodes.summary: The partial document explains eligibility, feasibility, funding, and closing requirements for Farm Labor Housing loans and grants. It covers who can apply, what grant applicants must prove, how project feasibility is evaluated, and how allowable grant amounts are calculated. It also describes final application processing rules, including security value, borrower contributions, loan and grant terms, and approval and closing procedures. Additional required documents at closing, such as board resolutions, loan agreements, and grant agreements, are also outlined.

nodes.title: Security Value and Borrower Contributions
nodes.summary: This document section explains how to determine financing for Farm Labor Housing projects, including the security value of the loan, the borrower’s required contribution, and how loan and grant amounts are covered. It outlines rules for projects seeking only a Section 514 loan versus those requesting grant funds, including the need to cover any gap between grant amounts and total development costs with borrower or other resources. It also sets the loan and grant terms for Off-Farm Labor Housing projects, such as a 1 percent interest rate, a 33-year loan term, and grants lasting as long as the housing is needed. Finally, it covers loan/grant approval and closing procedures, including required documents like board resolutions, loan agreements, and grant agreements for Farm Labor Housing projects.

nodes.title: Establishing a Profit Base on Initial Investment Return
nodes.summary: The document section discusses how to determine financial feasibility for Farm Labor Housing projects, including calculating the security value of the loan, the borrower’s required contribution, and how grant funds or Section 514 loans can cover any funding gap. It also explains loan and grant terms for Off-Farm Labor Housing, such as a 1% interest rate, a 33-year loan term, and grants lasting as long as the housing is needed. In addition, it covers rules for establishing a profit base on initial investment returns for certain limited partnerships. Finally, it outlines loan and grant approval and closing requirements, including required loan resolutions or agreements, and the execution of a grant agreement and resolution at closing.

nodes.title: LOAN/GRANT APPROVAL AND CLOSING [7 CFR 3560.571]
nodes.summary: The partial document explains how Farm Labor Housing loan and grant projects are evaluated, approved, and closed. It covers how to determine the security value of a project by considering both loan and grant amounts, how borrower contributions are calculated for loan-only and grant-funded projects, and how loan and grant rates and terms are set for Off-Farm Labor Housing projects. It also mentions conditions for establishing a profit base on initial investment return in certain limited partnership cases. Finally, it outlines the loan/grant approval and closing process, including required documents such as a board-adopted loan resolution or loan agreement, and a labor housing grant agreement and resolution executed at grant closing.

nodes.title: Loan Resolution or Loan Agreement
nodes.summary: The partial document explains how to determine financing for Farm Labor Housing projects, including security value, borrower contribution, and loan/grant funding requirements. It outlines that loan and grant amounts must be based on the applicant’s resources, approved loans, and ability to repay. It also covers specific rules for projects requesting only a Section 514 loan versus those requesting grant funds, including how any funding gap must be covered. The document then describes loan and grant terms for Off-Farm Labor Housing projects, such as a 1 percent interest rate, a 33-year loan term, and grant duration based on housing need. Finally, it addresses loan/grant approval and closing procedures, including required documents like board resolutions, loan agreements, and grant agreements for Farm Labor Housing projects.

nodes.title: Labor Housing Grant Agreement and Resolution
nodes.summary:
The document section covers how Farm Labor Housing and Off-Farm Labor Housing loans and grants are evaluated, approved, and closed. It explains how the security value of a project is determined by considering both loan and grant amounts, and how borrower contributions are assessed depending on whether the project uses only a Section 514 loan or includes grant funds. It also sets loan and grant terms and rates, including a 1 percent interest rate, a 33-year loan term, and grants lasting as long as housing is needed.

The section then outlines approval and closing requirements, including the need for board resolutions or loan agreements, grant agreements, and specific obligations related to tenant rents, safe and sanitary maintenance, and priority for domestic farm labor. It describes restrictive-use provisions for these loans and notes that construction financing must follow specific rules on equity contributions and the sequence of disbursement of loan and grant funds. Finally, it mentions that Section 516 grant-funded construction is subject to Davis-Bacon Act labor requirements, and introduces the topic of monitoring construction and project lease-up.

nodes.title: Restrictive-Use Provisions
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses key requirements for Farm Labor Housing grants and loans, including conditions that must be included in board resolutions, such as agency control over rents, maintaining safe and sanitary housing, and giving occupancy priority to domestic farm labor. It explains restrictive-use provisions for certain loans and references loan documents and related handbook guidance. The section on construction financing outlines rules for equity contributions, the order in which loan and grant funds must be disbursed, and notes that construction funded with a Section 516 grant is subject to Davis-Bacon Act labor standards.

nodes.title: CONSTRUCTION FINANCING
nodes.summary:
The partial document outlines key requirements for Farm Labor Housing programs, including grant and loan conditions, restrictive-use provisions, and construction financing rules. It explains that applicants’ boards and officers must understand the resolution terms, such as rent limits approved by the Agency, maintaining safe and sanitary housing, and giving priority to domestic farm labor for occupancy. It also notes that obligations tied to grants must follow the Farm Labor Housing grant agreement.

The document further distinguishes restrictive-use requirements for different types of Farm Labor Housing loans, referencing applicable federal regulations and loan documents. In the construction financing section, it states that equity contributions must be made before any Agency or interim loan disbursements, and when both loan and grant funds are involved, loan funds must be used before grant funds. Finally, it confirms that construction funded by a Section 516 grant is subject to Davis-Bacon Act labor standards and related Department of Labor regulations.

nodes.title: MONITORING CONSTRUCTION AND PROJECT LEASE-UP
nodes.summary:
The document section explains how to establish “diminished need” for farm labor housing and what evidence a borrower must submit for Agency review. It requires a current market analysis or survey, similar to loan origination requirements, to assess project viability, local economic conditions, farm worker demographics, and whether conditions are unlikely to improve in the next one to two years. The analysis must identify eligible tenants for off-farm labor housing under 7 CFR 3560.577 and summarize the sample used to document housing need. 

It also lists required supporting materials: Form HUD-935.2 showing efforts to attract eligible tenants, sample marketing or advertisements from the last three years, and Attachment 4-F for determining diminished need. If the submitted materials do not show adequate efforts to attract eligible tenants, the borrower must update them and continue marketing for an additional 12 months before resubmitting. Finally, the section explains that if diminished need is approved, units may be occupied by certain non-farm labor tenants, including very low-, low-, and moderate-income households or persons eligible under other housing benefit programs.

nodes.title: ESTABLISHING DIMINISHED NEEDS FOR FARM LABOR HOUSING
nodes.summary:
The partial document covers Rural Development handbook guidance for farm labor housing, focusing first on how to establish diminished need for off-farm labor housing. It explains that borrowers must submit a current market analysis showing project viability, local economic conditions, and farmworker demographics, including evidence that economic conditions are unlikely to improve soon. The analysis must identify eligible tenants under 7 CFR 3560.577, and borrowers must provide supporting items such as HUD Form 935.2, recent marketing/advertising samples, and Attachment 4-F. If the documentation does not show sufficient efforts to attract eligible tenants, it must be updated and the property must be marketed for an additional 12 months. It also notes that if diminished need is approved, units may then be occupied by certain non-farm labor tenants, including low- and moderate-income households or persons eligible under other housing programs.

The second main section introduces on-farm labor housing loans. It describes these projects as housing designed specifically for farmworkers employed by the farmowner or farmer association sponsor, and notes that they can be built in urban or rural areas on or off the farm if the need is justified. Funding is allocated annually, with applications handled on a first-come, first-served basis. The section ends with an application checklist listing required forms and documents, including budget and environmental forms, certification agreements, an appraisal, organizational documents, a survey, and an environmental checklist.

nodes.title: SECTION 2: ON-FARM LABOR HOUSING LOANS
nodes.summary: The partial document describes the USDA Rural Development process for on-farm labor housing loans. It explains that these loans are intended to provide affordable housing for farmworkers employed by a specific farm, and may be used in urban or rural areas, on or off the farm, if the need is justified. It also notes that funding is allocated annually, announced to field offices, and awarded on a first-come, first-served basis from National Office funds. The section then provides an application checklist for on-farm labor housing projects, listing required forms and supporting documents such as federal application forms, certifications, assurance and compliance agreements, an appraisal report, organizational documents, a survey, and an environmental checklist.

nodes.title: OVERVIEW
nodes.summary: The partial document explains the USDA Rural Development process for On-Farm Labor Housing Loans. It gives an overview of the program, describing how it helps farmowners and farmer associations provide affordable housing for farmworkers, including the fact that housing may be built in urban or rural areas, on or off the farm if the need is justified. It also notes that funding is allocated annually, announced to field offices, and awarded on a first-come, first-served basis from National Office funds. In addition, the document provides a checklist of required application materials for on-farm labor housing projects, including several federal forms, an appraisal report, organizational documents for partnerships or corporations, a survey, and an environmental checklist.

nodes.title: ON-FARM LABOR HOUSING CHECKLIST
nodes.summary:
This partial document covers Section 2 of HB-1-3560 on On-Farm Labor Housing Loans. It explains the purpose of these loans, which is to help farmowners and farmer associations provide affordable housing for farmworkers tied to their operations. It describes how funding is allocated annually, how applications are accepted and processed on a first-come, first-served basis, and provides a detailed checklist of required application, closing, and construction documents.

The section also outlines additional procedural requirements, including loan closing, preconstruction conference, inspections, title and insurance documents, and post-loan review. It notes special Truth in Lending and RESPA documentation requirements for 1–4 unit properties.

Finally, the document explains applicant eligibility rules for Section 514 Farm Labor Housing loans. Eligible borrowers must be farmowners or certain farmer entities engaged in agricultural or aquacultural operations, must own and operate the housing on a nonprofit basis, must use it for farm labor, must certify the workers’ involvement in farming, and must show that they cannot provide the housing themselves or obtain credit elsewhere on reasonable terms.

nodes.title: APPLICANT ELIGIBILITY [7 CFR 3560.605]
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines requirements for USDA Rural Development Section 514 Farm Labor Housing and On-Farm Labor Housing loans. It first mentions submission of Electric/Heat/Cooling Certifications for post-loan review and notes additional Truth in Lending and RESPA document requirements for 1–4 unit On-Farm Labor Housing properties. It then details applicant eligibility, explaining that borrowers must be eligible Section 515-type applicants and must be farm-related entities engaged in agricultural or aquacultural operations, own and operate the housing on a nonprofit basis, use it for farm labor, certify the workers’ involvement in farming, and demonstrate inability to provide housing or obtain credit elsewhere. It also describes a limited exception to the “credit elsewhere” rule when there is a local need for migrant farmworker housing and no other qualified providers are available. Finally, it covers project eligibility standards, including site and design requirements such as suitable placement on the farm, compliance with RD construction instructions, access to road frontage when feasible, and special rules for manufactured housing and future convertibility for seasonal housing.

nodes.title: PROJECT ELIGIBILITY [7 CFR 3560.605]
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses key rules and requirements for the On-Farm Labor Housing program under HB-1-3560. It explains when the Agency may waive the requirement that credit be unavailable elsewhere, specifically for migrant farmworker housing when no suitable public or nonprofit provider is available. It then outlines project eligibility standards, including site and design requirements for seasonal housing, single-family housing, road access, manufactured housing exceptions, and accessibility rules for small projects. The document also covers eligible and prohibited uses of loan funds, noting that funds can support seasonal or temporary facilities but cannot be used for prohibited purposes or to house certain family members of an individual farmowner or related applicant. It states that there are no funding priorities and that applications are funded on a first-come, first-served basis. Finally, it begins describing the initial application review process, highlighting eligibility considerations such as rural location not being required and the need to include washing facilities in the project budget.

nodes.title: LOAN FUND USES
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses key rules and procedures for the On-Farm Labor Housing program. It explains that small labor housing projects with fewer than three units are exempt from the requirement that 5 percent of units be fully accessible, though other accessibility rules still apply. It outlines eligible and prohibited uses of loan funds, including that funds may support seasonal or temporary-use facilities but cannot be used for prohibited purposes or to house the applicant’s immediate family in certain ownership structures. The document also notes that there are no funding priorities and applications are processed on a first-come, first-served basis. Finally, it begins describing how initial applications should be submitted and reviewed, including project eligibility considerations such as the fact that the project does not need to be in a rural location and must include adequate washing facilities.

nodes.title: Eligible Uses of Funds [7 CFR 3560.603]
nodes.summary: The partial document explains key rules for the On-Farm Labor Housing program. It notes that small projects with fewer than three units are exempt from the 5% fully accessible unit requirement, though other accessibility rules still apply. It then covers loan fund uses, including eligible uses such as seasonal or temporary facilities with furnishings and equipment, and prohibited uses, such as financing housing for the applicant or immediate family in certain ownership situations. It states that there are no funding priorities and that applications are funded on a first-come, first-served basis. Finally, it describes how initial applications should be submitted and reviewed, including project eligibility considerations like no rural-location requirement and ensuring washing facilities are included in the budget.

nodes.title: Prohibited Uses of Funds [7 CFR 3560.604]
nodes.summary: The partial document explains key rules for the On-Farm Labor Housing program. It notes that small labor housing buildings with fewer than three units are exempt from the 5 percent fully accessible unit requirement, though other accessibility requirements still apply. It outlines eligible loan fund uses, including seasonal or temporary facilities with furnishings and equipment, and prohibited uses, such as financing housing for the applicant or immediate family in certain farm ownership structures. The document also states that there are no funding priorities and that applications are funded on a first-come, first-served basis. Finally, it describes initial application processing and project eligibility considerations, including that projects do not need to be in rural designated areas and that applicants must include adequate washing facilities in the project budget.

nodes.title: PRIORITIES FOR FUNDING
nodes.summary: The partial document explains key rules for the On-farm Labor Housing program. It notes that small labor housing buildings with fewer than three units are exempt from the 5 percent fully accessible unit requirement, though other accessibility rules still apply. It outlines eligible and prohibited uses of loan funds, including allowance for seasonal or temporary-use facilities and restrictions on financing housing for the applicant or immediate family members in certain farmowner-related cases. It states that there are no funding priorities and applications are processed on a first-come, first-served basis. Finally, it describes initial application review procedures and highlights project eligibility considerations, such as the fact that the project does not need to be in a rural area and must include adequate washing facilities in the budget.

nodes.title: SUBMISSION AND PROCESSING OF INITIAL APPLICATIONS
nodes.summary: The partial document explains key rules and procedures for the On-farm Labor Housing program. It covers accessibility requirements for small housing projects, eligible and prohibited uses of loan funds, and the fact that funding is awarded on a first-come, first-served basis with no priorities. It also outlines how initial applications should be submitted and reviewed, including project eligibility, applicant eligibility, project feasibility, and financial capacity. Finally, it describes the worksheet and present value calculation method used to determine an applicant’s financial capacity for loan approval.

nodes.title: Project Eligibility
nodes.summary: The partial document explains key requirements for evaluating an On-farm Labor Housing loan or grant application. It covers applicant eligibility, including that the applicant must be an eligible entity and may need documentation if requesting a waiver of the “necessary credit elsewhere” test. It also addresses project feasibility, requiring proof that there is a need for on-farm labor housing in the market area, usually shown through employee housing needs. Finally, it discusses financial capacity, stating that applicants must have the legal and financial ability to carry out the loan obligations, and describes the use of an On-farm Labor Housing Worksheet and present value calculations to determine financial capacity using recent financial statements and applicable interest rates.

nodes.title: Applicant Eligibility
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines key requirements for On-Farm Labor Housing loan or grant processing. It explains that applicants must address whether the housing will be used year-round or seasonally and what construction standards will apply. It then covers applicant eligibility, including the need to qualify as an eligible entity and, if requesting a waiver of the “necessary credit elsewhere” test, to provide proper documentation. The document also discusses project feasibility, stating that the applicant must demonstrate a market need for on-farm labor housing, such as through employee housing needs. Finally, it details financial capacity requirements, including the use of an On-Farm Labor Housing Worksheet based on the most recent financial statements and a present value of an ordinary annuity (PVA) calculation to determine whether the applicant has sufficient legal and financial capacity to carry out the loan or grant obligations.

nodes.title: Project Feasibility
nodes.summary: The document outlines key requirements for determining eligibility and feasibility for an on-farm labor housing loan or grant application. It explains that the applicant must address whether the housing will be used year-round or seasonally and what construction standards will apply. It then covers applicant eligibility, including the need to qualify as an eligible entity and provide documentation if requesting a waiver of the “necessary credit elsewhere” test. The section on project feasibility states that the applicant must show there is a real need for on-farm labor housing in the market area, such as by documenting employee housing needs. Finally, it describes the financial capacity requirement, including the use of an On-farm Labor Housing Worksheet, completion based on the most recent financial statements, and calculation of financial capacity using a present value of an ordinary annuity formula or a specified multiplier depending on eligibility status.

nodes.title: Financial Capacity
nodes.summary: The partial document focuses on USDA Rural Development rules for On-Farm Labor Housing loan processing. It covers applicant eligibility requirements, including qualifying as an eligible entity and documenting any waiver requests. It explains project feasibility by showing the need for labor housing in the local market. It also details how to assess an applicant’s financial capacity using an On-Farm Labor Housing Worksheet, recent and prior-year financial statements, and present value calculations. The document then outlines final application processing, including loan limits, loan rates and terms, security value, and the prohibition of profit returns since the housing must operate on a nonprofit basis. Finally, it introduces loan approval and closing requirements, such as the need for an agency-approved loan resolution or agreement from the borrower’s board of directors.

nodes.title: SUBMISSION AND PROCESSING OF FINAL APPLICATIONS
nodes.summary: The partial document focuses on Rural Development procedures for On-Farm Labor Housing loans, especially the final application, approval, and closing stages. It explains how to determine loan limits, security value, and loan terms, including the maximum loan amount based on allowable total development costs or appraised security value, and sets the interest rate and repayment term. It also states that no profit base or return on borrower investment is allowed because the housing must operate on a nonprofit basis. In addition, it outlines loan approval and closing requirements, emphasizing that the borrower’s board must adopt an Agency-approved loan resolution or agreement and provide certified documentation before approval.

nodes.title: Determining Loan Limits, Rates, and Terms [7 CFR 3560.612 and 616]
nodes.summary:
The partial document covers Rural Housing Service guidance for On-Farm Labor Housing loans and an attachment clarifying the definition of “farm labor.” It explains how to calculate financial capacity using prior-year statements, then outlines final application processing, including loan limits, interest rate and term rules, and the prohibition on profit return to borrowers in nonprofit projects. It also describes required approval and closing documents, security and lien requirements, and construction monitoring and cost norm procedures using RSMeans or Marshall & Swift.

A major portion of the text is devoted to defining “farm labor” for eligibility purposes under the Farm Labor Housing program. It explains the statutory and regulatory definitions, distinguishes eligible farm labor from ineligible work, and clarifies the difference between post-harvest handling and processing. The attachment walks through the stages of an agricultural enterprise, provides examples of eligible and ineligible jobs at each stage, and includes tables showing how various agricultural commodities and enterprises relate to farm labor and processing worker activities. It ends with a reference to the eCFR for the full 7 CFR Part 3560.

structure.title: Appendix 2: 7 CFR part 11
structure.summary: This partial document is an appendix to HB-1-3560 that introduces 7 CFR Part 11, the National Appeals Division rules of procedure. It lists the main regulatory sections covering definitions, general applicability, informal review of adverse decisions, appealability, ex parte communications, division hearings, director review, basis for determinations, reconsideration, final determinations, judicial review, and filing deadlines. The excerpt also begins Section 11.1, defining “adverse decision” as an agency action that is unfavorable to a participant, including denials of equitable relief or failure to act within required timeframes, while excluding matters under the Board of Contract Appeals’ jurisdiction.

nodes.title: APPENDIX 2
nodes.summary: This partial document is an appendix to HB-1-3560 that presents the National Appeals Division regulations under 7 CFR Part 11. It includes the table of contents for Subpart A, listing key procedures such as definitions, applicability, informal review of adverse decisions, appealability determinations, ex parte communications, hearings, review of hearing officer determinations, basis for determinations, reconsideration, effective date of final decisions, judicial review, and filing deadlines. The excerpt then begins § 11.1, defining “adverse decision” as an agency decision unfavorable to a participant, including denied equitable relief or agency inaction within required or reasonable timeframes, while excluding matters under the Board of Contract Appeals’ jurisdiction.

nodes.title: 7 CFR Part 11— NATIONAL APPEALS DIVISION
nodes.summary: This excerpt is from Appendix 2 of HB-1-3560 and introduces 7 CFR Part 11, the National Appeals Division rules of procedure. It outlines the structure of Subpart A, listing the main sections governing definitions, general applicability, informal review of adverse decisions, appealability, ex parte communications, hearings, review and reconsideration processes, basis for determinations, effective dates, judicial review, and filing deadlines. It also cites the legal authority for the regulations and begins the definition section, explaining what constitutes an “adverse decision” and what is excluded from that term.

nodes.title: Subpart A – National Appeals Division Rules of Procedure
nodes.summary: This partial document is Appendix 2 of HB-1-3560 and introduces 7 CFR Part 11, the National Appeals Division rules of procedure. It lists the major subpart sections covering definitions, general statement, applicability, exceptions, informal review of adverse decisions, review of appealability, ex parte communications, hearings, director review, basis for determinations, reconsideration, effective date of final decisions, judicial review, and filing deadlines. The excerpt then begins Section 11.1, defining “adverse decision” as an agency action that is unfavorable to a participant, including denials of equitable relief or failure to act within required timeframes, while excluding decisions under the Board of Contract Appeals’ jurisdiction.

nodes.title: 11.1 Definitions.
nodes.summary:
This partial document outlines the USDA National Appeals Division (NAD) rules of procedure under 7 CFR Part 11. It first lists the subpart sections covering key topics such as definitions, general applicability, informal review, appealability, hearings, ex parte communications, director review, reconsideration, final determinations, judicial review, and filing deadlines.

The main body defines important terms used throughout the appeals process, including adverse decision, agency, agency record, appeal, appellant, authorized representative, case record, hearing, hearing officer, hearing record, implement, participant, record review, and Secretary. It also specifies which types of disputes and programs are excluded from NAD appeal procedures.

The document then explains the purpose and structure of the National Appeals Division as an independent unit within USDA, supervised by the Secretary but separate from other departmental offices. It states that program participants must first seek review through a NAD Hearing Officer, and may then request Director review before pursuing judicial review in court.

nodes.title: 11.2 General Statement.
nodes.summary:
The excerpt explains the National Appeals Division (NAD) regulations for USDA program appeals. It lists matters excluded from NAD jurisdiction, such as federal contracting disputes, FOIA appeals, suspension and debarment, export programs, crop insurance disputes, tenant grievances, personnel and EEO matters, tort claims, discrimination complaints, and tobacco quota review issues. It defines “record review” and “Secretary,” then states that the part establishes procedures for NAD hearings and appeals, describes the Division’s independence and authority, and notes that participants generally must seek NAD review before judicial review.

It also outlines the applicability of these rules to adverse agency decisions involving benefit denials, program compliance, payment determinations, and wetland/highly erodible land decisions, while excluding review of statutes or USDA regulations themselves. The section says the Federal Rules of Evidence do not apply in these proceedings. Finally, it describes informal review requirements: FSA field-level adverse decisions usually require prior informal review, while state-level FSA decisions and decisions from other agencies may be subject to optional informal review before an appeal to NAD.

nodes.title: 11.3 Applicability.
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines rules for appealing adverse agency decisions under §11 of the regulations. It explains what kinds of decisions are covered, such as denial of benefits, compliance determinations, payment issues, and wetland/highly erodible land findings. It also states that these procedures cannot be used to challenge statutes or USDA regulations issued under federal law, and that the Federal Rules of Evidence do not apply in these proceedings. In addition, it describes the required or optional informal review process before an appeal to the National Appeals Division (NAD), including special requirements for Farm Service Agency (FSA) decisions and references to the relevant review procedures in other CFR parts.

nodes.title: 11.4 Inapplicability of other laws and regulations.
nodes.summary: This excerpt outlines the scope and procedures for appealing adverse decisions within USDA-related programs. It explains which types of decisions are covered, such as denial of benefits, compliance determinations, payment amounts, and wetland or highly erodible land findings, and notes that the procedures cannot be used to challenge statutes or USDA regulations. It also states that the Federal Rules of Evidence do not apply to these proceedings. Finally, it describes the required or optional informal review process before an appeal can be accepted by the National Appeals Division (NAD), including specific review pathways for FSA and other agencies.

nodes.title: 11.5 Informal review of adverse decisions.
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines appeal and review procedures for adverse agency decisions under USDA/NAD rules. It explains which types of decisions are covered, such as denials of program participation or benefits, compliance issues, payment determinations, and wetland/highly erodible land findings, and notes that these procedures cannot be used to challenge statutes or USDA regulations. It also states that the Federal Rules of Evidence do not apply. The document then describes required and optional informal review processes, including agency-level review and mediation/alternative dispute resolution, and explains how these steps affect appeal deadlines. Finally, it covers Director review when an agency says a decision is not appealable, including time limits for requesting review, the Director’s authority to decide appealability, and the finality of that determination.

nodes.title: 11.6 Director review of agency determination of appealability and right of participants to Division hearing.
nodes.summary: The excerpt outlines procedures for mediation, appeals, and review of adverse agency decisions under NAD rules. It explains how requesting mediation or alternative dispute resolution affects appeal deadlines and hearing timelines. It describes the Director’s review process for determining whether an agency decision is appealable, including deadlines, finality, and delegation authority. It also covers how participants may file appeals of adverse decisions, required contents and timing of hearing requests, options for record review, and requirements for authorized representatives. Finally, it sets rules prohibiting ex parte communications with Division staff during the appeal process, with limited exceptions for procedural matters or noticed discussions involving all parties.

nodes.title: 11.7 Ex parte communications.
nodes.summary: This excerpt outlines the procedures for appealing adverse decisions through the Division, including strict deadlines for requesting a hearing, what a written hearing request must contain, and the option to request a record review instead of a hearing. It also explains requirements for an authorized representative to file proof of authorization. The document then covers rules against ex parte communications during appeals, including what communications are prohibited, what must be added to the hearing record if such communications occur, and possible consequences for violations. Finally, it begins the section on Division hearings, stating who may access the agency record, the authority of the Director and Hearing Officer to administer oaths and issue subpoenas, and how subpoenas for evidence or witnesses may be requested.

nodes.title: 11.8 Division hearings.
nodes.summary:
The partial document outlines procedural rules for Division hearings and appeals of adverse agency decisions. It covers restrictions on ex parte communications and how improper communications must be added to the hearing record. It explains hearing procedures, including access to the agency record, subpoena authority and service requirements, witness costs, and enforcement of subpoenas.

The document also details how hearings are conducted: deadlines for filing appeal materials, notice of hearing, pre-hearing conferences, evidence presentation, official recording of hearings, and what happens if a party does not appear. It states that the appellant bears the burden of proof, sets timelines for issuing hearing determinations, and explains when those determinations become final.

Finally, it describes Director review of Hearing Officer determinations, including who may request review, filing deadlines, notice and response procedures, and the Director’s authority to uphold, reverse, or modify decisions based on the record and any accepted additional information.

nodes.title: 11.9 Director review of determinations of Hearing Officers.
nodes.summary: The partial document covers the process for Director review of a Hearing Officer’s determination and the standards used in making appeal decisions. It explains who may request review, the deadlines for requests, required contents of the request, notice to other parties, response opportunities, and the Director’s review authority, including issuing a final determination, remanding for further proceedings, delegation of review authority, and granting equitable relief. It also outlines the basis for determinations, stating that Hearing Officers and the Director are not bound by prior agency findings and must rely on the case record, applicable laws, and regulations in effect at the relevant time.

nodes.title: 11.10 Basis for determinations.
nodes.summary: This section describes the review and appeals process for adverse agency decisions, including the Director’s authority to remand cases, issue final determinations, delegate review authority, and grant equitable relief. It explains the legal basis for determinations, emphasizing that decisions must rely on the case record and applicable laws and regulations rather than prior agency findings. It also covers reconsideration procedures for Director decisions, including who may request reconsideration, response timelines, and when a reconsidered decision becomes final. In addition, it outlines how final determinations are implemented by agencies, when such determinations become effective, and the availability and limits of judicial review in federal court.

nodes.title: 11.11 Reconsideration of Director determinations.
nodes.summary: This portion describes the final steps in an appeal process under HB-1-3560, Appendix 2. It explains how a party may request reconsideration of the Director’s determination within 10 days, what a valid reconsideration request must show, how other parties are notified and allowed to respond, and how quickly the Director must issue a reconsideration decision. It also covers when final determinations become effective and how agencies must implement them within 30 days, including that effectiveness may relate back to the application date, transaction date, or original adverse decision. Finally, it states that final determinations can be reviewed and enforced in federal district court, but only after the appellant has received a final determination from the Division.

nodes.title: 11.12 Effective date and implementation of final determinations of the Division.
